shock.adult_dose: Balanced Salt Solution (e.g., Ringer's Lactate): 500ml IV bolus
shock.notes_updates: Balanced Salt Solutions preferred over 0.9% Saline for septic shock to prevent hyperchloraemic acidosis.
0.9% Saline remains primary for hypovolaemia due to blood loss/dehydration.

inotropes.notes_updates: First-line vasopressor for septic shock. No longer restricted as 'Section 21'.
Second-line for septic shock if MAP targets not met with Noradrenaline.
Indicated for septic shock with cardiac dysfunction (persistent hypoperfusion despite fluids/vasopressors).
Largely superseded by Noradrenaline for shock.

resuscitation_medication.paediatric_dose: 10ug/kg IV OR 0.1ml/kg of 1:10 000 (1mg adrenaline/10ml normal saline)

resuscitation_medication.adult_dose: 300mg IV bolus over 3min (unstable pt); 150mg IV bolus over 3min (stable pt)

sedation.notes: Preferred for asthma/bronchospasm or trauma intubation

sedation.notes_updates: Avoid routine deep sedation; prioritize pain control and neuro-psychological support.
Adjust dose in obesity. Caution: Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS).
Preferred for asthma/bronchospasm or trauma intubation.

seizures.adult_dose: 10mg IM or buccal (if no IV access)

seizures.notes_updates: Safer alternative for women of child-bearing potential.

analgesia.notes_updates: Encouraged as part of multimodal analgesia.
Avoid if renal impairment or GI bleeding

neuroprotective.notes_updates: Monitor osmolar gap; avoid if hypotensive
Alternative to mannitol for raised ICP

broad_spectrum_antibiotics.notes_updates: Extended infusion: 3-4hr. High dose for meningitis/CNS

hyperkalaemia_management.notes: Drives K+ into cells. Monitor glucose q15min
Removes K+ from gut. Slow onset (several hours)

diuretics.notes: Monitor electrolytes. Ototoxicity at high doses

vitamins.notes: Give BEFORE glucose in malnourished/alcoholic patients

protocols.adult_dose: NAC Old Regimen: 150mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 1hr, then 50mg/kg in 500ml 5% Dextrose IV over 4hrs, then 100mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose IV over 16hrs | NAC SNAP Protocol: 100mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 2hrs, then 200mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose IV over 10hrs
protocols.notes: Toxic dose: 150mg/kg or >10g in adults | Use Paracetamol Treatment Nomogram to establish need for antidote | Do NOT withhold even if ingestion was 48-72hrs prior | Activated charcoal 50g PO if presentation <1hr post-ingestion | Paracetamol levels only useful if >4hrs post-ingestion | Start treatment preferably <8hrs post-ingestion | If in doubt, give NAC
protocols.notes_updates: Toxic dose: 150mg/kg or >10g in adults | NAC Old Regimen: 150mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 1hr, then 50mg/kg in 500ml over 4hrs, then 100mg/kg in 1L over 16hrs | SNAP Protocol: 100mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 2hrs, then 200mg/kg in 1L over 10hrs | Give activated charcoal 50g PO if <1hr post-ingestion | Paracetamol levels only if >4hrs post-ingestion | Do NOT withhold NAC even if 48-72hrs post-ingestion | Cautions: anaphylaxis, vomiting | High-risk patients: hypotensive, pre-existing liver dysfunction, elderly | Pregnancy: paracetamol crosses placenta, administer NAC as above

protocols.adult_dose: Atropine: 0.04-0.05mg/kg IV (3-5mg adult), double every 5min until atropinised | Infusion: 200mg Atropine in 200ml 0.9% NaCl (1mg/ml), 10-20% of total dose/hr | Pralidoxime: 1-2g IV
protocols.notes: Decontamination: remove clothing, wash patient with soap and water | Universal precautions mandatory | Avoid succinylcholine (causes prolonged paralysis) | Atropine treats muscarinic symptoms only | May cause anticholinergic toxidrome at high doses - monitor closely, insert urine catheter | Endpoint = clearing of respiratory secretions (atropinisation), NOT tachycardia or pupil dilation | Seizures: benzodiazepines only (Clonazepam, Diazepam, Lorazepam) - NO anticonvulsants | Monitor and correct electrolytes. Consider MgSO4 4g/day | Weakness (nicotinic): intubation and supportive management only way to control

protocols.adult_dose: Glucagon: 5-10mg IV bolus then 1-5mg/hr infusion | HIET: Actrapid 1 IU/kg IV stat then 0.5-10 IU/kg/hr infusion | Calcium chloride: 10ml 10% over 10min via large bore IV/central line (target Ca 2mmol) | Consider Intralipid emulsion
protocols.notes: Clinical features: Hypotension, bradycardia, hypoglycaemia, acidosis, CNS depression | ECG: Sinus bradycardia, AV blocks, junctional bradycardia, ventricular bradycardia | Propranolol also acts as Na channel blocker (TCA-like features) | Sotalol blocks K channels (QT prolongation, Torsades) | Enhanced elimination: Activated charcoal 50g via NGT | Haemodynamic support: Fluid bolus, high-dose inotropes | HIET protocol: Start with 50ml 50% dextrose bolus, Actrapid 1 IU/kg load, infusion 0.5 IU/kg/hr titrate up to 10 IU/kg/hr, dextrose maintenance 25g/hr via central line. Monitor HGT q20min for 1st hour then q1hr. Monitor potassium - replace if <2.5. | Therapeutic endpoints: EF>50%, SBP>90mmHg, HR>60bpm, resolution of acidaemia, euglycaemia, UO 1-2ml/kg/hr, QRS<120ms, improved GCS | Consider ECMO if no response to HIET
protocols.notes_updates: Clinical features: Hypotension, bradycardia, hypoglycaemia (BB) or hyperglycaemia (CCB), acidosis, CNS depression | ECG: Sinus bradycardia, AV blocks, junctional bradycardia, ventricular bradycardia | Propranolol: also Na channel blocker - TCA-like features | Sotalol: blocks K channels - QT prolongation, Torsades | Enhanced elimination: Activated charcoal 50g via NGT | Haemodynamic support: Fluid bolus, high-dose inotropes | Glucagon: 5-10mg IV bolus then 1-5mg/hr | HIET: Actrapid 1 IU/kg IV load, then 0.5-10 IU/kg/hr. Dextrose 25g/hr maintenance. Monitor HGT q20min then q1hr. Replace K+ if <2.5 | Calcium chloride: 10ml 10% over 10min (target Ca 2mmol) | Intralipid emulsion - specialist consultation | ECMO if no response to HIET | Therapeutic endpoints: EF>50%, SBP>90, HR>60, pH normalised, euglycaemia, UO 1-2ml/kg/hr, QRS<120ms

protocols.adult_dose: Activated charcoal: 50g if >150mg/kg ingested (up to 8hrs post-ingestion) | Urinary alkalinisation: Sodium bicarbonate 1ml/kg IV of 8.5% (Sodabic) | Goal serum pH 7.45-7.55, urine pH >7.5 | Haemodialysis for refractory cases

protocols.notes: Toxicity levels: Minimal <150mg/kg, Moderate 150-300mg/kg (hyperpnoea, vomiting, nausea, tinnitus), Severe >300mg/kg (metabolic acidosis, seizures, coma, hypotension, pulmonary oedema, hyperthermia), Lethal >500mg/kg | Chronic toxicity common in elderly - non-specific delirium, confusion, fever, unexplained acidosis | Levels correlate poorly with severity but serial levels monitor treatment response | Decontamination: 50g activated charcoal up to 8hrs post-ingestion if >150mg/kg. If >300mg/kg, secure airway FIRST. Consider MDAC if levels continue to rise | Avoid intubation if possible - high peri-intubation mortality | If intubation required: Sodabic boluses prior, minimise apnoeic period, match patient's respiratory rate to maintain respiratory alkalosis | Seizures: IV benzodiazepines, monitor for arrhythmias/hypoglycaemia | Blood gas: Respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA (lactic acidosis from uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation), hypokalaemia, hyper/hypoglycaemia | Neuroglycopenia can occur even at normal HGT levels | Winter's formula: 1.5 x HCO3 + 8 +/- 2 = expected CO2

Secure airway PRIOR to charcoal if >300mg/kg. Consider MDAC if levels rise | Enhanced elimination: Urinary alkalinisation with Sodabic 1ml/kg IV 8.5%. Goal serum pH 7.45-7.55, urine pH >7.5 | AVOID intubation if possible - high peri-intubation mortality. reversal_agents.notes: Develops over 24-72hrs | Features: hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, rhabdomyolysis | Resuscitate, withdraw precipitating agent, supportive care | Differential: serotonin syndrome, malignant hyperthermia, thyroid storm, sepsis, heat stroke, anticholinergic/sympathomimetic intoxication | Active cooling: target temp <39C within 30min (peripheral: ice bath, cold wet sheet, ice packs, fan; central: cold IV fluids) | Avoid succinylcholine (risk of hyperkalaemia) | Avoid antipyretics (NSAIDs and aspirin not effective)
reversal_agents.notes_updates: Develops over 24-72 hours | Features: hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, rhabdomyolysis, altered mental status, autonomic instability | Immediate: resuscitate, withdraw precipitating agent, supportive care | Bromocriptine 2.5-10mg PO/NG 8hrly (dopamine agonist) | Dantrolene 1-2.5mg/kg IV (muscle relaxant) | Active cooling: target temp <39C within 30min - peripheral (ice bath, cold wet sheet, ice packs, fan) and central (cold IV fluids) | Avoid succinylcholine - risk of hyperkalaemia | Avoid antipyretics - NSAIDs and aspirin not effective | Differential: serotonin syndrome, malignant hyperthermia, thyroid storm, sepsis, heat stroke, drug intoxication

reversal_agents.adult_dose: Dantrolene: 2.5mg/kg IV bolus (may repeat to cumulative 10mg/kg). Dissolve in sterile water
reversal_agents.notes: Dantrolene is the specific antidote for both Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome and Malignant Hyperthermia | Dissolve in sterile water (not saline) | Cooling measures: peripheral (ice bath, cold wet sheet, ice packs in groin/axillae, fan) and central (cold IV fluids) | Target temp <39C within 30 minutes | Avoid succinylcholine - risk of hyperkalaemia | Avoid antipyretics - not effective | May develop fulminant hepatic failure, multi-organ dysfunction, rhabdomyolysis, sepsis, AKI | Malignant hyperthermia triggered by succinylcholine and volatile anaesthetics | Intubation: avoid scoline
reversal_agents.notes_updates: Dantrolene: 2.5mg/kg IV bolus, may repeat to cumulative 10mg/kg | Dissolve in sterile water (NOT saline) | Target temperature <39C within 30 minutes | Peripheral cooling: ice bath immersion, cold wet sheet, ice packs in groin/axillae, fan cooling | Central cooling: cold IV fluids | Avoid succinylcholine - risk of hyperkalaemia | Avoid antipyretics - NSAIDs and aspirin not effective | May develop fulminant hepatic failure, MODS, rhabdomyolysis, sepsis, AKI - manage supportively | MH triggered by succinylcholine and volatile anaesthetics | NMS develops over 24-72hrs after antipsychotic exposure

management_steps.action: ABCs, general appearance, focused physical examination
12 & 15 lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival
Aspirin 300mg chewed stat (unless contraindicated - give Clopidogrel 300mg stat po if aspirin hypersensitivity)
Nitroglycerin (check ECG first for RV infarct): Nitrolingual spray 1 spray q5min (max 3 in 15min) OR sublingual 1 tablet q5min (max 3 doses)
Fentanyl IV 50mcg aliquots if discomfort not relieved by nitroglycerin
O2 via nasal prongs @ 2L/min if O2 sats < 94%, titrate
IV access, bloods (Troponin I, U&E, FBC, VBG)
If onset < 12 hours AND continuous chest pain → thrombolysis (Actilyse or Tenecteplase)

Heparin (Clexane 30mg IV stat then 1mg/kg SC after 15min OR unfractionated heparin 60IU/kg IV stat then infusion), Clopidogrel 300mg stat po, Simvastatin 40mg stat po, Beta-blocker if no contraindications
High risk: same adjunctive therapy as STEMI + admit HCU/Medicine + cardiology consult
Serial troponin and ECG at 3 hours (hs) or 6 hours (stat lab). If delta troponin negative, discharge with OPD follow-up within 1 week

drugs.notes: Chewed. Contraindicated in hypersensitivity - use Clopidogrel instead
If aspirin hypersensitivity or as adjunct in STEMI/NSTE-ACS
Contraindicated if SBP < 90, HR < 50 or > 100, RV infarction, PDE inhibitor use
For pain not relieved by nitroglycerin
LMWH alternative: unfractionated heparin 60IU/kg IV stat (max 4000IU) then 12IU/kg/hr infusion (max 1000IU/hr)
Contraindicated in haemodynamic instability, heart failure, pulmonary oedema, SBP < 100, HR ≤ 60, AV block 2°/3°, asthma

warnings: Door-to-needle time for thrombolysis: 30 minutes
Time of onset of symptoms or maximum pain must be < 12 hours for thrombolysis
Consider aortic dissection in central chest pain radiating to back with tearing sensation, differential radial pulse, differential arm BP > 15mmHg, widened mediastinum on CXR
RV infarction (associated with inferior MI): ST elevation in V4R - avoid nitrates
True posterior MI: tall R in V1 and STE ≥ 0.5mm in V7-9
protocols.disposition: STEMI: Refer to Internal Medicine/Cardiology urgently. NSTE-ACS high risk: Admit HCU/Medicine. Low risk: May discharge with OPD follow-up within 1 week if serial troponins negative.
protocols.notes_updates:
Symptoms: Chest pain or pressure, Dyspnoea, Diaphoresis, Nausea/vomiting, Pain radiating to arm, jaw, back, Syncope or pre-syncope
Signs: Tachycardia, Hypotension, Diaphoresis, New cardiac murmur, Signs of heart failure (elevated JVP, pulmonary crackles), Cardiogenic shock
Risk factors: Age ≥ 65 years, Smoking, Family history of CAD, Diabetes Mellitus, Hypercholesterolaemia, Hypertension, Previous MI, Drug abuse: cocaine, methamphetamine, HIV ± HAART (protease inhibitors)
Investigations - bedside: 12 & 15 lead ECG (including V4R, V7-9), Vital signs and O2 sats, Finger prick glucose, VBG
Investigations - lab: Troponin I (hs or stat lab), U&E, FBC
Investigations - imaging: Portable CXR, CTPA if PE suspected
Disposition: STEMI: Refer to Internal Medicine/Cardiology urgently. NSTE-ACS high risk: Admit HCU/Medicine. Low risk: May discharge with OPD follow-up within 1 week if serial troponins negative.
Warnings: Door-to-needle time for thrombolysis: 30 minutes; Time of onset of symptoms or maximum pain must be < 12 hours for thrombolysis; Consider aortic dissection in central chest pain radiating to back with tearing sensation, differential radial pulse, differential arm BP > 15mmHg, widened mediastinum on CXR; RV infarction (associated with inferior MI): ST elevation in V4R - avoid nitrates; True posterior MI: tall R in V1 and STE ≥ 0.5mm in V7-9

absolute: Known structural cerebral vascular lesion (e.g., AVM)
Ischaemic stroke within 3 months (except acute ischaemic stroke within 3 hours)
Significant closed head or facial trauma within 3 months
relative: History of chronic, severe, poorly controlled hypertension
SBP > 180mmHg or DBP > 110mmHg (lower BP with Nitrocine infusion first)
Traumatic or prolonged CPR (> 10min) or major surgery (< 3 weeks)
Active peptic ulcer disease (bled within past 2-4 weeks)
Prior Streptokinase exposure or allergic reaction

signs: ST elevation meeting criteria in 2 or more contiguous leads

bedside: Continuous BP, HR, O2 sats, GCS monitoring

management_steps.action: Confirm no contraindications (absolute or relative) - see ACS protocol
Obtain written and signed consent prior to thrombolysis
Actilyse dosing: 15mg IV bolus, then 0.75mg/kg over 30min (max 50mg), then 0.5mg/kg over 60min (max 35mg). Maximum total dose 100mg
Alternative - Tenecteplase (Metalyse): Weight < 100kg: 8000 IU IV over 10 seconds. Weight > 100kg: 10000 IU IV over 10 seconds
Continuous monitoring: BP, HR, O2 sats, GCS
ECGs at 0, 15, 30, 60, 90 min and with any rhythm changes or interventions
Document pain score out of 10 on each ECG along with vital signs
Reperfusion arrhythmias are common and usually transient - seldom require intervention

drugs.adult_dose: 15mg IV bolus, then 0.75mg/kg over 30min (max 50mg), then 0.5mg/kg over 60min (max 35mg)
drugs.notes: Maximum total dose 100mg
Use if late presentation ≥ 10 hrs post pain onset or allergy to Streptokinase

management_steps.action: Triage to RESUS if acutely distressed or unstable
Rapid ABC assessment, IV line, monitors
Crystalloid fluid bolus 10-20ml/kg ONLY if hypotensive. NB: Patients often intravascularly depleted despite peripheral fluid overload
NIV (CPAP or BiPAP) - significant reduction in intubation requirements if started early
Nitrocine: 1-2mg IV boluses OR infusion 30mcg/min, max 150-200mcg/min. Caution in preload dependent patients (RV infarction, pulmonary HT, aortic stenosis)
Furosemide (Lasix): Daily oral dose as IV bolus, or 20-40mg IV if not on Lasix previously. Caution in severe renal failure/anuria
Inotropes for cardiogenic shock: Dobutamine preferred (see infusion protocol). May add adrenaline if poor response or sepsis
Find and treat underlying cause/precipitant: ACS, arrhythmia, infection, hypertension, valvular pathology

drugs.notes: Vasodilator, decreases preload. Caution in preload dependent patients (RV infarct, pulmonary HT, aortic stenosis)

drugs.adult_dose: 20-40mg IV (if not previously on) or convert daily oral dose to IV
drugs.notes: Caution in severe renal failure/anuria. Consider infusion (discuss with cardiology)
Preferred inotrope for cardiogenic shock
Add if poor response to dobutamine or sepsis present

warnings: Patients are often intravascularly depleted despite peripheral fluid overload
Caution with nitrocine in preload dependent patients (RV infarction, pulmonary HT, aortic stenosis)
Caution with furosemide in severe renal failure/anuria

Non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema causes: CVA, head injury, negative pressure oedema

protocols.notes_updates:
Symptoms: Dyspnoea, Orthopnoea, Effort intolerance, Peripheral oedema, Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea (PND)
Signs: Tachypnoea, Tachycardia, Gallop rhythm (S3), Elevated JVP, Chest crackles (pulmonary oedema), Cardiac murmurs, Hypotension (cardiogenic shock)
Risk factors: Previous CCF, ACS history, Uncontrolled hypertension, Valvular heart disease, Cardiomyopathy, Arrhythmias (AF common), Non-compliance with medications, Infection/sepsis
Investigations - bedside: ECG (AF, new T wave changes, LVH, LBBB, cerebral T waves), VBG, HGT, Urine βHCG, POCUS: > 3 B lines bilaterally, pleural effusions, poor contractility, dilated IVC
Investigations - lab: FBC, U&E, Troponin, proBNP (if diagnosis in doubt)
Investigations - imaging: CXR: pulmonary oedema, pulmonary venous congestion, cardiomegaly, Kerley B lines
Disposition: Internal Medicine/Cardiology consult. Will likely require High Care bed. Continue serial ECG and VBG monitoring in ED.
Warnings: Patients are often intravascularly depleted despite peripheral fluid overload; Caution with nitrocine in preload dependent patients (RV infarction, pulmonary HT, aortic stenosis); Caution with furosemide in severe renal failure/anuria; New murmur may indicate acute mitral regurgitation - requires urgent surgery; Non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema causes: CVA, head injury, negative pressure oedema

management_steps.action: ABCs if unstable. Keep O2 sats > 94%
Check HGT immediately - exclude hypoglycaemia (stroke mimic)
Keep NPO until swallowing assessment done
Nurse in left lateral position (high aspiration risk)
Time since onset of symptoms - document 'last seen well' time
Urgent non-contrast CT brain to exclude haemorrhage
Doctor to accompany patient to CT scan
If onset < 3 hours (consider 3-4.5 hours): thrombolysis with Alteplase after radiologist confirms no ICH/obvious infarct
Consult neurologist, obtain written signed consent
Alteplase dosing: 0.9mg/kg IV (10% as bolus, remainder over 60 min). Max dose 90mg
Optimise BP < 185/110mmHg before thrombolysis (see Hypertensive Emergencies protocol)
If > 4.5 hours: non-contrast CT, medicine/neurology referral
For symptomatic ICH after alteplase: stop infusion, cryoprecipitate 10U/kg, tranexamic acid 1g IV, haematology/neurosurgery consult

warnings: TIME IS KEY - do not delay
Wake-up stroke is currently NOT for thrombolysis
1 in 6-10 patients may have improvement with thrombolysis
1 in 20 may develop significant bleed (5%) - life-threatening
Risk of bleed increases to 8% if given between 3-4.5 hours
Exclude stroke mimics: hypoglycaemia, seizures (Todd's paresis), migraines, tumours, infection
Do not delay thrombolysis for lab results except glucose and INR if on warfarin
protocols.disposition: All patients require Medicine/Neurology referral. Thrombolysis patients need ICU/HCU admission.
protocols.notes_updates:
Symptoms: Sudden onset focal neurological deficit, Unilateral weakness or numbness, Speech difficulty (aphasia/dysarthria), Visual disturbance, Severe headache (less common), Ataxia, Vertigo (posterior circulation)
Signs: Unilateral facial droop, Unilateral arm drift/weakness, Unilateral leg weakness, Aphasia (expressive or receptive), Dysarthria, Hemianopia, Ataxia, Decreased GCS, NIHSS > 6 (thrombolysis threshold)
Risk factors: Hypertension, Diabetes Mellitus, Atrial fibrillation, Previous stroke/TIA, Smoking, Hypercholesterolaemia, Age > 55 years
Investigations - bedside: HGT (exclude hypoglycaemia), ABG, 12 lead ECG, NIHSS score, VAN score (for endovascular therapy eligibility), Blood pressure monitoring
Investigations - lab: INR (if indicated), FBC, U&E
Investigations - imaging: Urgent non-contrast CT brain (within 25 minutes), CT angiogram (consider), CT perfusion scan or MRI DWI (for thrombectomy selection)
Disposition: All patients require Medicine/Neurology referral. Thrombolysis patients need ICU/HCU admission.
Warnings: TIME IS KEY - do not delay; Wake-up stroke is currently NOT for thrombolysis; 1 in 6-10 patients may have improvement with thrombolysis; 1 in 20 may develop significant bleed (5%) - life-threatening; Risk of bleed increases to 8% if given between 3-4.5 hours; Exclude stroke mimics: hypoglycaemia, seizures (Todd's paresis), migraines, tumours, infection; Do not delay thrombolysis for lab results except glucose and INR if on warfarin

management_steps.action: Assess stability: hypotension, acute cardiac failure, chest pain, dyspnoea, altered mental status
Unstable AF: Synchronised cardioversion 150-200J (consider max dose first, max 3 attempts). Use BP-preserving analgesia/sedation (fentanyl/ketamine/midazolam)
If cardioversion fails AND no WPW: Amiodarone 150mg IV in D5W over 30min then 900mg in 200ml D5W at 8.3ml/hr. Consider MgSO4 2-4g IV over 15min
Stable AF: Determine if candidate for cardioversion (< 48hrs, new onset, no LV dysfunction, no valvular disease, no VTE history, therapeutic anticoagulation)
If candidate for cardioversion: synchronised cardioversion with analgesia/sedation. Consider anticoagulation
If NOT candidate for cardioversion: RATE CONTROL (goal HR < 110bpm)
Rate control options: Amiodarone 150mg IV then infusion OR Beta-blocker (Carvedilol 3.125mg PO stat, Atenolol 50mg PO stat, or Propranolol if thyroid-related) OR Calcium channel blocker OR Digoxin
Anticoagulation: Balance HAS-BLED risk vs CHA2DS2-VASc benefit. Start Clexane 1mg/kg SC stat in ED if no contraindications
Look for and treat underlying cause (THE ATRIAL FIBS mnemonic)
Recent evidence: No survival benefit in rate vs rhythm control. Rate control and anticoagulation supported by recent trials

drugs.adult_dose: 150mg IV in D5W over 30min, then 900mg in 200ml D5W at 8.3ml/hr
drugs.notes: First-line for unstable AF if cardioversion fails. Also for rate control in stable AF.
Consider as adjunct in AF
Beta-blocker for rate control
Beta-blocker for rate control
Use if thyroid-related AF
Start in ED if no contraindications for anticoagulation
Alternative rate control agent

warnings: DO NOT use rate-controlling agents (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, amiodarone) in WPW with AF - risk of VF
WPW + AF: Use electrical cardioversion ONLY
WPW ECG features: Irregularly irregular, rapid > 200bpm, wide QRS varying beat-to-beat, delta wave
Balance HAS-BLED bleeding risk vs CHA2DS2-VASc stroke benefit for anticoagulation decisions
If onset > 48 hours and not anticoagulated, do NOT cardiovert without transoesophageal echo or adequate anticoagulation first
protocols.disposition: Unstable AF: Admit to HCU/ICU. Stable AF: May discharge with cardiology follow-up if rate controlled and anticoagulated.
protocols.notes_updates:
Symptoms: Palpitations, Chest pain, Dyspnoea, Dizziness, Syncope or pre-syncope, Fatigue
Signs: Irregularly irregular pulse, Tachycardia (HR > 100 bpm), Hypotension (unstable AF), Signs of heart failure, Variable intensity S1
Risk factors: Thyroid disease, Hypothermia, PE/embolism, Alcohol, Trauma (cardiac contusion), Recent surgery (e.g., CABG), Ischaemia/infarct, Atrial enlargement, Valvular heart disease (mitral stenosis), Stimulants (cocaine, theophylline, caffeine), Fever/anaemia/high-output states
Investigations - bedside: 12 lead ECG, Vital signs, HGT
Investigations - lab: U&E (exclude electrolyte abnormalities), Troponin (if chest pain), TFTs (thyroid), FBC (anaemia, infection)
Investigations - imaging: CXR (if indicated)
Disposition: Unstable AF: Admit to HCU/ICU. Stable AF: May discharge with cardiology follow-up if rate controlled and anticoagulated.
Warnings: DO NOT use rate-controlling agents (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, amiodarone) in WPW with AF - risk of VF; WPW + AF: Use electrical cardioversion ONLY; WPW ECG features: Irregularly irregular, rapid > 200bpm, wide QRS varying beat-to-beat, delta wave; Balance HAS-BLED bleeding risk vs CHA2DS2-VASc stroke benefit for anticoagulation decisions; If onset > 48 hours and not anticoagulated, do NOT cardiovert without transoesophageal echo or adequate anticoagulation first

risk_factors: > 2 rescue inhalers used in last month

imaging: CXR indications: non-responders, O2 sats < 92%, suspected pneumothorax/pneumonia, chest pain, CV instability, possible foreign body

management_steps.action: Continuous nebulisations for ALL patients: Beta-2 agonists (Salbutamol/Fenoterol) AND Anticholinergic (Ipratropium Bromide)
MILD (PEFR > 75%, O2 > 95%, pulse < 100): Oral Prednisone 0.5mg/kg stat
MODERATE (PEFR 50-70%, O2 90-95%, pulse 100-120): Hydrocortisone 200mg IV stat
SEVERE (PEFR < 50%, O2 < 90%, pulse > 120, pCO2 > 42): Hydrocortisone 200mg IV stat, MgSO4 25-50mg/kg IV over 20min, NIV (CPAP/BiPAP), prepare for intubation
RSI for severe/refractory: Ketamine 1mg/kg then infusion 0.15mg/kg/hr. In-line nebulisation
In extremis/difficult IV access: Adrenaline 0.3mg subcut (repeat in 5 min)
If intubated: Volume Assist Control, TV 6ml/kg, I:E ratio 1:3-1:5, RR 10-12 bpm, allow higher pressures initially
Discharge criteria: Pulse < 110, RR < 25, O2 > 92% on room air, no high-risk features, improvement sustained > 1 hour after last nebulisation, PEFR > 75% predicted

drugs.notes: Beta-2 agonist. ALL severity levels.
Anticholinergic agent. Combined with beta-agonist.
Mild asthma only
Moderate and severe asthma
Severe asthma only
Induction for RSI in severe asthma
In extremis/difficult IV access only

warnings: Silent chest = life-threatening - no wheeze due to minimal air movement
Rising pCO2 in asthma is ominous and indicates tiring - prepare for intubation
pCO2 > 42mmHg in severe asthma indicates respiratory failure
Post-intubation deterioration: consider tube displacement, obstruction, pneumothorax, equipment failure, breath stacking
Preload with 20ml/kg IV fluids before intubation to avoid precipitous BP drop
Continue in-line nebulisations after intubation

disposition.moderate: Observe/Respiratory referral. May discharge if meets criteria.
disposition.severe: Urgent Internal Medicine referral, ICU/HCU admission

    "Pulse < 100 bpm"
  ],
  "moderate": [
    "Using accessory muscles",
    "PEFR/FEV1 50-80% predicted",
    "O2 sats 90-95%",
    "Pulse 100-120 bpm",
    "pH < 7.25"
  ],
  "severe": [
    "Altered consciousness",
    "PEFR/FEV1 < 50% or unable",
    "O2 sats < 90%",
    "Pulse > 120 bpm",
    "pH < 7.20",
    "pO2 < 60mmHg"
  ]
}
Risk factors: Smoking, Infection (viral or bacterial), Non-compliance with medications, Air pollution exposure, Co-morbid cardiac disease
Investigations - bedside: PEFR/FEV1, O2 sats, Vital signs, ABG (pH < 7.35 and pCO2 > 45 indicate need for NIV)
Investigations - lab: FBC, U&E, CRP, ABG/VBG
Investigations - imaging: CXR (exclude pneumothorax, pneumonia, cardiac failure)
Disposition: Mild: Discharge with antibiotics if indicated, steroids, inhalers, Resp OPD follow-up. Moderate-Severe: Admit. Severe with NIV: HCU/ICU.
Warnings: Find and treat the cause of acute exacerbation (infection, PE, pneumothorax, pneumonia, cardiac failure, non-compliance); NIV criteria: pH < 7.35 and pCO2 > 45mmHg; Avoid excessive O2 in CO2 retainers - target O2 sats 88-92%; Post-intubation: Preload with 20ml/kg IV fluids, continue inline nebulisations; If deterioration after intubation: consider tube displacement, obstruction, pneumothorax, equipment failure

components.name: B - Blood Pressure SBP ≤ 90mmHg or DBP ≤ 60mmHg

interpretation.meaning: Very severe (mortality 40%) - ICU/High Care admission, consider PJP/TB cover

CURB-65 0-1 (low risk): Amoxicillin 1g TDS PO x 5 days +/- Azithromycin 500mg OD PO x 3 days if atypical features on CXR. If penicillin allergic: Moxifloxacin 400mg PO x 5 days

CURB-65 3-5 (severe): Urgent admission. Augmentin 1.2g IV stat OR Ceftriaxone 2g IV stat AND Azithromycin 500mg PO/IV AND Hydrocortisone 100mg IV stat. ABG, FBC, U&E, CRP, blood cultures
CURB-65 4-5: ICU/High Care admission. Consider PJP/TB cover. Intubate if required
If TB suspected: advise sputum AFBs and GXP

components.name: L - No unilateral Lower limb oedema

management_steps.action: Calculate Wells score and PERC score
Low risk (Wells < 2) + PERC negative: PE excluded, no further testing
Low risk (Wells < 2) + PERC positive: D-dimer. If below age-adjusted level: PE excluded
Moderate risk (Wells 2-4): D-dimer. If above age-adjusted level: consider CTPA
High risk (Wells > 4): Start Clexane 1mg/kg SC stat (if no contraindication), proceed to CTPA (if GFR > 60ml/min)
Massive PE (sustained hypotension SBP < 90 for 15min, requiring inotropes, persistent bradycardia with shock, RV dysfunction on echo, myocardial necrosis): Thrombolysis
Thrombolysis for massive PE: Alteplase (Actilyse) ≥ 65kg: 10mg bolus then 90mg over 2 hours. < 65kg: 1.5mg/kg total (10mg bolus + remainder over 2 hours). Cardiac arrest: 50mg IVI stat
Stable on low-dose pressor: consider 10mg bolus then 40mg over 2 hours (0.5mg/kg)
Adjunct: 5mg nebulised nitrocine over 15 min, repeat PRN
Consider PESI score for risk stratification

drugs.notes: Massive PE only. Cardiac arrest: 50mg IVI stat.
Adjunct for massive PE

warnings: High index of suspicion in unexplained dyspnoea, tachypnoea, tachycardia, unilateral leg swelling

CTPA is gold standard (GFR > 60ml/min required)
McConnell's sign on echo: akinesia of mid RV free wall with sparing of apex = specific for PE

Signs: Tachypnoea, Tachycardia (HR > 100), Hypotension (massive PE), Hypoxaemia, Signs of DVT (unilateral leg swelling), Signs of RV strain, Shock (massive PE)

Broad spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition

Nosocomial infections, broader spectrum

Within 6 hours: apply vasopressors for MAP > 65, reassess volume status if persistent hypotension or lactate > 4, remeasure lactate

Don't wait for fluid bolus to finish before considering inotropic support

peripheral: Intermittent episodes seconds to < 1 min (BPPV) or hours to days (vestibular neuritis)

management_steps.action: Classify type of dizziness: vertigo, near-syncope, disequilibrium, non-specific
Differentiate peripheral vs central vertigo using clinical findings table
Acute vestibular syndrome (continuous vertigo > 24 hours with nausea, vomiting, ataxia, nystagmus): Apply HINTS exam
HINTS exam: Head Impulse Test (normal/negative = central = worrying), Nystagmus (bidirectional/direction-changing = central), Test of Skew (refixation = central)
Any single central HINTS sign = posterior circulation stroke until proven otherwise - urgent CT/MRI
BPPV: Dix-Hallpike to elicit nystagmus, then Epley manoeuvre to reposition canaliths
Meniere's disease: Recurrent attacks with unilateral ear fullness, hearing loss, roaring tinnitus. HINTS may be normal. NB: recurring attacks may represent TIAs/basilar ischemia
Symptomatic treatment: antiemetics, short-term vestibular suppressants
Refer all central causes for neurosurgical/neurology consultation

warnings: A normal (negative) head impulse test is worrying - suggests central cause (posterior circulation stroke)
Any central HINTS sign = stroke until proven otherwise
Do NOT attribute continuous vertigo in elderly/vascular patients to 'inner ear' without ruling out stroke
Meniere's attacks with accelerated frequency may represent impending basilar ischemia/TIAs
Vertebrobasilar insufficiency can cause isolated vertigo without other neurological signs
All patients with acute vestibular syndrome and any central sign need urgent neuroimaging
protocols.disposition: Central vertigo/stroke: Admit. Peripheral vertigo (BPPV, vestibular neuritis): May discharge with follow-up if no red flags.

}

protocols.item: ENT: Epistaxis, Croup, Epiglottitis, Acute Otitis Media, Pharyngitis
protocols.notes_updates: EPISTAXIS | Important information to note: Duration/frequency/side, Severity & estimated blood loss, Bleeding diathesis, Comorbidities, Current medication (oral/nasal), History of recent trauma | Investigations: Blood tests are not routinely required. Excessive bleeding -> Ward Hb. FBC/INR if: Shocked, Age > 65 years, Pale, On Warfarin, Bleeding disorder | NO evidence between hypertension & epistaxis. Epistaxis is more common in hypertensive patients due to vascular fragility. Epistaxis & the associated anxiety cause an acute elevation in BP | Immediate Management: ABCD approach. Ask patient to blow nose to remove all clots. Sit patient forward & ask them to pinch their nose (press alae firmly against septum) for 10-15 minutes. Patient to spit out excess blood (swallowing will cause vomiting). If bleeding stops -> observe for 30 mins for re-bleeding. If no re-bleeding -> Discharge. TTO: Bactroban ointment apply 8 hourly for 3/7 | Still bleeding / Re-bleed -> Nasal Packing: Oxymetazoline (Iliadin) nasal spray 2 puffs. Anaesthetise prior to packing: Cotton wool soaked in 2% Lignocaine placed into the nose. Nasal nerve block. Merocel Nasal Tampon: Trim tampon if patient has a small nose, Insert along the floor of the nostril, Pack both nostrils -> tamponade, Expand with adrenaline 1:10 000, Leave in situ for 24-48 hours. If no nasal tampons: Soak a 10 x 10 cm piece of gauze in Cyclokapron [500mg/5ml] - insert into bleeding nostril. Pack with ribbon gauze soaked in 1:10 000 adrenaline | Still bleeding? Posterior bleed is likely -> Posterior Nasal Packing: Call a senior doctor. Administer Cyclokapron 15mg/kg (max 1g) IV over 15 min. Remove nasal packing. Insert a Foley's catheter (size 10-14F) along the floor of the nasal cavity until the tip is visualized in the oropharynx. Inflate Foley's bulb with +/-10-20ml of water for injection. Retract catheter gently so that it butts against the posterior palate. Keep catheter in place by clamping with an umbilical cord clamp. Place gauze around the umbilical cord clamp as well as between the alar/nose & clamp to prevent pressure necrosis. Repack both nostrils with new anterior packing. ENT Referral | Admission Criteria: Elderly, Posterior nasal packing, COPD, Cardiac disorders | Discharge Instructions: Counsel patient on how to stop own bleeds (pinching nares for at least 15 minutes). Avoid blowing nose, sniffing & nose picking. Encourage nasal hydration - Normal saline drops / Vaseline to keep nostrils moist. If discharged with anterior packing in situ: refer within 48 hours for removal & review at ENT OPD, prophylactic antibiotics - Augmentin oral for 3/7 | CROUP | Presentation: Barking cough, Inspiratory stridor, widespread wheeze, increased WOB. +/- Fever, but no signs of toxicity. Age: 6-36 months, can affect older children. Uncommon < 6 months, rare < 3 months. Rapid onset, progress over 12 to 48 hours. 1-4 day preceding coryzal illness. Clear rhinorrhoea, low grade temperature, mild tachypnoea. Males > females. Winter, seasonal peak in spring. Common viral: parainfluenza 1 & 3, RSV, Influenza A & B. Common bacterial: Staph Aureus | Westley Croup Severity Score: Stridor (None=0, With agitation=1, At rest=2), Chest wall retractions (None=0, Mild=1, Moderate=2, Severe/Worsening=3), Air entry (Normal=0, Decreased=1, Severely decreased=2), Cyanosis (None=0, With agitation=1, At rest=2), Conscious Level (Normal=0, Altered=2). Mild <=2, Moderate 3-7, Severe 8-11, Impending Respiratory Failure >=12 | Clinical Assessment: Minimal handling, Position of comfort, Defer IV access, Avoid distress - nurse with parents, limit exam/phlebotomy, Clinical diagnosis - CXR only if doubt of diagnosis, Use Westley Croup severity score to assess and manage | Croup Treatment - Mild (<=2): Steroids ALL children with croup require steroid administration. Oral: Prednisolone 1mg/kg x 2/7 OR Dexamethasone single dose 0.15mg/kg (max 10mg). IM: Dexamethasone 0.15mg/kg/dose (max 10mg). Budesonide 2mg - can mix with adrenalin. No need for nebulisation. No need for adrenalin. Oxygen only if SATS <93%. Observations: 30 minutes post steroid administration. Intubation: Not required | Croup Treatment - Moderate (3-7): Steroids - Oral: Prednisolone 1mg/kg x 2/7 OR Dexamethasone single dose 0.15mg/kg (max 10mg). IM: Dexamethasone 0.15mg/kg/dose (max 10mg). Budesonide 2mg - can mix with adrenalin. Nebulise. Adrenalin (1:1,000) 0.5ml/kg/dose Max 5ml - Requires adrenalin nebs if stridor is not improving or worsening, Repeated 15-20 minutes. Oxygen only if SATS <93%. Observations: 30-60 minutes. Intubation: Not required. No improvement: Improvement but then deterioration, Consider differential diagnosis if no improvement, Consider transfer to appropriate facility, Call for help - paediatrician and/or anaesthetist | Croup Treatment - Severe (8-11): Steroids - IM or IV: Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg (max 12mg). Budesonide 2mg - Nebulise. Adrenalin (1:1,000) 0.5ml/kg/dose Max 5ml - Requires adrenalin nebs if stridor is not improving or worsening, Repeated 15-20 minutes. Oxygen only if SATS <93%. Observations: Move to resuscitation area for constant monitoring. Intubate if: Rapid relapse, No response to adrenalin, Most senior clinician should perform intubation (paediatrician or anaesthetist). No improvement: Call for help - paediatrician and/or anaesthetist, Consider differential diagnosis if no improvement, Consider transfer to appropriate facility/ICU | Croup Treatment - Respiratory Failure (>=12): Steroids - IM or IV: Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg (max 12mg). Budesonide 2mg - Nebulise continuously. Adrenalin (1:1,000) 0.5ml/kg/dose Max 5ml - Requires continuous adrenalin nebs if in extremis. Oxygen only if SATS <93%. Observations: Call for HELP. Must be in resuscitation area and monitored continuously. Intubate if: Rapid relapse, No response to adrenalin, Most senior clinician should perform intubation (paediatrician or anaesthetist), Need 2 ET tubes: 1 smaller size than usual, Etomidate 0.3mg/kg IVI recommended for induction | Urgent Paeds Referral: Severe airway obstruction, No improvement with nebulized adrenaline, >2 doses nebulized adrenaline required | Discharge Criteria: Stable vitals, No stridor at rest, Normal colour & LOC, Tolerating oral fluids, Minimum 30 minutes post steroids, Minimum 3 hours post adrenaline, Access to transport to return. Mild distress: own transport back. Severe distress/agitation/cyanosis: EMS | Admission Criteria - Absolute: Symptoms persisting >4 hours after steroid administration, Audible stridor at rest, Significant resp effort, O2 requirement, Poor response to ED treatment, Hx of severe obstruction prior to presentation, Hx of previous severe croup, Known structural airway abnormality. Relative: <6 months, Poor oral intake/dehydration, Social issues (distance from facility/transport/inadequate monitoring, repeat presentation), Late evening/night presentation, Uncertain diagnosis | EPIGLOTTITIS | Clinical Features: Drooling, Dysphagia, Dysphonia, Dyspnoea, Distress, Acutely ill with high fever, 'Hot potato voice', Unable to swallow, Tripod position - allow pt to remain in position of comfort. Thumbprint sign on X-ray | Management: URGENT ENT consult - intubate in theatre. Prepare for emergency cricothyrotomy if pt must be intubated in ED. Defer painful procedures e.g. bloods in paediatric pts. Defer X-rays unless there is diagnostic uncertainty. Ceftriaxone 2g IV stat | Airway Pearls: Fibreoptic laryngoscopy = gold standard. Avoid supraglottic devices. Dual setup for surgical airway. No benefit to steroids/nebulized adrenaline | ACUTE OTITIS MEDIA | Clinical Features: Ear pain, Fever, Anorexia, Vomiting, Lethargy, Middle ear effusion, Haemorrhagic or cloudy tympanic membrane, Bulging tympanic membrane | Management: Ear pain - oral paracetamol/NSAIDS. Consider topical anaesthetic eardrops if TM is intact. Most cases resolve spontaneously - limit use of antibiotics. Consider antibiotics if: AOM in the only hearing ear, Cochlear implants. Amoxicillin 45mg/kg BD 5/7. Alternatives: Cefuroxime/Azithromycin. Failure to respond to first line ABs or observation within 48-72 hours: Initiate antibiotics if pt managed conservatively, Initiate Co-amoxyclav (Augmentin) 22.5mg/kg/dose BD 5/7 | Complications: TM perforation - does not alter Mx. Acute mastoiditis - urgent ENT referral, IV antibiotics, +/- CT Brain/mastoid. Effusion - refer to ENT if >3/12 persistent effusion. Paediatric and ENT referral: Systemically unwell child, Neonate, Acute mastoiditis, Cochlear implant | PHARYNGITIS & TONSILLITIS | Clinical Features - Bacterial: Group A Beta-haemolytic strep 50% of bacterial infections. Typically age 5-15 (rare under 2 years). Throat pain, Fever, Anorexia/dehydration due to odynophagia, Vomiting (more common in children), Lethargy (children), Hoarseness or voice changes - consider other pathology!, Tonsillar exudate MAY occur, Palatal petechiae/scarlatiform rash = bacterial (uncommon findings) | Modified Centor Score for Strep Pharyngitis: Age 3-14 (+1), 15-44 (0), >45 (-1). Exudate or swelling on tonsils (+1). Tender/swollen anterior cervical lymph nodes (+1). Fever Temp >38C (+1). Cough Present (0), Absent (+1). 0-2: Strep unlikely, 2-3: throat culture/rapid strep test, >4: Strep likely - antibiotics | Differential Diagnosis, Mimics and Complications: Viral pharyngitis - 90% of cases. Cough, coryza, conjunctivitis. Deep neck space infection, Ludwigs angina: trismus, muffled voice, decreased neck range of motion - urgent CT, IV antibiotics. Infectious mononucleosis (EBV/CMV): posterior cervical chain lymphadenopathy. Epiglottitis: toxic child in tripod position. Diphtheria: stridor, drooling, pseudomembrane. Peritonsillar abscess: POCUS can assist with dx. Lemierre syndrome: rare complication - thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein - bacteraemia and septic emboli. Oropharyngeal malignancy: pain >7 days, smoking, ETOH use | Management: Discharge with amoxicillin 50mg/kg 10/7 and GOOD ANALGESIA. Macrolide in penicillin allergy e.g. Azithromycin. Admit if: Unable to swallow, Systemic illness, Risk of upper airway obstruction, Peritonsillar abscess, Diagnostic uncertainty, Concern for acute rheumatic fever/glomerulonephritis

drugs.paediatric_dose: Not specified for ENT; 22.5mg/kg/dose BD 5/7 for AOM second line; 30mg/kg IV for appendicitis
drugs.notes: Prophylactic antibiotics after anterior packing in situ for epistaxis; also for AOM second line
For mild and moderate croup

drugs.notes: All children with croup require steroids
Can mix with adrenalin for croup. Nebulise for moderate/severe, nebulise continuously for respiratory failure

drugs.paediatric_dose: Not specified for epiglottitis in this protocol
drugs.notes: For epiglottitis

drugs.paediatric_dose: Oral for AOM ear pain; oral for pharyngitis analgesia
drugs.notes: Ear pain in AOM; analgesia for pharyngitis

drugs.paediatric_dose: Oral for AOM ear pain; oral for pharyngitis analgesia
drugs.notes: Ear pain in AOM; analgesia for pharyngitis
For AOM ear pain
First line for AOM when antibiotics indicated
Alternative for AOM

drugs.notes: Alternative for AOM; pharyngitis in penicillin allergy
Second line for AOM if failure to respond within 48-72 hours
For strep pharyngitis

management_steps.details: ABCD approach. Note duration, frequency, side, severity, estimated blood loss, bleeding diathesis, comorbidities, current medication, recent trauma.
management_steps.caution: FBC/INR only if shocked, age >65, pale, on warfarin, or bleeding disorder

management_steps.details: Ask patient to blow nose to remove clots. Sit patient forward. Ask them to pinch nose (press alae firmly against septum) for 10-15 minutes. Patient spits out excess blood.
management_steps.caution: Swallowing blood will cause vomiting

management_steps.details: Observe for 30 mins for re-bleeding. If no re-bleeding -> Discharge with Bactroban ointment 8 hourly for 3/7.
management_steps.caution: Counsel patient on self-management: pinch nares for 15 min, avoid blowing nose/sniffing/picking, use saline drops/Vaseline

management_steps.action: Epistaxis - If still bleeding or re-bleeds: Anterior packing
management_steps.details: Oxymetazoline (Iliadin) nasal spray 2 puffs. Anaesthetise with 2% Lignocaine cotton wool. Use Merocel Nasal Tampon along floor of nostril, pack both nostrils, expand with adrenaline 1:10 000, leave 24-48h. Alternative: gauze soaked in Cyclokapron 500mg/5ml.
management_steps.caution: Trim tampon if small nose. If packing in situ, refer ENT OPD within 48h, give Augmentin oral 3/7 prophylaxis

management_steps.action: Epistaxis - If still bleeding: Posterior packing
management_steps.details: Call senior doctor. Cyclokapron 15mg/kg (max 1g) IV over 15 min. Remove anterior packing. Insert Foley catheter 10-14F along floor of nasal cavity until visualized in oropharynx. Inflate bulb with 10-20ml water. Retract gently against posterior palate. Secure with umbilical cord clamp. Repack both nostrils. ENT referral.
management_steps.caution: Place gauze around clamp and between alar/nose & clamp to prevent pressure necrosis

management_steps.details: Minimal handling, position of comfort, defer IV access, avoid distress. Clinical diagnosis. Use Westley Croup Severity Score. Mild <=2, Moderate 3-7, Severe 8-11, Respiratory Failure >=12.
management_steps.caution: CXR only if diagnostic doubt

management_steps.details: Steroids: Prednisolone 1mg/kg PO x 2/7 OR Dexamethasone 0.15mg/kg PO/IM single dose (max 10mg). Budesonide 2mg. No nebulisation or adrenalin needed. O2 only if SATS <93%. Observe 30 min post steroid.
management_steps.caution: ALL children with croup require steroids

management_steps.details: Steroids as for mild. Budesonide 2mg nebulised. Adrenalin (1:1,000) 0.5ml/kg/dose (max 5ml) nebulised, repeated 15-20 min if stridor not improving. O2 if SATS <93%. Observe 30-60 min.
management_steps.caution: Requires adrenalin nebs if stridor not improving or worsening

management_steps.details: Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg IM/IV (max 12mg). Budesonide 2mg nebulised. Adrenalin (1:1,000) 0.5ml/kg/dose (max 5ml) nebulised, repeated 15-20 min. Move to resuscitation. O2 if SATS <93%.
management_steps.caution: Intubate if rapid relapse or no response to adrenalin. Most senior clinician should intubate.

management_steps.details: Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg IM/IV (max 12mg). Budesonide 2mg nebulised continuously. Adrenalin (1:1,000) 0.5ml/kg/dose (max 5ml) continuous nebulisation if in extremis. Resuscitation area. Call for HELP.
management_steps.caution: Need 2 ET tubes (1 smaller size). Etomidate 0.3mg/kg IVI recommended for induction. Most senior clinician must intubate.

management_steps.details: Stable vitals, no stridor at rest, normal colour & LOC, tolerating oral fluids, minimum 30 min post steroids, minimum 3 hours post adrenalin, access to transport.
management_steps.caution: Mild distress: own transport. Severe distress/agitation/cyanosis: EMS transport

management_steps.details: URGENT ENT consult - intubate in theatre. Prepare for emergency cricothyrotomy if must intubate in ED. Defer painful procedures in paediatric patients. Defer X-rays unless diagnostic uncertainty. Ceftriaxone 2g IV stat.
management_steps.caution: Fibreoptic laryngoscopy = gold standard. Avoid supraglottic devices. Dual setup for surgical airway. No benefit to steroids/nebulized adrenalin.

management_steps.details: Analgesia: oral paracetamol/NSAIDs. Consider topical anaesthetic eardrops if TM intact. Most cases resolve spontaneously - limit antibiotics. Antibiotics if: AOM in only hearing ear, cochlear implants. Amoxicillin 45mg/kg BD 5/7 first line.
management_steps.caution: TM perforation does not alter management

management_steps.details: If failure to respond to first line or observation within 48-72 hours: Co-amoxyclav (Augmentin) 22.5mg/kg/dose BD 5/7. Refer ENT if effusion >3 months.
management_steps.caution: Urgent ENT referral for: systemically unwell child, neonate, acute mastoiditis, cochlear implant

management_steps.details: Use Modified Centor Score. Consider bacterial vs viral. 0-2: strep unlikely. 2-3: throat culture/rapid strep test. >4: strep likely - antibiotics.
management_steps.caution: 90% of pharyngitis cases are viral

management_steps.details: Discharge with amoxicillin 50mg/kg 10/7 and good analgesia. Macrolide (e.g. Azithromycin) in penicillin allergy.
management_steps.caution: Admit if: unable to swallow, systemic illness, risk of upper airway obstruction, peritonsillar abscess, diagnostic uncertainty, concern for acute rheumatic fever/glomerulonephritis

westley_croup_score.interpretation: Mild <=2, Moderate 3-7, Severe 8-11, Impending Respiratory Failure >=12

modified_centor_score.interpretation: 0-2: Strep unlikely, 2-3: throat culture/rapid strep test, >4: Strep likely - antibiotics

diagnostic_criteria.epistaxis_admission_criteria: Elderly, Posterior nasal packing, COPD, Cardiac disorders
diagnostic_criteria.croup_admission_absolute: Symptoms persisting >4 hours after steroid administration, Audible stridor at rest, Significant respiratory effort, O2 requirement, Poor response to ED treatment, Hx of severe obstruction prior to presentation, Hx of previous severe croup, Known structural airway abnormality
diagnostic_criteria.croup_admission_relative: <6 months, Poor oral intake/dehydration, Social issues, Late evening/night presentation, Uncertain diagnosis

clinical_features.epistaxis: Duration/frequency/side, severity & estimated blood loss, bleeding diathesis, comorbidities, current medication, recent trauma. NOT caused by hypertension directly but more common in hypertensives due to vascular fragility.
clinical_features.croup: Barking cough, inspiratory stridor, widespread wheeze, increased work of breathing, +/- fever, no signs of toxicity. Age 6-36 months. Rapid onset over 12-48 hours. Preceding coryzal illness 1-4 days. Males > females. Winter/spring seasonal.
clinical_features.epiglottitis: Drooling, dysphagia, dysphonia, dyspnoea, distress, acutely ill with high fever, hot potato voice, unable to swallow, tripod position. Thumbprint sign on X-ray.
clinical_features.acute_otitis_media: Ear pain, fever, anorexia, vomiting, lethargy, middle ear effusion, haemorrhagic or cloudy TM, bulging TM
clinical_features.pharyngitis_bacterial: Group A Beta-haemolytic strep 50% of bacterial infections. Age 5-15 (rare under 2 years). Throat pain, fever, anorexia/dehydration due to odynophagia, vomiting (more common in children), lethargy, hoarseness/voice changes, tonsillar exudate may occur, palatal petechiae/scarlatiform rash = bacterial (uncommon)

protocols.monitoring: Croup: Observe 30 min post steroid for mild, 30-60 min for moderate, continuous monitoring for severe/respiratory failure. Epistaxis: Observe 30 min for re-bleeding after initial control.
warnings: Epistaxis: NO evidence between hypertension and epistaxis as causative, but more common in hypertensive patients due to vascular fragility. Epistaxis and associated anxiety cause acute elevation in BP.
Epiglottitis: Avoid supraglottic devices. Dual setup for surgical airway. No benefit to steroids/nebulized adrenaline.
Croup: ALL children with croup require steroids. Defer IV access. Minimal handling. Position of comfort.
Croup intubation: Need 2 ET tubes (1 smaller size than usual). Etomidate 0.3mg/kg IVI recommended for induction.
AOM: Most cases resolve spontaneously - limit use of antibiotics.
Pharyngitis: 90% of cases are viral. Consider deep neck space infection, epiglottitis, diphtheria, peritonsillar abscess in differential.

disposition.epistaxis_discharge: If no re-bleeding after 30 min observation. Counsel on self-management. Bactroban ointment. If anterior packing in situ: refer ENT OPD within 48h, prophylactic Augmentin oral 3/7.
disposition.epistaxis_admit: Elderly, posterior nasal packing, COPD, cardiac disorders.
disposition.croup_discharge: Stable vitals, no stridor at rest, normal colour & LOC, tolerating oral fluids, 30 min post steroids, 3 hours post adrenalin.
disposition.croup_admit_absolute: Symptoms >4h post steroids, stridor at rest, significant respiratory effort, O2 requirement, poor response to ED treatment, hx severe obstruction, hx previous severe croup, known structural airway abnormality.
disposition.croup_admit_relative: <6 months, poor oral intake/dehydration, social issues, late evening/night presentation, uncertain diagnosis.
disposition.epiglottitis: URGENT ENT consult, intubate in theatre.
disposition.aom_refer: Systemically unwell child, neonate, acute mastoiditis, cochlear implant.
disposition.pharyngitis_admit: Unable to swallow, systemic illness, risk of upper airway obstruction, peritonsillar abscess, diagnostic uncertainty, concern for acute rheumatic fever/glomerulonephritis.

protocols.notes_updates: SIGNS OF DEHYDRATION | Loss of weight | Sunken eyes & fontanelle | Dry mucous membranes | Thirst | Loss of skin turgor | Acidotic breathing | Decreased urine output | SIGNS OF SHOCK | CRT > 3 secs | Cold peripheries | Thready pulses | Listless | Tachycardia | Decreased BP | Depressed LOC | MANAGEMENT OF SHOCK & SEVERE DEHYDRATION | Resuscitation: ABC approach, Oxygen, monitors, IV line. Fluid bolus 20ml/kg with a crystalloid (Ringers Lactate / 0.9% Normal Saline) unless: Severely malnourished, Cardiac failure, Severe pneumonia, Hypernatraemia (Na+ >150 mmol/l), Encephalopathy, <3 months old. Still shocked - give 10ml/kg boluses until pulse is palpable or maximum of 60ml/kg in total has been given. | Correct Electrolyte Abnormalities: Hypokalaemia (K+ <2.5): 15% KCl (ml) = [(0.3) x (weight in kgs) x (4)] / 2 (correct over 4 hours). Hypoglycaemia: 2ml/kg of 25% Dextrose (5ml 50% dextrose + 5ml NS). | INDICATIONS FOR BLOOD GAS: ALL severely dehydrated & shocked children | Malnourished | Moderately dehydrated but with an atypical presentation | If IV rehydration is required | Complications - ileus, seizures | INDICATIONS FOR ANTIBIOTICS: Severe malnutrition | Suspected systemic infection | Immunocompromised | Dysentery | Cholera with severe dehydration | NB! All IV fluids must be controlled and preferably given using an infusion pump | MANAGEMENT OF MILD TO MODERATE DEHYDRATION | First Line = Oral rehydration solution (ORS). Oral or via NGT. Volume according to the degree of dehydration: 12.5ml/kg/hr for 4 hours. Reassess: if tolerated & improving clinically -> Discharge. Not tolerated & no clinical improvement -> Admit & commence IV fluids - 1/2 DD @ 10mls/kg/hr | Special investigations: Urine dipstix & blood glucose on ALL patients. Blood gas (see indications). Tailor further blood & radiological investigations to clinical findings. | Disposition: Urgent Paediatric referral

drugs.paediatric_dose: 20ml/kg fluid bolus for shock/severe dehydration; 10ml/kg repeat boluses until pulse palpable or max 60ml/kg total
drugs.notes: Contraindications to rapid bolus: severely malnourished, cardiac failure, severe pneumonia, hypernatraemia (Na+>150), encephalopathy, <3 months old

drugs.paediatric_dose: [(0.3) x (weight in kg) x (4)] / 2 ml of 15% KCl (correct over 4 hours)
drugs.notes: For K+ <2.5. All IV fluids must be controlled and preferably given using infusion pump.
For hypoglycaemia. Prepared as 5ml 50% dextrose + 5ml NS
First line for mild to moderate dehydration. Reassess after 4 hours.
For admitted patients with mild-moderate dehydration not tolerating ORS

management_steps.details: Signs of dehydration: loss of weight, sunken eyes/fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, thirst, loss of skin turgor, acidotic breathing, decreased urine output. Signs of shock: CRT >3 secs, cold peripheries, thready pulses, listless, tachycardia, decreased BP, depressed LOC.
management_steps.caution: Assess degree of dehydration accurately to guide management

management_steps.details: ABC approach. Oxygen, monitors, IV line. Fluid bolus 20ml/kg with crystalloid (Ringers Lactate / 0.9% NS). If still shocked, give 10ml/kg boluses until pulse palpable or maximum 60ml/kg total.
management_steps.caution: DO NOT give rapid bolus if: severely malnourished, cardiac failure, severe pneumonia, hypernatraemia (Na+>150), encephalopathy, <3 months old

management_steps.details: Hypokalaemia (K+<2.5): 15% KCl (ml) = [(0.3) x weight(kg) x 4] / 2, correct over 4 hours. Hypoglycaemia: 2ml/kg of 25% Dextrose (5ml 50% dextrose + 5ml NS).
management_steps.caution: All IV fluids must be controlled and preferably given using an infusion pump

management_steps.details: Urine dipstix and blood glucose on ALL patients. Blood gas if: severely dehydrated/shocked, malnourished, moderately dehydrated with atypical presentation, IV rehydration required, complications (ileus, seizures). Tailor further investigations to clinical findings.
management_steps.caution: Consider antibiotics if: severe malnutrition, suspected systemic infection, immunocompromised, dysentery, cholera with severe dehydration

management_steps.details: First line = Oral rehydration solution (ORS). Oral or via NGT. 12.5ml/kg/hr for 4 hours. Reassess: if tolerated and improving -> discharge. If not tolerated and no improvement -> admit and commence IV fluids 1/2 DD @ 10mls/kg/hr.
management_steps.caution: Ensure correct preparation of ORS

management_steps.details: Urgent Paediatric referral for all patients requiring admission.
management_steps.caution: Monitor for complications: ileus, seizures, electrolyte abnormalities

diagnostic_criteria.signs_of_dehydration: Loss of weight, sunken eyes & fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, thirst, loss of skin turgor, acidotic breathing, decreased urine output
diagnostic_criteria.signs_of_shock: CRT >3 secs, cold peripheries, thready pulses, listless, tachycardia, decreased BP, depressed LOC
diagnostic_criteria.indications_for_blood_gas: ALL severely dehydrated & shocked children, malnourished, moderately dehydrated with atypical presentation, if IV rehydration required, complications (ileus, seizures)
diagnostic_criteria.indications_for_antibiotics: Severe malnutrition, suspected systemic infection, immunocompromised, dysentery, cholera with severe dehydration

clinical_features.dehydration: Loss of weight, sunken eyes & fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, thirst, loss of skin turgor, acidotic breathing, decreased urine output
clinical_features.shock: CRT >3 secs, cold peripheries, thready pulses, listless, tachycardia, decreased BP, depressed LOC

protocols.monitoring: Monitor vitals, CRT, urine output. All IV fluids must be controlled and preferably given using an infusion pump. Reassess at 4 hours after ORS initiation.
warnings: DO NOT give rapid fluid bolus if: severely malnourished, cardiac failure, severe pneumonia, hypernatraemia (Na+>150 mmol/l), encephalopathy, <3 months old
All IV fluids must be controlled and preferably given using an infusion pump
Consider antibiotics for: severe malnutrition, suspected systemic infection, immunocompromised, dysentery, cholera with severe dehydration
protocols.disposition: Urgent Paediatric referral. Discharge if mild-moderate dehydration improving with ORS. Admit if not tolerating ORS, no clinical improvement, severe dehydration, shock, or any complications.

protocols.notes_updates: SECONDARY HEADACHE - DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Intracranial haemorrhage: Hx of trauma, hypertension, thunderclap headache, anticoagulant use | Infection - intra OR extracranial: fever, vomiting, meningism, rash, photophobia. NB Malaria | Space occupying lesions: High index of suspicion in HIV, progressive symptoms, focal neurology | Carotid/vertebrobasilar dissection: Neck pain, cerebellar or cranial nerve fallout, trivial trauma | Cerebral venous thrombosis: Gradual onset, facial swelling/erythema, subtle CT findings often missed | Temporal arteritis: Jaw pain, tender temporal area/scalp, absent temporal artery pulsation | Acute hydrocephalus: Structural abnormalities, post infective, previous shunt | Hypertensive encephalopathy: Poorly controlled, hypertensive emergency | Pre-eclampsia: ALWAYS check pregnancy test | Acute angle closure glaucoma: Visual loss, painful red eye, pupillary defect, raised IOP | Carbon monoxide toxicity: Fire in enclosed space | ENT/dental pathology: Infective, traumatic, chronic | Shunt failure/pathology: Prior neurosurgery/hydrocephalus | HEADACHE RED FLAGS | Onset: Sudden/thunderclap, exertional, post traumatic | Symptoms: Altered mental state, seizure, focal neurology, meningism, visual changes, fever | Medications: ARVs, TB Rx, recent antimicrobials, immunosuppressants, anticoagulants/anti-platelet agents, illicit drugs | History: No prior headaches, worst headache ever, worsening over weeks/months, immune compromise, subtle neurology/cognitive decline | Comorbidities: Malignancy, immunosuppression, vasculitis, recent/current pregnancy, bleeding/clotting disorders, poorly controlled HT, previous neurosurgery | Examination: Abnormal vital signs, altered mental status, focal neurology, meningism, features of raised ICP | PRIMARY HEADACHE SYNDROMES | TENSION: Pressure/tightness, Bilateral/frontal, 30 minutes-7 days, Stiff/sore shoulder/neck muscles/eye strain/poor posture, Mild-Mod severity, Equal gender, NSAIDs/Paracetamol/non-pharmacological measures e.g. massage | MIGRAINE: Gradual onset pulsating/throbbing, Unilateral (70%) or bilateral, 4-72 hrs, Nausea/vomiting/photophobia/visual auras, Mod-Severe, Females, Metoclopramide/triptans/prochlorperazine/NSAIDs/paracetamol/consider steroids. AVOID OPIOIDS | CLUSTER: Rapid onset deep/excruciating/explosive, Unilateral peri-orbital/temporal, 15 minutes-3 hours, Ipsilateral eye redness/lacrimation/rhinorrhoea/sweating/Horner Syndrome, Severe, Males, Oxygen/Paracetamol/NSAIDs. Consider triptans/opioids/steroids/ketamine in severe refractory cases | DISPOSITION | Neuro/neurosurgical consult as appropriate | Close monitoring for deterioration if secondary headache | Consider outpatient Neurology review for primary headache syndromes

drugs.notes: For tension headache, migraine, cluster headache
For tension headache, migraine, cluster headache
For migraine
For migraine and cluster headache
For migraine
Consider for migraine and cluster headache
For cluster headache
For severe refractory cluster headache cases only

management_steps.details: Onset: Sudden/thunderclap, exertional, post traumatic. Symptoms: Altered mental state, seizure, focal neurology, meningism, visual changes, fever. Medications: ARVs, TB Rx, recent antimicrobials, immunosuppressants, anticoagulants/anti-platelet agents, illicit drugs. History: No prior headaches, worst headache ever, worsening over weeks/months, immune compromise. Comorbidities: Malignancy, immunosuppression, vasculitis, recent/current pregnancy, bleeding/clotting disorders, poorly controlled HT, previous neurosurgery. Examination: Abnormal vital signs, altered mental status, focal neurology, meningism, features of raised ICP.
management_steps.caution: ALWAYS check pregnancy test. NB Malaria in endemic areas.

management_steps.details: Secondary causes: Intracranial haemorrhage, infection, space occupying lesions, carotid/vertebrobasilar dissection, cerebral venous thrombosis, temporal arteritis, acute hydrocephalus, hypertensive encephalopathy, pre-eclampsia, acute angle closure glaucoma, carbon monoxide toxicity, ENT/dental pathology, shunt failure.
management_steps.caution: Any red flag = secondary headache until proven otherwise

management_steps.details: TENSION: Pressure/tightness, bilateral/frontal, 30 min-7 days, stiff/sore neck/shoulders, mild-mod, equal gender. MIGRAINE: Pulsating/throbbing, unilateral(70%), 4-72 hrs, nausea/vomiting/photophobia/auras, mod-severe, females. CLUSTER: Deep/excruciating/explosive, unilateral peri-orbital/temporal, 15 min-3 hrs, ipsilateral redness/lacrimation/rhinorrhoea/Horner, severe, males.
management_steps.caution: AVOID OPIOIDS in migraine

management_steps.details: TENSION: NSAIDs, Paracetamol, non-pharmacological measures (massage). MIGRAINE: Metoclopramide, triptans, prochlorperazine, NSAIDs, paracetamol, consider steroids. CLUSTER: Oxygen, Paracetamol, NSAIDs. Consider triptans, opioids, steroids, ketamine in severe refractory cases.
management_steps.caution: AVOID OPIOIDS in migraine

management_steps.details: Neuro/neurosurgical consult as appropriate for secondary headaches. Close monitoring for deterioration if secondary headache. Consider outpatient Neurology review for primary headache syndromes.
management_steps.caution: Secondary headaches require urgent evaluation and appropriate specialist referral

diagnostic_criteria.headache_red_flags_symptoms: Altered mental state, seizure, focal neurology, meningism, visual changes, fever
diagnostic_criteria.headache_red_flags_medications: ARVs, TB Rx, recent antimicrobials, immunosuppressants, anticoagulants/anti-platelet agents, illicit drugs
diagnostic_criteria.headache_red_flags_history: No prior headaches, worst headache ever, worsening over weeks/months, immune compromise, subtle neurology/cognitive decline
diagnostic_criteria.headache_red_flags_comorbidities: Malignancy, immunosuppression, vasculitis, recent/current pregnancy, bleeding/clotting disorders, poorly controlled HT, previous neurosurgery
diagnostic_criteria.headache_red_flags_examination: Abnormal vital signs, altered mental status, focal neurology, meningism, features of raised ICP
diagnostic_criteria.tension_headache: Pressure/tightness, bilateral frontal, 30 min-7 days, stiff/sore neck/shoulders/eye strain, mild-moderate, equal gender
diagnostic_criteria.migraine: Gradual onset pulsating/throbbing, unilateral(70%) or bilateral, 4-72 hrs, nausea/vomiting/photophobia/visual auras, moderate-severe, females
diagnostic_criteria.cluster_headache: Rapid onset deep/excruciating/explosive, unilateral peri-orbital/temporal, 15 min-3 hrs, ipsilateral eye redness/lacrimation/rhinorrhoea/sweating/Horner syndrome, severe, males

secondary_headache_causes.intracranial_haemorrhage: Hx of trauma, hypertension, thunderclap headache, anticoagulant use
secondary_headache_causes.infection: Fever, vomiting, meningism, rash, photophobia. NB Malaria
secondary_headache_causes.space_occupying_lesions: High index of suspicion in HIV, progressive symptoms, focal neurology
secondary_headache_causes.carotid_vertebrobasilar_dissection: Neck pain, cerebellar or cranial nerve fallout, trivial trauma
secondary_headache_causes.cerebral_venous_thrombosis: Gradual onset, facial swelling/erythema, subtle CT findings often missed
secondary_headache_causes.temporal_arteritis: Jaw pain, tender temporal area/scalp, absent temporal artery pulsation
secondary_headache_causes.acute_hydrocephalus: Structural abnormalities, post infective, previous shunt
secondary_headache_causes.acute_angle_closure_glaucoma: Visual loss, painful red eye, pupillary defect, raised IOP

clinical_features.primary_headache_tension: Pressure/tightness, bilateral frontal, 30 min-7 days, stiff/sore neck/shoulders/eye strain/poor posture, mild-moderate, equal gender
clinical_features.primary_headache_migraine: Gradual onset pulsating/throbbing, unilateral(70%) or bilateral, 4-72 hrs, nausea/vomiting/photophobia/visual auras, moderate-severe, females
clinical_features.primary_headache_cluster: Rapid onset deep/excruciating/explosive, unilateral peri-orbital/temporal, 15 min-3 hrs, ipsilateral eye redness/lacrimation/rhinorrhoea/sweating/Horner syndrome, severe, males

protocols.monitoring: Close monitoring for deterioration if secondary headache. Monitor vital signs, GCS, focal neurology.
warnings: AVOID OPIOIDS in migraine
ALWAYS check pregnancy test - consider pre-eclampsia
NB Malaria in endemic areas for headache with fever
Secondary headaches require urgent evaluation and appropriate specialist referral
Cluster headache: consider triptans, opioids, steroids, ketamine in severe refractory cases only
protocols.disposition: Neuro/neurosurgical consult as appropriate for secondary headaches. Close monitoring for deterioration if secondary headache. Consider outpatient Neurology review for primary headache syndromes.

protocols.notes_updates: SICKLE CELL CRISIS presentations include: Fever, Vaso-occlusive crisis, Acute chest syndrome, Acute splenic sequestration, Aplastic crisis, Stroke, Priapism | High index of suspicion required - patients may have normal vitals despite serious pathology | Triage in for even minor complaints and involve Haematology early | Precipitants: Infection, Dehydration, Hypoxia, Drugs (sedatives, local anaesthetics), Changes in temperature, Trauma | Patients at high risk of infection due to functional asplenia and prophylactic hydroxyurea treatment | ASSESSMENT: Always consider presence of all types of sickle cell crisis regardless of dominant presentation | Check symptoms/signs of local and systemic infection, respiratory signs, increasing spleen size, shock, organ failure | Baseline and current Hb, CXR if fever/chest pain/hypoxia, CT Brain if stroke suspected | Consider FBC, reticulocytes, bilirubin, haemolytic screen and crossmatch, blood film for sickle cells | TYPES OF SICKLE CELL CRISIS: Fever (consider veno-occlusive disease, acute chest syndrome, osteomyelitis, infection) | Vaso-occlusive crisis - assume cause for any painful presentations | Acute chest syndrome - life-threatening lung infarction, assume if hypoxia + chest pain | Acute splenic sequestration - typically in infants, increased spleen size, LUQ pain, fall in Hb>2g/dL, thrombocytopaenia | Aplastic crisis - fall in Hb and reticulocytes <1%, triggered by Parvovirus B19 | Stroke - avoid thrombolytics, avoid hyperviscosity | Priapism - stuttering (2-4hr, recurrent) vs severe (>4hr, may cause permanent impotence) | HAEMOPHILIA: Haemophilia A = Factor VIII (80%), Haemophilia B = Factor IX (20%) | Acquired causes: malignancies, pregnancy, auto-immune (SLE, RA), idiopathic | Most patients know their intrinsic factor activity % and type of factor they take | If unsure assume 0%, try to use what patient is on | Minor bleeds (soft tissue, muscle, haemarthrosis, epistaxis): 50% factor replacement | Major bleeds (CNS, GI, neck/airway, major trauma): 100% factor replacement | Factor VIII required = weight x 0.5 x (% activity desired - % intrinsic activity) | Factor IX required = weight x 1 x (% activity desired - % intrinsic activity) | Additional options: DDAVP 0.3mcg/kg IV in 30ml NS over 15-30min, FFP, PCC, cryoprecipitate (consult Haematology) | Haemophilia with inhibitor: rFVIIa 90mcg/kg OR FEIBA 75-100u/kg for life/limb threatening scenarios | TTP: Deficiency of ADAMTS13 resulting in large vWF multimers, increased platelet aggregation, end organ dysfunction | TTP Clinical Pentad: Thrombocytopaenia, Haemolytic anaemia, Neurological manifestations, Renal impairment, Fever | TTP Risk factors: Infection, HIV, Pregnancy, Malignancy, Pancreatitis, Autoimmune, Chemotherapy, Drugs | TTP ED Management: Plasma exchange transfusion with FFP, Glucocorticoids, Platelet transfusion CONTRAINDICATED | HUS: In children mostly, history of bloody diarrhoea, Shiga-toxin producing bacteria, prominent renal impairment | HUS ED Management: Supportive, maintain fluids/electrolytes, control hypertension, blood transfusion if indicated, haemodialysis if indicated | HUS: Avoid antibiotics, narcotics and antimotility agents | ITP: Mild gradual course, mucocutaneous bleeding, petechiae/purpuric rash, chronic: fatigue, anaemia | ITP ED Management: Platelet transfusion for severe bleeding, Glucocorticoids Prednisone 1mg/kg/day, IVIG 1g/kg IV, Anti-D 50-75mcg/kg IV | ITP: Asymptomatic with platelets >30 - do not transfuse | DIC: Always secondary to underlying disorder - sepsis, trauma, malignancy, pancreatitis, PE, surgery, transfusion reactions, obstetric, hepatic failure | DIC Clinical features: May be chronic with little overt effects or acute catastrophe, haemorrhage, micro-thrombosis leading to multiorgan failure | DIC Labs: Low Hb, low platelets, fragmented RBCs, prolonged aPTT/PT/INR, low fibrinogen, high D-dimer, low coagulation factors | DIC ED Management: ABC approach, treat the cause, FFP 15-20ml/kg, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.5g/L, platelet transfusion if <50 or bleeding | TRANSFUSION REACTIONS: Stop transfusion immediately, majority managed supportively | ABO mismatch/haemolysis: fever, tachycardia, chest pain, shock, DIC | TRALI: ARDS-type non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, shock, more common with FFP | TACO: Fluid overload, may mimic TRALI, more common in chronic anaemia | Other reactions: Anaphylaxis, Rh mismatch, hypothermia, electrolyte abnormalities (low Ca/Mg/K), infection, minor reactions (urticaria, pyrexia, tachycardia)

drugs.item: Augmentin (empiric broad spectrum antibiotics for sickle cell fever)
drugs.notes: Empiric antibiotics for fever in sickle cell - full septic workup required

drugs.item: Packed Red Cells (PRCs) transfusion - Sickle Cell
drugs.adult_dose: Leuco-depleted PRCs if Hb < 5g/dL or Hb >20% drop from baseline
drugs.notes: Exchange transfusion if baseline high Hb, multi-lobar infiltrate, persistent hypoxia, neurological abnormalities, MOD

drugs.item: Packed Red Cells (PRCs) transfusion - Acute haemorrhage
drugs.adult_dose: PRC and FFP 1:1 ratio if >1 unit PRC required, Hb <10
drugs.notes: Normal Hb may be falsely reassuring in early bleeding

drugs.adult_dose: Hb <7; Hb <10 if high risk of myocardial ischaemia
drugs.notes: 1 unit raises Hb by 1.5, Hct by 3%
FDP = 1 unit FFP. Ward stock, room temp. Life-threatening bleeding until FFP available. Warfarin overdose if INR>1.5 and actively bleeding
Plt<10 consider even if not bleeding. Plt<50 if bleeding/invasive procedure. Massive transfusion: PRC:FFP:Plts = 4:4:1. Not usually given in ITP, TTP, HUS
Pharmacy stock. Warfarin overdose; FFP alternative; reversal of DOACs
DIC, haemophilia, von Willebrand disease, hypofibrinogenaemia - all in consultation with Haematology
Minor bleeds: 50% activity. Major bleeds: 100% activity
Minor bleeds: 50% activity. Major bleeds: 100% activity
Alternative if factor not available - consult Haematology
Haemophilia with inhibitor - life/limb threatening scenarios
Haemophilia with inhibitor - life/limb threatening scenarios
TTP specific treatment
TTP alternative to prednisone
Foundation of ITP treatment in ED
ITP immunosuppressive treatment
ITP alternative treatment
Vaso-occlusive crisis pain management
Vaso-occlusive crisis pain management - use with caution
Aggressive pain management for vaso-occlusive crisis - see pain management protocol

management_steps.details: Correct hypoxia, ensure adequate hydration (fluid bolus 20ml/kg if hypovolaemic)
management_steps.caution: High index of suspicion - patients may have normal vitals despite serious pathology

management_steps.action: Assess all types of sickle cell crisis
management_steps.details: Check infection signs, respiratory signs, spleen size, shock, organ failure, baseline and current Hb
management_steps.caution: Always consider all types regardless of dominant presentation

management_steps.details: Empiric broad spectrum antibiotics (Augmentin 1.2g IV), full septic workup
management_steps.caution: High risk of infection due to functional asplenia and immunosuppression

management_steps.details: Aggressive pain management: paracetamol, NSAIDs, opiates (see pain management protocol)

Supplemental O2 if Sats<92%, aggressive pain management to prevent hypoventilation, IV fluids with caution, empiric antibiotics if infection suspected, transfuse PRCs if Sats<92% or Hb<5g/dL or Hb decrease >20% from baseline
management_steps.caution: Life-threatening lung infarction

management_steps.details: Oxygen, hydration, PRC transfusion. Avoid over-transfusion due to rebound when sequestration resolves
management_steps.caution: Typically occurs in infants. Associated with increased spleen size and LUQ pain

management_steps.details: Supportive, oxygen, hydration, PRC transfusion, monitor temperatures (high risk of opportunistic infections), exchange transfusion
management_steps.caution: Fall in Hb and reticulocytes <1%, triggered by Parvovirus B19

management_steps.details: Supportive, avoid thrombolytics, avoid hyperviscosity, exchange transfusion - goal HbS <30%
management_steps.caution: Do NOT use thrombolytics

management_steps.details: Oxygen, IV fluid bolus if hypovolaemic, analgesia +/- penile block, corporal blood therapeutic aspiration, exchange transfusion
management_steps.caution: Urgent Urology referral. Stuttering (2-4hr) vs severe (>4hr)

management_steps.details: Plasma exchange transfusion with FFP, Glucocorticoids (Prednisone 1mg/kg/day or Methylprednisolone 1g/day)
management_steps.caution: Platelet transfusion CONTRAINDICATED unless life-threatening bleeding

management_steps.details: Supportive, maintain fluid status and electrolytes, control hypertension, blood transfusion if indicated, haemodialysis if indicated
management_steps.caution: Avoid antibiotics, narcotics and antimotility agents

management_steps.details: Severe bleeding: platelet transfusion. Immunosuppressive: Prednisone 1mg/kg/day, IVIG 1g/kg, Anti-D 50-75mcg/kg. Asymptomatic Plt>30: do not transfuse
management_steps.caution: Goal is safe platelet count

management_steps.details: Minor bleeds: 50% factor replacement. Major bleeds: 100% factor replacement. Use patient's usual factor if known
management_steps.caution: If unsure assume 0% intrinsic activity. All other resuscitation still applies

management_steps.details: ABC approach, treat the cause, FFP 15-20ml/kg, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.5g/L, platelets if <50 or bleeding
management_steps.caution: DIC is always secondary to underlying disorder

management_steps.details: STOP transfusion immediately, manage supportively, check for ABO mismatch, TRALI, TACO, anaphylaxis
management_steps.caution: Continuous monitoring during all transfusions required

diagnostic_criteria.TTP_pentad: Thrombocytopaenia, Haemolytic anaemia, Neurological manifestations, Renal impairment, Fever
diagnostic_criteria.TTP_labs: Increased reticulocytes, low Hb, low platelets, schistocytes, increased LDH, bilirubin, Direct Coombs negative, normal to mildly elevated D-dimer and fibrinogen
diagnostic_criteria.HUS_features: History of bloody diarrhoea, Shiga-toxin producing bacteria, prominent renal impairment/failure, hypertension
diagnostic_criteria.ITP_workup: FBC/retics/smear/Coombs/U&E/CRP/Blood grouping and Rhesus/Hep C/HIV/LFT/Clotting profile - diagnosis of exclusion
diagnostic_criteria.DIC_labs: Low Hb, low platelets, fragmented RBCs, prolonged aPTT/PT/INR, low fibrinogen, high D-dimer, low coagulation factors and inhibitors
diagnostic_criteria.DIC_score: Calculated score based on ISTH criteria (platelet count, fibrinogen, PT prolongation, D-dimer)
diagnostic_criteria.sickle_cell_assessment: FBC, reticulocytes, bilirubin, haemolytic screen, crossmatch, blood film, CXR if fever/chest pain/hypoxia, CT Brain if stroke

haemophilia_classification.Minor_bleeds: Soft tissue, muscle, haemarthrosis, epistaxis - 50% factor replacement
haemophilia_classification.Major_bleeds: CNS, GI, neck/airway, major trauma - 100% factor replacement

protocols.monitoring: Continuous monitoring during transfusion | Monitor for signs of transfusion reactions | Regular limb and pulse checks for restrained patients | Monitor fluid status, electrolytes, Hb, platelet count | Check platelet count at 1hr and 24hrs after platelet infusion
