warnings: Platelet transfusion CONTRAINDICATED in TTP unless life-threatening bleeding
Avoid thrombolytics in sickle cell stroke
Avoid over-transfusion in acute splenic sequestration due to rebound
Normal Hb may be falsely reassuring in early bleeding
Rh negative females of childbearing age MUST receive Rh negative products
Consent is required for all blood products
Blood warming only necessary if >2 units to be transfused (>50ml/kg/hr adults)
protocols.disposition: Urgent and early Haematology referral for all sickle cell crisis | Urgent Haematology referral for TTP, ITP | Renal/Haematology/Paediatrics referral for HUS | Internal Medicine/ICU referral for DIC | Urology referral for priapism

protocols.notes_updates: Hypertensive Emergency = Elevated blood pressure (>180/110) WITH life-threatening acute end organ damage | Hypertensive Urgency = old terminology, manage as asymptomatic HT, NO indication for IV anti-hypertensives | Life-threatening end organ damage includes: Hypertensive encephalopathy and retinopathy, Haemorrhagic/acute ischaemic CVA, ACS (STEMI/NSTE-ACS), Acute LV dysfunction/Congestive heart failure, Acute pulmonary oedema (SCAPE), Aortic dissection, Acute renal failure, Eclampsia/Pre-eclampsia | Management of Hypertensive Emergencies: ABC approach to resuscitation | A - definitive airway if depressed level of consciousness | B - Acute pulmonary oedema but intubation not indicated: start non-invasive ventilation (see NIV protocol) | C - Fluid bolus with Balsol/Ringers Lactate 10ml/kg prior to commencing IV anti-hypertensive agent | TREATMENT GOAL: Within 1st hour reduce MAP by 20-25% only; DBP not less than 110mmHg | Exception - Aortic Dissection: Goal systolic <100-120 within 20 minutes if tolerated | Exception - ICH: avoid systolic BP <130 | No role in the ED for stat oral antihypertensives | For uncontrolled hypertension without end-organ damage: Treat initial presenting complaint, screen for TOD, True HT: discharge on oral antihypertensives (HCTZ + ACE-inhibitor/Ca Channel blocker), Reactive HT: reassure no TTO, Follow up at local clinic | Exclude reversible causes of elevated BP: incorrect cuff/technique, pain, anxiety, full bladder, medications

drugs.notes: First line for aortic dissection, encephalopathy, CVA

drugs.adult_dose: Add 16mls (80mg) + 2nd vial (100mg) of Labetalol to 200ml 0.9% NS (0.76mg/ml). Start at 80ml/hr, increase by 10ml/hr every 5 min until target. Maximum 160ml/hr (2mg/min)
drugs.notes: Alternative: Esmolol (see infusions protocol)

drugs.adult_dose: 10mg in 200ml NS. Start at 12mls/hr (10mcg/min). Double dose every 2 min until target BP reached. Maximum 200mcg/min
drugs.notes: For acute pulmonary oedema, acute LV dysfunction, ACS. Alternative: Isoket (see infusions)
Alternative to Nitrocine
Alternative to Labetalol

management_steps.action: Assess for acute life-threatening end-organ damage
management_steps.details: Check for: hypertensive encephalopathy/retinopathy, haemorrhagic/ischaemic CVA, ACS, acute LV dysfunction/heart failure, acute pulmonary oedema, aortic dissection, acute renal failure, eclampsia/pre-eclampsia
Airway: definitive airway if depressed consciousness. Breathing: NIV if acute pulmonary oedema. Circulation: fluid bolus Balsol/Ringers 10ml/kg before IV anti-hypertensive
management_steps.caution: Do not lower BP too rapidly

management_steps.details: General: Reduce MAP by 20-25% within 1st hour, DBP not <110mmHg. Aortic dissection: SBP <100-120 within 20min if tolerated. ICH: avoid SBP <130
management_steps.caution: Excessive BP reduction can cause end-organ hypoperfusion

management_steps.action: Labetalol for aortic dissection, encephalopathy, CVA
management_steps.details: Bolus 20mg IV, then infusion 80ml/hr titrating up by 10ml/hr every 5min, max 160ml/hr (2mg/min)
management_steps.caution: Monitor for bradycardia, hypotension. Contraindicated in asthma, heart block

management_steps.action: Nitroglycerin for acute pulmonary oedema, LV dysfunction, ACS
management_steps.details: Infusion 10mcg/min (12ml/hr), double every 2min, max 200mcg/min
management_steps.caution: Monitor for hypotension, headache. Tachyphylaxis with prolonged use

management_steps.details: Incorrect cuff/technique, pain, anxiety, full bladder, medications
Admit for ongoing management and monitoring, Internal Medicine/ICU referral

management_steps.action: Management of hypertensive urgency (no end-organ damage)
management_steps.details: Manage as asymptomatic HT, screen for TOD, plan BP reduction over 2-3 days, consider admission if severely elevated/poor follow-up
management_steps.caution: NO indication for IV anti-hypertensive agents in urgency

diagnostic_criteria.hypertensive_emergency_definition: BP >180/110 WITH life-threatening acute end-organ damage
diagnostic_criteria.hypertensive_urgency_definition: Severely elevated BP WITHOUT acute end-organ damage - old terminology
diagnostic_criteria.end_organ_damage_criteria: Hypertensive encephalopathy/retinopathy, haemorrhagic/ischaemic CVA, ACS, acute LV dysfunction/CHF, acute pulmonary oedema, aortic dissection, acute renal failure, eclampsia/pre-eclampsia

protocols.monitoring: Continuous BP monitoring with arterial line if available | Monitor for signs of end-organ damage | Check electrolytes, renal function | Monitor for medication side effects (bradycardia, hypotension)
warnings: NO role for stat oral antihypertensives in the ED
NO indication for IV anti-hypertensives in hypertensive urgency
Do NOT lower DBP below 110mmHg in general hypertensive emergency
For ICH: avoid systolic BP <130
For aortic dissection: goal systolic <100-120 within 20 minutes if tolerated
Within 1st hour: reduce MAP by 20-25% only
Fluid bolus 10ml/kg required prior to commencing IV anti-hypertensive agent
protocols.disposition: Admit all hypertensive emergencies for ongoing IV anti-hypertensive management and monitoring | Internal Medicine/ICU referral | Hypertensive urgency: plan BP reduction over 2-3 days as inpatient/outpatient | Follow up at local clinic for repeat BP/Rx continuation

protocols.notes_updates: Definition of Acute Liver Failure (ALF): Rapid development of severe acute liver injury characterised by impaired synthetic function (INR >1.5) and complicated by development of encephalopathy, in a person without history of liver disease or who had well-compensated liver disease | Causes (DAVES mnemonic): Drugs (paracetamol, TB drugs, ARVs, NSAIDs, traditional medicine, illicit drugs), Alcohol, Viral (Hep A/B/C/E, EBV, CMV, HSV, HZV, ParvoB19), Extras (acute fatty liver of pregnancy, HELLP, ischaemic necrosis, vascular, metabolic, autoimmune, Wilson's disease, Budd-Chiari, idiopathic, heatstroke, Reye's, malignancy), Sepsis | WEST HAVEN GRADING OF HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY: Grade 0 (minimal/subclinical): No clinical manifestations, some abnormalities on psychometric testing | Grade I (mild): Euphoria/depression, mild confusion, slurred speech, disordered sleep, asterixis may/may not be present | Grade II (moderate): Lethargy, moderate confusion, asterixis usually present | Grade III (severe): Marked confusion, incoherent speech, drowsy but rousable, asterixis present | Grade IV (coma): Comatose | Alternate HE Grading - Intellectual and Neuromuscular Function: Grade I: Impaired attention/irritable/depressed/personality changes; Tremor/apraxia/incoordination | Grade II: Drowsy/poor memory/behavioural changes/sleep disturbances; Asterixis/ataxia/slow slurred speech | Grade III: Confusion/somnolence/disorientation/amnesia; Hypoactive reflexes/clonus/nystagmus/muscle rigidity | Grade IV: Stupor/coma; Dilated pupils/decerebrate posture/oculocephalic reflex absent | COMPLICATIONS OF ALF: Neurological (hepatic encephalopathy, cerebral oedema, seizures, raised ICP), Coagulopathy and bleeding, Metabolic (hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, hypophosphataemia, lactic acidosis, hyperammonaemia), Infection (septicaemia, peritonitis, pneumonia, UTI), Respiratory (hypoxia, aspiration, ARDS), Cardiovascular (hypotension, hypertension from raised ICP), Renal failure | JAUNDICE WORKUP: Assess urobilinogen and bilirubin fraction | Unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia: Indirect>direct bilirubin, total bili increased, direct normal/mildly increased | Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia: Direct>indirect bilirubin, total bili increased, direct markedly increased | Unconjugated causes: Haemolysis (TTP, infections e.g. Malaria), haematoma resorption, dyserythropoiesis/haemoglobinopathies | Conjugated - hepatocellular: Viral hepatitis, fulminant hepatic failure, alcoholic hepatitis, toxins/drugs (paracetamol OD), medications (TB treatment), ischaemia (shock, Budd-Chiari), autoimmune, HELLP in pregnancy | Conjugated - obstructive: Choledocholithiasis, cholangitis/cholecystitis, strictures, neoplasms, HIV-associated cholangiopathy, extrinsic compression (pancreatic/liver neoplasms, TB, AAA, liver abscess) | General Management Approach Goals: Identification of aetiology and initiation of specific treatment, Supportive and symptomatic management with timely transfer to critical care unit, Early consultation with Liver Transplant specialists | If decompensated liver failure in cirrhosis patient: search for and correct precipitants (GI bleed, infection, uraemia, electrolyte abnormalities, drugs/toxins, increased protein in diet, non-adherence to treatment) | Supportive care: Correct electrolyte abnormalities especially sodium, potassium and magnesium | Monitor and correct hypoglycaemia | Monitor fluid status: diuretics if overloaded | Correct coagulopathy: if bleeding consider Vit K 5-10mg IM/IV and FFPs | GI bleeding: transfuse blood products, IV PPI, consider somatostatin and urgent surgical referral

drugs.adult_dose: 150mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 1hr, then 50mg/kg in 500ml 5% Dextrose over 4hrs, then 100mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose over 16hrs
drugs.notes: Used in majority of drug/toxin ALF cases. DO NOT withhold even if 48-72hrs post-ingestion. Continue until hepatic function resolves (resolving HE, INR<1.5, declining transaminases). If hepatic function not resolved, continue 150mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose over 24hrs

drugs.adult_dose: 100mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 2hrs, then 200mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose over 10hrs
drugs.notes: Preferred regimen - inform admitting doctor to continue. Less anaphylaxis and vomiting than old regimen. IF IN DOUBT, GIVE NAC
Give with Penicillin G
For coagulopathy and bleeding in ALF
Only if actively bleeding, undergoing surgery or requires procedure
If platelet count <50 and actively bleeding or undergoing surgery. Check count at 1hr and 24hrs after infusion
Reduces ammonia thus reducing cerebral oedema
Short-acting benzodiazepine to terminate seizures. If seizures continue, load with phenytoin
Aim to maintain serum osmolarity <320 mOsm/L. Be vigilant for fluid overload if renal failure present
Maintain sodium levels of 145-155 to prevent raised intracranial pressure
Suspected Herpes or Varicella Zoster causing ALF
If features of acute adrenal insufficiency
If hypotension persists after correcting hypovolaemia with crystalloids
For mushroom poisoning - give with NAC

management_steps.details: ABCs, blood glucose, oxygen if hypoxic, establish IV access
management_steps.caution: Check glucose immediately - hypoglycaemia common

management_steps.details: Intubate and ventilate if unresponsive from hepatic encephalopathy (RSI to prevent aspiration)
management_steps.caution: Nurse head up 30 degrees post-intubation

management_steps.details: First correct hypovolaemia with crystalloids. Commence inotropes (Adrenaline) if needed
management_steps.caution: Patients often intravascularly depleted despite peripheral fluid overload

management_steps.details: Monitor glucose 2 hourly, correct hypoglycaemia immediately
management_steps.caution: Hypoglycaemia is common and dangerous in ALF

management_steps.action: Stop all hepatotoxic and nephrotoxic drugs
management_steps.details: Review medication chart and stop all potentially harmful drugs
Correct hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, hypophosphataemia and other electrolyte abnormalities. Consider stress-dose steroids if features of acute adrenal insufficiency
management_steps.caution: Especially monitor sodium, potassium, magnesium

management_steps.action: Cerebral oedema, seizures, raised ICP and hepatic encephalopathy management
management_steps.details: Exclude other causes of depressed LOC (hypoglycaemia, intracranial bleed). Lactulose 10mls TDS PO. Seizures: Midazolam 0.1mg/kg IV, load phenytoin if continues. Raised ICP: Mannitol 0.5-1g/kg, maintain serum osmol <320. HE: 30% hypertonic saline 5-20ml/hr, maintain Na+ 145-155
Vitamin K 10mg IV slow bolus. FFP 15ml/kg only if actively bleeding, undergoing surgery or requires procedure. Platelets 10ml/kg if count <50 and bleeding or surgery

Indicated if suspect paracetamol or drug-induced ALF, mushroom poisoning (with Penicillin G). Standard: 150mg/kg/1hr, then 50mg/kg/4hrs, then 100mg/kg/16hrs. SNAP: 100mg/kg/2hrs then 200mg/kg/10hrs. Continue until hepatic function improves
management_steps.caution: DO NOT withhold even if 48-72hrs post-ingestion. Recommend until evidence of improved hepatic function - resolving HE, improving coagulopathy INR<1.5, declining transaminases

management_steps.details: Identify and treat any suspected infection. NO role for prophylactic antibiotics. Suspected Herpes/Varicella Zoster: Acyclovir 5-10mg/kg q8hrly IV
management_steps.caution: Sepsis is a common precipitant and complication

management_steps.action: Jaundice workup if presentation is jaundice
management_steps.details: If paracetamol OD suspected - start NAC immediately. Viral hepatitis studies as indicated. Supportive care. If pregnant: urgent obstetrics referral

Bedside: Glucose, Urine dipstix and U-betaHCG. Laboratory: Blood gas, FBC, U&E, CMP, LFT, INR, plasma ammonia, paracetamol level, blood culture. Type and screen. Radiological: CXR, abdominal ultrasound
ALL patients with ALF referred to physician. If intubated - admit/transfer to ICU. Early consultation with Liver Transplant specialists
management_steps.caution: ALF is a medical emergency requiring urgent specialist involvement

diagnostic_criteria.ALF_definition: Rapid development of severe acute liver injury with INR>1.5 and encephalopathy, without history of liver disease or with well-compensated liver disease
diagnostic_criteria.ALF_causes_DAVES: Drugs (paracetamol, TB drugs, ARVs, NSAIDs, traditional medicine), Alcohol, Viral (Hep A/B/C/E, EBV, CMV, HSV, HZV, ParvoB19), Extras (fatty liver pregnancy, HELLP, ischaemia, vascular, metabolic, autoimmune, Wilson's, Budd-Chiari, heatstroke, Reye's, malignancy), Sepsis

hepatic_encephalopathy_west_haven.Grade_0: Minimal/subclinical - no clinical manifestations, some psychometric abnormalities
hepatic_encephalopathy_west_haven.Grade_I: Mild - euphoria/depression, mild confusion, slurred speech, disordered sleep, asterixis may/may not be present
hepatic_encephalopathy_west_haven.Grade_II: Moderate - lethargy, moderate confusion, asterixis usually present
hepatic_encephalopathy_west_haven.Grade_III: Severe - marked confusion, incoherent speech, drowsy but rousable, asterixis present

diagnostic_criteria.jaundice_workup_unconjugated: Increased urobilinogen, indirect>direct bilirubin. Causes: haemolysis (TTP, malaria), haematoma resorption, dyserythropoiesis
diagnostic_criteria.jaundice_workup_conjugated_hepatocellula: Direct>indirect bilirubin, increased transaminases, increased INR. Causes: viral hepatitis, FHF, alcoholic hepatitis, paracetamol OD, ischaemia, autoimmune, HELLP
diagnostic_criteria.jaundice_workup_conjugated_obstructive: Direct>indirect bilirubin, increased ALP/GGT. Causes: choledocholithiasis, cholangitis, strictures, neoplasms, HIV cholangiopathy, extrinsic compression

ALF_history_features: Target symptoms consistent with presenting complication

protocols.monitoring: Glucose monitoring 2 hourly | Electrolyte monitoring (especially Na+, K+, Mg2+, phosphate) | Coagulation profile (INR, PT, aPTT) | Platelet count at 1hr and 24hrs post-transfusion | Serum osmolarity if mannitol given (maintain <320 mOsm/L) | Ammonia levels | LFTs and transaminases trending | Neurological status and GCS monitoring | Blood pressure and fluid status monitoring
warnings: Avoid sedatives - give short-acting benzodiazepines only if needed
NAC: DO NOT withhold even if ingestion was 48-72hrs prior to presentation
If paracetamol OD suspected - start NAC immediately without waiting for levels
NO role for prophylactic antibiotics unless specific infection suspected
FFPs only if actively bleeding, undergoing surgery or requires procedure - not for INR correction alone
All patients with ALF require urgent physician referral and potential ICU admission
Early Liver Transplant specialist consultation is critical
protocols.disposition: ALL patients with ALF should be referred to a physician for further management | If intubated - admit/transfer to ICU | Early consultation with Liver Transplant specialists | Pregnant patients with HELLP: urgent obstetrics referral to plan for delivery | GI bleeding: urgent surgical referral

protocols.notes_updates: MCHA FORMS: Form 22 if brought by police (condition of patient on handover from SAPS), Form 1 to commence emergency treatment, Form 48 to commence physical restraints, Forms 4/5/7 to admit as assisted or involuntary user | GENERAL MANAGEMENT: Rapid assessment and sedation/restraint if violent or severely agitated - see AGITATION PROTOCOL | Check vital signs including HGT, monitor O2 saturation if heavily sedated | Comprehensive history and exam to exclude underlying medical conditions | Investigations as per protocol | Hand over to Psychiatry on call if bloods/vitals/exam is normal | Psychiatry to arrange rapid transfer to ward as soon as bed is available | Patients will NOT wait in ED for additional investigations/management unless medically indicated | Ensure patient comfort: toilet needs, regular meals, smoke breaks etc | ACUTE DETERIORATION causes: Underlying medical condition (sepsis/endocrine/rheumatological), Trauma, Non-compliance, Sub/supra-therapeutic medication levels, Recreational drug/alcohol use, Intracranial pathology, Poor social support, Pregnancy, Acute psychological stressor | RED FLAGS: Age >60, Trauma, Meningitis features, Recent travel history, Auto-immune/endocrine features, Potentially toxic drug levels (Epilim, Lithium), Advanced RVD features, Post-ictal features, Acute drug intoxication/withdrawal (Medical admission), Pregnancy/Recent postpartum, New onset visual/tactile hallucinations, Focal Neurology | ALL FEMALE PATIENTS MUST HAVE A PREGNANCY TEST DOCUMENTED IN THE FILE | EXCLUSIONS requiring medical admission: Advanced RVD, Thyroid dysfunction, SLE, new neurology | COMPLICATIONS ASSOCIATED WITH ANTIPSYCHOTIC DRUGS: EXTRAPYRAMIDAL SIDE EFFECTS/ACUTE DYSTONIC REACTION: fixed abnormal posture, oculogyric crisis, laryngospasm | NEUROLEPTIC MALIGNANT SYNDROME: develops over 24-72hrs, hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, rhabdomyolysis | NMS management: Resuscitate, withdraw precipitating agent, supportive care

drugs.notes: Repeat every 2 minutes prn, maximum 4 doses. For fixed abnormal posture, oculogyric crisis, laryngospasm from antipsychotics
For mild agitation in MHCU
Time of onset: 10 minutes. For moderate agitation
Time of onset: up to 30 minutes. Max 3 doses every 30-60 minutes. Alternative oral: 5mg PO
Combined with Midazolam 10mg IM for severe/extreme agitation
Time of onset: <5 minutes. For severe/extreme agitation when other measures fail
Combined with Haloperidol 10mg IM
Alternative for moderate agitation

management_steps.details: Assess for violence and severe agitation. Safety is first priority - contain patient to protect staff and self
management_steps.caution: See AGITATION PROTOCOL for sedation/restraint if violent

management_steps.details: Check HGT, O2 saturation if heavily sedated, full vital signs
management_steps.caution: Must exclude underlying medical causes

management_steps.details: Exclude underlying medical conditions. Check for acute deterioration causes: sepsis, endocrine, trauma, non-compliance, drug levels, recreational drugs, intracranial pathology
management_steps.caution: Agitation is a symptom, not a diagnosis

management_steps.details: Index MHCU baseline: VBG, FBC, U&E, CRP, TPHA/RPR (CRP<60, WCC<20). CSF ONLY if acute meningitis features OR advanced RVD. CTB if clinically indicated - low threshold for immune compromised. With contrast preferred
VBG, HGT only. Further investigations only as clinically indicated. Consider drug levels - anti-epileptics, mood stabilisers
ALL female patients must have pregnancy test documented
management_steps.caution: Pregnancy/recent postpartum is a red flag

management_steps.details: Age>60, trauma, meningitis features, travel hx, auto-immune/endocrine, toxic drug levels, advanced RVD, post-ictal, intoxication/withdrawal, pregnancy/postpartum, new visual/tactile hallucinations, focal neurology
management_steps.caution: If red flags present - may need medical admission rather than psychiatry referral

management_steps.details: Form 22 if brought by police, Form 1 for emergency treatment, Form 48 for physical restraints, Forms 4/5/7 for assisted/involuntary admission

Must be prescribed by doctor on Form 48. At least 6 staff: 1 per limb, 1 for head, 1 to administer medication. Never pressure on neck or cover mouth/nose. Supine position HOB 30 degrees. 4 or 5 point medical grade restraints tied to bedframe not rails. Regular limb and pulse checks every 30 min. Max 4 hours adults. Female pt: at least 1 female team member
management_steps.caution: Use in conjunction with chemical restraint to avoid injury/rhabdomyolysis

management_steps.details: Mild: Lorazepam 1-2mg SL. Moderate: Midazolam 2-5mg IM OR Haloperidol 2.5-10mg IM (max 3 doses q30-60min) OR Etomine 40mg IM. Severe/Extreme: Ketamine 5mg/kg IM OR Haloperidol 10mg IM + Midazolam 10mg IM
management_steps.caution: Sedated patients must be placed in recovery position in monitored bed. Any sedation can cause respiratory depression and hypotension. Resuscitation facilities must be available

management_steps.details: EPS/Acute dystonic reaction: Biperiden 2.5mg IV slowly, repeat q2min prn max 4 doses. NMS: Resuscitate, withdraw precipitating agent, supportive care
management_steps.caution: NMS develops over 24-72hrs with hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, rhabdomyolysis

management_steps.details: If bloods/vitals/exam normal, hand over to Psychiatry on call. Psychiatry arranges rapid transfer to ward. Patient will NOT wait in ED for additional investigations unless medically indicated

management_steps.action: Ensure patient comfort while in ED
management_steps.details: Toilet needs, regular meals, smoke breaks etc.
Normal investigations + no red flags: Psychiatry referral/transfer. Red flags or abnormal investigations: Medical admission for further workup
management_steps.caution: If all normal and no red flags, refer to Psychiatry

diagnostic_criteria.index_MHCU_baseline_investigations: VBG, FBC, U&E, CRP, TPHA/RPR (CRP<60, WCC<20). CSF ONLY if acute meningitis features OR advanced RVD - Chemistry, cell count, MCS, India Ink, CLAT, TB GXP. Add VDRL if TPHA/RPR positive. CTB if clinically indicated - low threshold for immune compromised, with contrast preferred
diagnostic_criteria.known_MHCU_baseline_investigations: VBG, HGT only. Further investigations only as clinically indicated. Consider drug levels - anti-epileptics, mood stabilisers

antipsychotic_complications.extrapyramidal_side_effects_acute_dyston: Fixed abnormal posture, oculogyric crisis, laryngospasm. Treatment: Biperiden 2.5mg IV slowly repeat q2min prn max 4 doses
antipsychotic_complications.neuroleptic_malignant_syndrome: Develops over 24-72hrs, hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, rhabdomyolysis. Treatment: Resuscitate, withdraw precipitating agent, supportive care

agitation_sedation_levels.moderate: Midazolam 2-5mg IM OR Haloperidol 2.5-10mg IM (max 3 doses q30-60min) OR Etomine 40mg IM
agitation_sedation_levels.severe_extreme: Ketamine 5mg/kg IM (<5min onset) OR Haloperidol 10mg IM + Midazolam 10mg IM

clinical_features.physical_restraints: Prescribed on Form 48. At least 6 staff (1 per limb, 1 head, 1 medication). Supine HOB 30 degrees. 4-5 point medical grade restraints to bedframe. Regular limb/pulse checks q30min. Max 4 hours adults. Female pt: at least 1 female team member. Never pressure on neck or cover mouth/nose

protocols.monitoring: Monitor O2 saturation if heavily sedated | Regular limb and pulse checks every 30 minutes if restrained | Monitor in recovery position until fully awake and alert | Resuscitation facilities must be available for all sedated patients | Check HGT and vital signs on all MHCU presentations
warnings: ALL FEMALE PATIENTS MUST HAVE A PREGNANCY TEST DOCUMENTED
Sedated patients must be placed in recovery position in a monitored bed until fully awake and alert
Any sedation can cause respiratory depression and hypotension - resuscitation facilities must be available
Patients will NOT wait in ED for additional investigations unless medically indicated
Physical restraints must be prescribed by a doctor on Form 48
Red flags require medical admission, not just psychiatry referral
Exclusions (Advanced RVD, thyroid dysfunction, SLE, new neurology) require medical admission
protocols.disposition: If all investigations normal and no red flags: hand over to Psychiatry on call, arrange rapid transfer to ward | If red flags present or abnormal investigations: medical admission for further workup | Pregnancy/recent postpartum: consider obstetrics involvement | Acute intoxication/withdrawal: medical admission

protocols.item: Neisseria Meningitidis infection control and chemoprophylaxis
protocols.notes_updates: Transmission: Prolonged (>8 hours) direct close contact (<1 metre) with large-droplet respiratory secretions | Infection Control Department must be informed immediately | Notification based on clinical suspicion OR laboratory confirmation | Notifiable disease | Primary prevention: selective antimicrobial chemoprophylaxis limited to special circumstances only | Aim of chemoprophylaxis: prevent secondary cases by eliminating nasopharyngeal carriage | Chemoprophylaxis should be administered ASAP, ideally within 24 hours after identification of index patient | Chemoprophylaxis >14 days after onset of illness is ineffective | Close contacts defined as: (1) anyone directly exposed to patient's oral secretions (kissing, sharing food/beverages/utensils, mouth-to-mouth resuscitation, endotracheal intubation/management), (2) household members (same dwelling, sharing eating utensils), (3) institutional contacts sharing sleeping quarters or kitchen (boarding schools, military camps, barracks), (4) nursery school or day care centre close contacts only (pupils and teachers sharing classroom) | Mass chemoprophylaxis for non-close contacts is unwarranted and not recommended | Procedure before chemoprophylaxis: (1) Notify Infection Control of exposure type (respiratory droplet, mucosal splash, percutaneous), (2) Infection Control advises on necessity, (3) Staff report to staff clinic for WCA notification, (4) No staff to take antimicrobial drugs from ward stock | Standard and respiratory droplet isolation precautions must be implemented while in ED | Patient may be de-isolated after 24 hours therapy with agents that reliably eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage (Ceftriaxone/Cefotaxime) | Patients treated with Penicillin G alone cannot be de-isolated until they receive chemoprophylaxis with appropriate antibiotic

drugs.notes: Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts of meningococcal disease
Alternative chemoprophylaxis option for non-pregnant adults and children
Preferred for pregnant adults; also reliably eradicates nasopharyngeal carriage allowing de-isolation after 24 hours

management_steps.action: Identify close contacts of index patient
management_steps.details: Assess for: direct exposure to oral secretions, household members, institutional contacts, nursery/day care contacts
management_steps.caution: Mass chemoprophylaxis for non-close contacts is unwarranted

management_steps.details: Report type of exposure: respiratory droplet, mucosal splash, or percutaneous
management_steps.caution: Notification can be based on clinical suspicion or laboratory confirmation

management_steps.action: Infection Control advises on necessity of chemoprophylaxis
management_steps.details: This limits unnecessary and indiscriminate antibiotic use
management_steps.caution: No staff to take antimicrobial drugs from ward stock

management_steps.action: Implement standard and respiratory droplet isolation precautions
management_steps.details: Isolation in separate cubicle, surgical masks when entering, eye protection, gloves for all contact, hand washing before and after patient contact
management_steps.caution: Patient may be de-isolated after 24 hours with Ceftriaxone/Cefotaxime

management_steps.action: Administer chemoprophylaxis to eligible close contacts
management_steps.details: Choose one of: Ciprofloxacin, Rifampicin, or Ceftriaxone based on patient category (non-pregnant adult, pregnant adult, or child)
management_steps.caution: Administer ASAP ideally within 24 hours; >14 days after onset is ineffective

management_steps.details: Staff advised to take chemoprophylaxis must report to staff clinic for WCA notification

close_contact_definition: Anyone directly exposed to patient's oral secretions (kissing, sharing food/beverages/utensils, mouth-to-mouth resuscitation, ET intubation/management)
Household members (same dwelling, sharing eating utensils)
Institutional contacts sharing sleeping quarters or kitchen
Nursery school or day care centre close contacts only

clinical_features.transmission_route: Prolonged (>8 hours) direct close contact (<1 metre) with large-droplet respiratory secretions
clinical_features.incubation: Secondary disease highest in first few days after onset in index patient

protocols.monitoring: Monitor patient for response to therapy; de-isolation assessment at 24 hours
warnings: Mass chemoprophylaxis for non-close contacts is unwarranted and not recommended
Chemoprophylaxis >14 days after onset of illness in index patient is ineffective
Patients treated with Penicillin G alone cannot be de-isolated until they receive chemoprophylaxis
No staff to take antimicrobial drugs from ward stock
Neisseria meningitidis is a notifiable disease
protocols.disposition: Refer to Infection Control Department for guidance; notify as per notifiable disease requirements

protocols.notes_updates: Decision tree for acute vs chronic hypertension management in the ED. | First assess for acute life-threatening end-organ damage (TOD): hypertensive encephalopathy and retinopathy, haemorrhagic/ischaemic CVA, ACS (STEMI/NSTE-ACS), acute LV dysfunction/congestive heart failure, acute pulmonary oedema (SCAPE), aortic dissection, acute renal failure, eclampsia/pre-eclampsia. | If YES TOD: This is a Hypertensive Emergency - requires immediate IV antihypertensive therapy in the ED (see Hypertensive Emergencies protocol). | If NO TOD: This is uncontrolled or poorly controlled hypertension (not an emergency). | For non-emergent hypertension: No role in ED for stat oral antihypertensives. Treat the initial presenting complaint. Screen for TOD. True HT (with TOD): Discharge on oral antihypertensive agents - HCTZ + ACE-inhibitor/Ca Channel blocker. Reactive HT (no TOD): reassure, no TTO. Follow up at local Clinic for repeat BP/Rx continuation. | Always exclude reversible causes of elevated BP: incorrect cuff/technique, pain, anxiety, full bladder, medications.

drugs.adult_dose: As per outpatient regimen - initiate at local clinic
drugs.notes: First-line oral antihypertensive for discharge of true HT patients. To be initiated at local clinic follow-up.

drugs.adult_dose: As per outpatient regimen - initiate at local clinic
drugs.notes: Oral antihypertensive for discharge. Examples: Enalapril, Lisinopril.

drugs.adult_dose: As per outpatient regimen - initiate at local clinic
drugs.notes: Oral antihypertensive for discharge. Examples: Amlodipine.

management_steps.details: Confirm elevated BP. Exclude reversible causes: incorrect cuff/technique, pain, anxiety, full bladder, medications.
management_steps.caution: Always verify BP with correct cuff size and technique

management_steps.action: Screen for acute life-threatening end-organ damage (TOD)
management_steps.details: Check for: Hypertensive encephalopathy and retinopathy, Haemorrhagic/Ischaemic CVA, ACS (STEMI/NSTE-ACS), Acute LV dysfunction/Congestive heart failure, Acute pulmonary oedema (SCAPE), Aortic dissection, Acute renal failure, Eclampsia/Pre-eclampsia
management_steps.caution: These conditions require immediate intervention

management_steps.details: If YES acute TOD present: This is a HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY. Proceed to immediate IV antihypertensive therapy. See Hypertensive Emergencies protocol for specific management.
management_steps.caution: Do NOT lower BP too rapidly - risk of hypoperfusion

management_steps.details: No role for stat oral antihypertensives in ED. Treat the initial presenting complaint. Determine if true HT or reactive HT.
management_steps.caution: Do not treat asymptomatic elevated BP in ED

management_steps.details: Discharge on oral antihypertensive agents: HCTZ + ACE-inhibitor OR Calcium Channel blocker. Arrange local clinic follow-up for repeat BP and prescription continuation.
management_steps.caution: Ensure patient has follow-up arranged

management_steps.details: Reassure patient. No take-out (TTO) medication. Follow up at local clinic for repeat BP.
management_steps.caution: Reactive HT often normalizes when trigger removed

diagnostic_criteria.hypertensive_emergency: Severely elevated blood pressure WITH acute end-organ damage (TOD): Hypertensive encephalopathy and retinopathy | Haemorrhagic/Ischaemic CVA | ACS (STEMI/NSTE-ACS) | Acute LV dysfunction/Congestive heart failure | Acute pulmonary oedema (SCAPE) | Aortic dissection | Acute renal failure | Eclampsia/Pre-eclampsia
diagnostic_criteria.hypertensive_urgency: Severely elevated blood pressure WITHOUT acute end-organ damage. NOT a hypertensive emergency.
diagnostic_criteria.reversible_causes: Incorrect cuff size/technique | Pain | Anxiety | Full bladder | Medications (e.g., stimulants, decongestants, NSAIDs)

clinical_features.end_organ_damage_signs: Altered mental status (encephalopathy), Focal neurological deficits (CVA), Chest pain (ACS/ADHF), Dyspnoea (pulmonary oedema), Flank pain/decreased urine output (ARF), Seizures/eclampsia (pregnancy)
clinical_features.asymptomatic_elevated_bp: No symptoms - incidental finding of elevated BP

protocols.monitoring: Blood pressure monitoring (automated cuff or arterial line for emergencies) | Continuous cardiac monitoring in hypertensive emergency | Neurological status | Urine output | Renal function (U&E, creatinine)
warnings: No role in ED for stat oral antihypertensives for asymptomatic elevated BP
Do NOT lower BP too rapidly in hypertensive emergency - risk of cerebral/hypoperfusion injury
Always exclude reversible causes before diagnosing true hypertension
Pregnant patients with elevated BP require obstetric assessment for pre-eclampsia/eclampsia
Hypertensive emergency requires immediate IV therapy and ICU admission
True HT with TOD: Discharge on oral agents with clinic follow-up
Reactive HT (no TOD): Reassure, no TTO, clinic follow-up
protocols.disposition: Hypertensive Emergency: ICU/High care admission for IV antihypertensive therapy and monitoring. | True HT (no emergency): Discharge on HCTZ + ACE-I or CCB with local clinic follow-up within 1 week. | Reactive HT: Reassure, no medication, local clinic follow-up for repeat BP.

protocols.notes_updates: Diagnostic approach to jaundice in the ED. | ABCs first: If unstable, resuscitate (ABC, IV, monitors, check glucose, pregnancy test). | History: Ask about pain, fever, prior surgeries, time of onset, medications, herbal medications, alcohol/drug use, HIV history, travel, work, and family history. | Exam must include vital signs (fever/hypotension - consider cholangitis), mental status, neurologic exam, asterixis, lungs (crackles, pleural effusions), CVS (heart failure), abdomen (ascites, tenderness, masses, hepatosplenomegaly), skin (spider naevi, ecchymoses, dilated abdominal wall vessels). | Initial investigations: FBC/U&E/complete LFT/LDH/CMP/CRP/lipase/INR/VBG/glucose/paracetamol level if suspected overdose. Viral hepatitis serology if indicated (Hep A IgM/Hep B SAg/Hep B cAb/Hep C Ab). Malaria testing if travel history. CXR/POCUS. Abdominal USS: assess biliary system, hepatic architecture and vessels, masses. | West Haven Grading for Hepatic Encephalopathy: Grade 0 (subclinical), Grade I (mild - euphoria/depression, mild confusion), Grade II (moderate - lethargy, moderate confusion, asterixis), Grade III (severe - marked confusion, incoherent speech, drowsy but rousable), Grade IV (coma). | Causes of ALF (ABCDEF): A-Acetaminophen/Hep A/autoimmune, B-Hep B/Budd-Chiari, C-Hep C/CMV/cryptogenic, D-Hep D/drugs-toxins (mushroom/herbal), E-Extras (Wilson's). | If decompensated liver failure in cirrhosis patient: search for precipitants - GI bleed, infection, uraemia, electrolyte abnormalities, drugs/toxins, increased protein in diet, non-adherence to treatment.

drugs.adult_dose: Per Paracetamol Overdose protocol - start immediately if paracetamol OD suspected
drugs.notes: Start immediately if paracetamol overdose suspected. Do not wait for levels. Hepatoprotective even in non-paracetamol ALF (limited evidence).
For coagulopathy correction in liver failure. Only effective if there is residual synthetic liver function.
For active bleeding with coagulopathy. Not for routine correction of abnormal INR without bleeding.
For GI bleed prophylaxis/treatment in liver failure patients.
For variceal bleeding in decompensated cirrhosis. Give with urgent surgical referral.
For raised ICP in acute liver failure with cerebral oedema. Monitor renal function and osmolality.
For hepatic encephalopathy. Titrate to 2-3 soft bowel motions per day.
Adjunct to lactulose for hepatic encephalopathy. Reduces ammonia-producing gut bacteria.

management_steps.details: ABCs, IV access, monitors, check glucose, pregnancy test in appropriate female patients.
management_steps.caution: Hypotension may indicate sepsis (cholangitis) or adrenal insufficiency

management_steps.details: Ask about pain, fever, prior surgeries, time of onset, medications (including herbal/traditional), alcohol/drug use, HIV history, travel, family history. Exam: vital signs, mental status, neurologic exam, asterixis, lungs, CVS, abdomen (ascites, tenderness, hepatosplenomegaly), skin (spider naevi, ecchymoses, dilated abdominal wall vessels).
management_steps.caution: Fever with hypotension and jaundice = cholangitis until proven otherwise

management_steps.details: FBC, U&E, complete LFT (AST/ALT/ALP/GGT/bilirubin), LDH, CMP, CRP, lipase, INR, VBG, glucose. Paracetamol level if overdose suspected. Viral hepatitis serology (Hep A IgM, Hep B SAg, Hep B cAb, Hep C Ab). Malaria rapid and smear if travel to endemic region. CXR, POCUS.
management_steps.caution: Always check paracetamol level - overdoses may be unintentional or concealed

management_steps.details: Assess urobilinogen and bilirubin fraction. Unconjugated (indirect): Markedly elevated urobilinogen, indirect > direct bilirubin, total bili elevated with direct normal/mildly elevated. Conjugated (direct): Absent/mildly elevated urobilinogen, direct > indirect bilirubin, both total and direct elevated.
management_steps.caution: Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia suggests hepatocellular or obstructive pathology

management_steps.details: Pattern 1 - Haematological: Normal transaminases, normal ALP/GGT, normal INR. Pattern 2 - Hepatocellular: Markedly elevated transaminases, normal/elevated ALP/GGT, elevated INR. Pattern 3 - Obstructive: Normal/elevated transaminases, markedly elevated ALP/GGT, normal/elevated INR, elevated lipase.
management_steps.caution: Elevated INR with elevated transaminases = hepatocellular damage/liver failure

management_steps.action: Determine cause and direct management - Haematological
management_steps.details: Possible causes: Haemolysis (TTP, infections e.g. malaria), Haematoma resorption (history of trauma/bleeding diathesis), Dyserythropoiesis/haemoglobinopathies. Management: Cross-match and transfuse PRCs if warranted (Hb<7, HD unstable, CCF). Urgent haematology consult.
management_steps.caution: Check peripheral blood film, LDH, haptoglobin, reticulocyte count

management_steps.action: Determine cause and direct management - Hepatocellular
management_steps.details: Possible causes: Viral hepatitis, Fulminant hepatic failure, Alcoholic hepatitis, Toxins/drugs (paracetamol overdose), Medications (TB treatment), Ischaemia (shock, Budd-Chiari), Autoimmune, HELLP syndrome in pregnancy. Management: If paracetamol OD suspected - start NAC immediately. Exclude other toxins. Viral hepatitis studies. Supportive care for acute liver failure. Refer GIT/ID. Pregnant patients: urgent obstetrics referral.
management_steps.caution: HELLP syndrome is an obstetric emergency - urgent delivery planning required

management_steps.action: Determine cause and direct management - Obstructive
management_steps.details: Intrinsic: Choledocholithiasis, Cholangitis/cholecystitis, Strictures, Neoplasms, HIV-associated cholangiopathy. Extrinsic: Neoplasms (pancreatic/liver), Infiltrative (TB), Obstructing AAA, Liver abscess. Management: Urgent abdominal USS. Early antibiotics (e.g. Augmentin/ Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole) and urgent surgical consult for suspected cholangitis.
management_steps.caution: Cholangitis with shock = Reynolds pentad - requires emergent biliary drainage

management_steps.action: Supportive care for acute liver failure
management_steps.details: Correct electrolyte abnormalities (esp. sodium, potassium, magnesium). Monitor and correct hypoglycaemia. Monitor fluid status - diuretics if overloaded. Correct coagulopathy (Vit K 5-10mg IM/IV, FFP if bleeding). GI bleed management (transfuse, IV PPI, somatostatin, urgent surgical referral). Monitor GCS - if prolonged confusion or focal signs, urgent CTB to exclude ICH. Raised ICP may require mannitol 0.5-1g/kg IV. Discontinue hepatotoxic drugs.
management_steps.caution: Avoid sedatives if possible - can worsen encephalopathy and mask neurological deterioration

management_steps.details: Haematological: Haematology admission. Hepatocellular: GIT/ID referral, ICU if fulminant failure. Obstructive: Surgical referral for biliary drainage. All acute liver failure patients require ICU-level care.
management_steps.caution: Fulminant hepatic failure has high mortality - early specialist involvement essential

diagnostic_criteria.acute_liver_failure_definition: Rapid development of severe acute liver injury characterised by impaired synthetic function (INR > 1.5) and complicated by the development of encephalopathy, in a person without a history of liver disease or who had well-compensated liver disease.
diagnostic_criteria.unconjugated_hyperbilirubinaemia: Markedly elevated urobilinogen | Indirect > direct bilirubin | Total bilirubin elevated, direct normal/mildly elevated | Normal transaminases | Normal ALP/GGT | Normal INR
diagnostic_criteria.conjugated_hyperbilirubinaemia: Absent/mildly elevated urobilinogen | Direct > indirect bilirubin | Both total and direct bilirubin elevated
diagnostic_criteria.hepatocellular_pattern: Markedly elevated transaminases (AST/ALT) | Normal or mildly elevated ALP/GGT | Elevated INR | Normal lipase
diagnostic_criteria.obstructive_pattern: Normal or mildly elevated transaminases | Markedly elevated ALP/GGT | Normal or mildly elevated INR | Elevated lipase
diagnostic_criteria.haematological_pattern: Normal transaminases | Normal ALP/GGT | Normal INR | Evidence of haemolysis on FBC/film
diagnostic_criteria.west_haven_he_grade_0: Subclinical - no clinical manifestations, some abnormalities on psychometric testing
diagnostic_criteria.west_haven_he_grade_1: Mild - euphoria/depression, mild confusion, slurred speech, disordered sleep, asterixis may/may not be present
diagnostic_criteria.west_haven_he_grade_2: Moderate - lethargy, moderate confusion, asterixis usually present
diagnostic_criteria.west_haven_he_grade_3: Severe - marked confusion, incoherent speech, drowsy but rousable, asterixis present

clinical_features.history_findings: Jaundice onset, Pain (RUQ/epigastric), Fever, Alcohol use, Drug/medication use (prescription/illicit/herbal), Travel history, HIV status, Family history, Pregnancy, Prior surgeries
clinical_features.examination_findings: Jaundice, RUQ tenderness, Hepatomegaly, Splenomegaly, Ascites, Evidence of bleeding (bruising, GI bleed), Evidence of infection, Asterixis (hepatic flap), Evidence of encephalopathy (confusion, altered conscious level), Spider naevi, Ecchymoses, Dilated abdominal wall vessels (caput medusae), Pallor (anaemia)
clinical_features.acute_liver_failure_signs: Jaundice, Coagulopathy (elevated INR), Encephalopathy (any grade), Hypoglycaemia, Ascites, Oedema
clinical_features.red_flags: Altered mental status (any grade HE), Hypotension, Fever with jaundice (cholangitis), GI bleeding, Pregnancy (HELLP), Paracetamol overdose history, Rapidly rising INR

protocols.monitoring: Continuous cardiac and pulse oximetry monitoring | Blood glucose monitoring (hypoglycaemia common in ALF) | INR/PT monitoring | LFT trends (AST, ALT, ALP, GGT, bilirubin) | Electrolytes (Na+, K+, Mg2+) | Fluid balance/urine output | GCS monitoring (at least hourly if encephalopathy present) | Ammonia level if available | Lactate
warnings: Always suspect paracetamol overdose - check levels and start NAC immediately if any suspicion
Fever + jaundice + hypotension = cholangitis (Reynolds pentad if also altered mental status and confusion) - requires emergent biliary drainage
HELLP syndrome in pregnancy requires urgent obstetric referral and delivery planning
Avoid sedatives in liver failure - can worsen encephalopathy and mask neurological deterioration
Hypoglycaemia is common in acute liver failure - monitor and treat promptly
Coagulopathy correction (FFP) only if actively bleeding - abnormal INR alone is not an indication
Raised ICP in ALF requires mannitol and ICU-level care
Discontinue ALL hepatotoxic drugs immediately
Acute liver failure patients require ICU admission - high mortality without specialist care
Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic vein thrombosis) presents with painful hepatomegaly and ascites - urgent imaging required
Always consider autoimmune hepatitis - may respond to immunosuppression
Wilson's disease can present as acute liver failure in young patients - check serum ceruloplasmin and copper
protocols.disposition: Haematological jaundice: Haematology admission for further workup and management. | Hepatocellular jaundice (acute liver failure): ICU admission mandatory. Urgent GIT/Hepatology referral. Consider transfer to liver transplant centre if criteria met (Grade III-IV encephalopathy, INR > 3.5, creatinine > 1.5, jaundice to encephalopathy interval > 7 days, aetiology: paracetamol overdose, seronegative hepatitis, drug-induced, Wilson's disease). | Obstructive jaundice: Surgical/GIT referral. Antibiotics and urgent biliary drainage if cholangitis. | All patients with INR > 1.5 and encephalopathy require ICU care.

protocols.notes_updates: INDICATION: ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI) with symptom onset < 12 hours | TIME IS MUSCLE - do not delay reperfusion | DOOR TO NEEDLE TIME: 30 MINUTES | STEMI CRITERIA - ST elevation in 2 or more contiguous leads: J-point elevation in men ≥40 years: 2mm in V2-V3, 1mm in other leads | J-point elevation in men <40 years: 2.5mm in V2-V3, 1mm in other leads | J-point elevation in women: 1.5mm in V2-V3, 1mm in other leads | Contiguous leads: Inferior (II, III, aVF), Lateral (I, aVL, V5, V6), Anterior (V3, V4), Septal (V1, V2) | STEMI EQUIVALENTS: Patients who do not present with classical ECG changes but have acutely occluded coronary artery/s | STEMI equivalents often associated with poorer outcome and worse prognosis, benefit from timely intervention | True posterior MI: tall R in V1 and STE ≥ 0.5mm in V7-V9 | RV infarction: ST elevation V4R | CONSIDER AORTIC DISSECTION: Central chest pain radiating to back - tearing sensation, differential radial pulse, differential arm BP > 15mmHg, widened mediastinum on CXR (>8cm), POCUS aortic root >4.5cm, dissection flap, pericardial effusion, aortic regurg | SGARBOSSA CRITERIA for LBBB or paced rhythm: Criteria A (5 points) ≥1mm concordant ST elevation in at least one lead | Criteria B (3 points) ≥1mm concordant ST depression in lead V1, V2 or V3 | Criteria C (2 points) ≥5mm discordant ST elevation in at least one lead | Total score ≥3 has specificity of 90% for diagnosing myocardial infarction | ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATIONS TO THROMBOLYSIS: Any prior intracranial haemorrhage | Known structural cerebral vascular lesion e.g. AV malformation | Known malignant intracranial neoplasm | Ischaemic stroke within 3 months except acute ischaemic stroke within 3 hours | Suspected aortic dissection | Active bleeding or bleeding diathesis | Significant closed head trauma or facial trauma within 3 months | RELATIVE CONTRAINDICATIONS TO THROMBOLYSIS: History of chronic, severe, poorly controlled hypertension | SBP > 180mmHg or DBP > 110mmHg - lower BP with nitroglycerin (Nitrocine) infusion | Traumatic or prolonged CPR (>10 min) - NB consider whether MI may have caused the arrest | Major surgery (<3 weeks) | Recent (within 2-4 weeks) internal bleeding | Non-compressible vascular punctures | Pregnancy | Active peptic ulcer disease (bled within the past 2-4 weeks) | Current use of anticoagulants e.g. Warfarin | PRIOR TO THROMBOLYSIS: Written and signed consent required | Provided there are NO contraindications | ALTEPLASE DOSING PROTOCOL: 15mg IV bolus then 0.75mg/kg over 30 minutes (not to exceed 50mg) then 0.5mg/kg over 60 minutes (not to exceed 35mg) | MAXIMUM TOTAL DOSE: 100mg | ALTERNATIVE THROMBOLYTIC - Tenecteplase (Metalyse): For late patient presentation ≥10 hrs post pain onset or allergy to Streptokinase | Tenecteplase dosing: Weight <100kg - 8000 IU IV over 10 seconds | Weight >100kg - 10000 IU IV over 10 seconds | ADJUNCTIVE TREATMENTS: Aspirin 300mg chewed stat | Nitroglycerin: Nitrolingual Spray 1 spray at 5 minute intervals (max 3 sprays in 15min) or Sublingual 1 tablet repeated for total 3 doses at 5 min intervals | Contraindications to Nitroglycerin: Hypotension SBP <90mmHg, severe bradycardia <50bpm, severe tachycardia >100bpm, RV infarction (associated with inferior MI), use of phosphodiesterase inhibitors (Sildenafil/Vardenafil within 24hrs, Tadalafil within 48hrs) | Fentanyl IV if discomfort not relieved by nitroglycerin: 50mcg aliquots | Heparin: LMWH Clexane 30mg IV stat then 1mg/kg SC after 15 min, or if no LMWH: Heparin 60IU/kg (max 4000IU) IV stat then infusion 12IU/kg/hr (max 1000IU/hr) | Clopidogrel (Plavix) 300mg stat po | Simvastatin (Zocor) 40mg stat po | Beta-blocker if no contraindications: Atenolol (Tenormin) 50mg stat po | Contraindications to Beta-blockers: Haemodynamic instability, heart failure, pulmonary oedema, SBP <100mmHg, bradycardia HR ≤60bpm, AV block 2nd or 3rd degree, asthma | POST-THROMBOLYSIS MONITORING: Patient requires continuous monitoring - Blood pressure, heart rate, O2 Sats, GCS | ECGs to be done at 0 min prior to commencing and then at 15 min, 30 min, 60 min and 90 min | ECGs also to be done with any rhythm changes or interventions | Please indicate patient's pain score out of 10 on each ECG as well as vital signs | Reperfusion arrhythmias are common and usually transient and seldom require intervention | MANAGEMENT OF BLEEDING AFTER ALTEPLASE: Stop alteplase infusion | FBC, PT (INR), aPTT, fibrinogen level, and type and screen | Emergent non-contrast CTB if intracranial bleed suspected | Cryoprecipitate 10 U/kg infused over 10-30 min | Tranexamic acid 1g IV infused over 10 min | Haematology and neurosurgery consultations | Supportive therapy including BP management, temperature, and glucose control | Risk factors for ACS: Age ≥65 years, smoker, family history of CAD, diabetes mellitus, hypercholesterolaemia, hypertension, drug abuse (cocaine, methamphetamine), previous MI, HIV ± HAART (PI) | Aspirin contraindications: Known hypersensitivity - give Clopidogrel 300mg stat po instead | Relative: Active peptic ulcer disease, bleeding disorder, allergy | Refer to Internal Medicine/Cardiology for all STEMI patients

drugs.adult_dose: 15mg IV bolus then 0.75mg/kg over 30 min (max 50mg) then 0.5mg/kg over 60 min (max 35mg). Maximum total dose 100mg
drugs.notes: First-line thrombolytic for STEMI. For acute STEMI with symptom onset <12 hours and no contraindications. Written and signed consent required prior to administration. Door to needle time target: 30 minutes. See package insert for dose/kg.

drugs.adult_dose: Weight <100kg: 8000 IU IV over 10 seconds. Weight >100kg: 10000 IU IV over 10 seconds
drugs.notes: Alternative thrombolytic for late patient presentation (≥10 hours post pain onset) or allergy to Streptokinase
Give immediately unless hypersensitive (then give Clopidogrel 300mg stat po instead). Contraindications: Active peptic ulcer disease, bleeding disorder, allergy.
Thienopyridine antiplatelet. Give to aspirin-allergic patients as substitute (300mg stat). Also given as adjunctive therapy with aspirin in STEMI.

drugs.adult_dose: 1 spray at 5 minute intervals (max 3 sprays in 15 min)
drugs.notes: Check ECG first. Contraindications: SBP <90mmHg, severe bradycardia <50bpm, severe tachycardia >100bpm, RV infarction, PDE5 inhibitors (Sildenafil/Vardenafil within 24hrs, Tadalafil within 48hrs).

drugs.adult_dose: 1 tablet repeated for total 3 doses at 5 min intervals
drugs.notes: Alternative to spray. Same contraindications.
For discomfort not relieved by nitroglycerin. Titrate to pain relief.

drugs.adult_dose: 30mg IV stat then 1mg/kg SC after 15 min
drugs.notes: LMWH heparin. Preferred over unfractionated heparin. First dose IV, subsequent dosing SC.
Use if LMWH (Clexane) not available. Monitor aPTT.
High-intensity statin therapy for all STEMI patients unless contraindicated.
Beta-blocker. Only if no contraindications: haemodynamic instability, heart failure, pulmonary oedema, SBP <100mmHg, bradycardia ≤60bpm, AV block 2nd/3rd degree, asthma.
For management of bleeding after alteplase administration.
For management of bleeding after alteplase administration. Antifibrinolytic agent.
For BP lowering when SBP >180mmHg or DBP >110mmHg prior to thrombolysis. Also consider for ongoing management. See infusion protocol.

management_steps.details: Triage to ORANGE/Resus. ABC approach. General appearance assessment. Focused physical examination. 12 and 15 lead ECG (V4R, V7-9). Check vital signs and O2 sats. Establish IV access. Finger prick glucose. Bloods: Troponin I, U&E, FBC, VBG. Detailed history. Portable CXR.
management_steps.caution: Time is muscle - do not delay. Door to needle time target is 30 minutes.

management_steps.details: ST elevation in 2 or more contiguous leads per gender/age-specific criteria. Check for STEMI equivalents (true posterior MI, RV infarction). Calculate Sgarbossa score if LBBB or paced rhythm present.
management_steps.caution: Consider aortic dissection: central chest pain radiating to back, tearing sensation, differential radial pulse, differential arm BP >15mmHg, widened mediastinum on CXR >8cm, POCUS aortic root >4.5cm

management_steps.action: Check time window and ongoing symptoms
management_steps.details: Confirm symptom onset or maximum pain <12 hours. Confirm ongoing continuous chest pain. If onset >12 hours, assess if still continuous chest pain.
management_steps.caution: If >12 hours AND pain resolved, may not be candidate for thrombolysis - refer to Internal Medicine/Cardiology

management_steps.details: Review ALL absolute contraindications: prior intracranial haemorrhage, known structural cerebral vascular lesion, known malignant intracranial neoplasm, ischaemic stroke within 3 months (except acute stroke within 3 hours), suspected aortic dissection, active bleeding or bleeding diathesis, significant closed head or facial trauma within 3 months. Review ALL relative contraindications.
management_steps.caution: If any absolute contraindication present, DO NOT thrombolyse. Refer urgently to cardiology for primary PCI consideration.

management_steps.details: Support ABCs. If O2 sats <94%, start nasal prong O2 at 2L/min and titrate. Aspirin 300mg chewed stat. Nitroglycerin spray 1 spray at 5 min intervals (max 3 in 15 min). Fentanyl 50mcg aliquots IV if pain not relieved.
management_steps.caution: Check nitroglycerin contraindications before administration: SBP <90, HR <50 or >100, RV infarction, recent PDE5 inhibitor use

management_steps.details: Written and signed consent required prior to thrombolysis. Explain risks and benefits to patient or family member.
management_steps.caution: Document consent clearly. If patient unable to consent, obtain from next of kin.

management_steps.details: 15mg IV bolus administered over 1-2 minutes, then 0.75mg/kg IV infusion over 30 minutes (max 50mg), then 0.5mg/kg IV infusion over 60 minutes (max 35mg). Maximum total dose 100mg.
management_steps.caution: Follow weight-based dosing carefully. Do not exceed maximum doses. Monitor for allergic reactions. Alternative: Tenecteplase for late presenters (≥10h) or streptokinase allergy.

management_steps.details: Clexane 30mg IV stat then 1mg/kg SC after 15 min. If no LMWH available: Heparin 60IU/kg IV stat (max 4000IU) then infusion 12IU/kg/hr (max 1000IU/hr).
management_steps.caution: Monitor for bleeding. Adjust doses in elderly/low body weight.

management_steps.action: Start adjunctive antiplatelet and other therapies
management_steps.details: Clopidogrel 300mg stat po. Simvastatin 40mg stat po. Atenolol 50mg stat po if no contraindications (haemodynamic instability, heart failure, pulmonary oedema, SBP <100, HR ≤60, AV block 2nd/3rd degree, asthma).
management_steps.caution: Verify no beta-blocker contraindications before administration.

management_steps.details: Continuous monitoring of blood pressure, heart rate, O2 sats, GCS. ECGs at 0 min (prior), 15 min, 30 min, 60 min, and 90 min. Additional ECGs with any rhythm changes or interventions. Record pain score out of 10 and vital signs on each ECG.
management_steps.caution: Reperfusion arrhythmias are common and usually transient - seldom require intervention. Be prepared to treat VF/pulseless VT per ACLS if needed.

management_steps.details: All STEMI patients require cardiology referral post-thrombolysis. Consider need for rescue PCI or early catheterisation.
management_steps.caution: Arrange transfer to PCI-capable centre if available and clinically indicated.

management_steps.action: Manage bleeding complications if they occur
management_steps.details: Stop alteplase infusion immediately. FBC, PT (INR), aPTT, fibrinogen level, type and screen. Emergent non-contrast CTB if intracranial bleeding suspected. Cryoprecipitate 10 U/kg over 10-30 min. Tranexamic acid 1g IV over 10 min. Haematology and neurosurgery consultations. Supportive therapy including BP management, temperature and glucose control.
management_steps.caution: Intracranial haemorrhage is the most feared complication. Any new neurological deficit post-thrombolysis requires immediate CT brain and cessation of infusion.

STEMI_ST_Elevation_Criteria.Men_40_years_and_older: J-point elevation ≥2mm in leads V2-V3 and ≥1mm in all other leads
STEMI_ST_Elevation_Criteria.Men_under_40_years: J-point elevation ≥2.5mm in leads V2-V3 and ≥1mm in all other leads
STEMI_ST_Elevation_Criteria.Women: J-point elevation ≥1.5mm in leads V2-V3 and ≥1mm in all other leads
STEMI_ST_Elevation_Criteria.Requirement: ST elevation in 2 or more contiguous leads

STEMI_Equivalents: True posterior MI: tall R in V1 and STE ≥0.5mm in V7-V9
RV infarction: ST elevation in V4R
Patients with acutely occluded coronary arteries who do not present with classical ECG changes
Often associated with poorer outcome and worse prognosis

Sgarbossa_Criteria_LBBB_or_Paced_Rhythm.Criteria_A_5_points: ≥1mm concordant ST elevation in at least one lead
Sgarbossa_Criteria_LBBB_or_Paced_Rhythm.Criteria_B_3_points: ≥1mm concordant ST depression in V1, V2 or V3
Sgarbossa_Criteria_LBBB_or_Paced_Rhythm.Criteria_C_2_points: ≥5mm discordant ST elevation in at least one lead
Sgarbossa_Criteria_LBBB_or_Paced_Rhythm.Interpretation: Total score ≥3 has specificity of 90% for MI

Aortic_Dissection_Red_Flags: Central chest pain radiating to back - tearing sensation
Absolute_Contraindications_Thrombolysis: Known structural cerebral vascular lesion e.g. AV malformation
Ischaemic stroke within 3 months (except acute ischaemic stroke within 3 hours)
Significant closed head trauma or facial trauma within 3 months
Relative_Contraindications_Thrombolysis: History of chronic, severe, poorly controlled hypertension
SBP >180mmHg or DBP >110mmHg - lower BP with Nitrocine infusion
Traumatic or prolonged CPR (>10 min) - NB consider whether MI may have caused the arrest
Active peptic ulcer disease (bled within past 2-4 weeks)
Current use of anticoagulants e.g. Warfarin

clinical_features.STEMI_Presentation: Chest pain or symptoms suggestive of ischaemia or infarction with ST elevation on ECG
clinical_features.Time_Window: Symptom onset or maximum pain <12 hours for thrombolysis
clinical_features.True_Posterior_MI: Tall R wave in V1 with ST elevation ≥0.5mm in V7-V9
clinical_features.RV_Infarction: ST elevation in V4R (associated with inferior MI)

protocols.monitoring: Continuous monitoring post-thrombolysis is MANDATORY: Blood pressure, heart rate, O2 sats, GCS monitored continuously | ECG timing: At 0 minutes (prior to commencing thrombolysis), then at 15 min, 30 min, 60 min, and 90 min post-thrombolysis | Additional ECGs must be performed with any rhythm changes or interventions | Record patient's pain score out of 10 on each ECG as well as vital signs | Reperfusion arrhythmias are common post-thrombolysis and are usually transient - they seldom require intervention | Monitor for signs of bleeding including intracranial haemorrhage (new headache, altered GCS, focal neurology), gastrointestinal bleeding (haematemesis, melaena), and access site bleeding | Door to needle time target: 30 minutes | All patients require cardiology/Internal Medicine referral and ongoing care in monitored setting
warnings: TIME IS MUSCLE - do not delay reperfusion therapy
Door to needle time target: 30 minutes
Maximum total Alteplase dose: 100mg - do not exceed
Intracranial haemorrhage is the most feared and life-threatening complication of thrombolysis
Any new neurological deficit, severe headache, or altered consciousness post-thrombolysis requires immediate cessation of infusion and emergent CT brain
Suspected aortic dissection is an absolute contraindication to thrombolysis - thrombolysis in dissection can be fatal
RV infarction (associated with inferior MI) is a contraindication to nitroglycerin and beta-blockers - causes hypotension
Concurrent anticoagulation (heparin) increases bleeding risk - monitor closely
Recent PDE5 inhibitor use (Sildenafil/Vardenafil within 24 hours, Tadalafil within 48 hours) is a contraindication to nitroglycerin - risk of profound hypotension
Traumatic or prolonged CPR >10 minutes is a relative contraindication - however consider whether the MI may have caused the cardiac arrest
Active bleeding or bleeding diathesis is an absolute contraindication
Current use of anticoagulants (e.g. Warfarin) is a relative contraindication - increased bleeding risk
Non-compressible vascular punctures are a relative contraindication - avoid arterial punctures and venepuncture at non-compressible sites
Reperfusion arrhythmias are common post-thrombolysis - usually transient, be prepared to treat per ACLS if haemodynamically significant
Written and signed consent is mandatory prior to thrombolysis
All STEMI patients require referral to Internal Medicine/Cardiology regardless of thrombolysis outcome
If SBP >180mmHg or DBP >110mmHg, lower BP with nitroglycerin (Nitrocine) infusion before thrombolysis
For streptokinase: prior exposure or prior allergic reaction is a relative contraindication (use alternative thrombolytic)
Post-thrombolysis bleeding management: Stop infusion, FBC/INR/aPTT/fibrinogen, CT brain if indicated, cryoprecipitate 10U/kg, tranexamic acid 1g IV, haematology and neurosurgery consults
protocols.disposition: All STEMI patients MUST be referred to Internal Medicine/Cardiology following thrombolysis | Patient requires ongoing care in a monitored setting (high care/ICU/step-down) | Consider need for rescue PCI if failed reperfusion (ongoing chest pain, <50% ST resolution at 90 min) | Arrange transfer to PCI-capable centre if available and clinically indicated | If thrombolysis is contraindicated, urgent referral for primary PCI is required | If symptom onset >12 hours and chest pain has resolved, refer to cardiology for risk stratification and non-urgent management | Continue aspirin, clopidogrel, statin, and beta-blocker (if tolerated) post-discharge | Arrange cardiology follow-up within 1 week | Document door-to-needle time and reperfusion outcomes | Patient education: cardiac rehabilitation, medication compliance, lifestyle modification, warning symptoms requiring return to ED

decontamination.skin_decon: Remove clothing, wash patient with soap and water. Universal precautions.

management_steps.details: Remove clothing, wash patient with soap and water. Universal precautions.
General management principles of a poisoned patient
0.04-0.05 mg/kg IV, double every 5 minutes until atropinisation (clearing of respiratory secretions)
Once atropinised, start infusion at 10-20% of total atropinisation dose per hour
Check blood glucose. Use benzodiazepine only (Clonazepam, Diazepam, Lorazepam). NO anticonvulsant agents.
Consider MgSO4 4g/day (weak evidence)
Check if patient can lift and hold head up for 10 seconds; ask patient to squeeze your hand. Intubation and supportive management only way to control respiratory failure from weakness.

drugs.adult_dose: 0.04-0.05 mg/kg IV (3-5 mg adult) STAT, double every 5 minutes until atropinisation ('escalating atropinisation')
drugs.route: IV (can administer IM if no immediate IV access)
drugs.frequency: Double every 5 minutes until atropinisation achieved
drugs.notes: 100mg amps in resus, NB SECTION 21 FORM required. Only treats muscarinic symptoms. May cause anticholinergic toxidrome at high doses - monitor closely. NB urine catheter.

drugs.adult_dose: 200 mg Atropine in 200 ml 0.9% NaCl (1 mg/ml). 10-20% of total dose already given per hour
drugs.notes: Start once atropinised. Total dose refers to dose given for escalating atropinisation.

monitoring.target: Clearing of secretions = endpoint of atropinisation (NOT based on tachycardia or pupil dilation)

warnings: Avoid succinylcholine if intubation required (causes prolonged paralysis)
Atropine only treats muscarinic symptoms, not nicotinic
May cause anticholinergic toxidrome at high doses - monitor closely
Urine catheter essential due to urinary retention from atropine
SECTION 21 FORM required for atropine (100mg amps in resus)
protocols.disposition: Internal Medicine consultation
protocols.notes_updates:
Muscarinic: D - Diaphoresis, diarrhoea, U - Urination, M - Miosis, B - Bradycardia, bronchorrhoea, bronchospasm, E - Emesis, L - Lacrimation, S - Salivation
Nicotinic: Fasciculations, Weakness, Hypertension, Tachycardia, Mydriasis
Cns: Anxiety, Ataxia, Restlessness, Confusion, Convulsions, Coma
Disposition: Internal Medicine consultation
Warnings: Avoid succinylcholine if intubation required (causes prolonged paralysis); Atropine only treats muscarinic symptoms, not nicotinic; May cause anticholinergic toxidrome at high doses - monitor closely; Urine catheter essential due to urinary retention from atropine; SECTION 21 FORM required for atropine (100mg amps in resus)

management_steps.details: Remove trigger if possible
Place supine and elevate legs
0.5 ml IM 1:1000 anterolateral thigh STAT. Repeat every 3-5 min if no improvement.
Apply immediately
Establish monitoring and IV access
Partial obstruction: Nebulized adrenaline 5mg, prepare for difficult airway. Complete obstruction: Bag-mask ventilation with high-flow O2, prepare for difficult airway.
Continuous nebulization with beta-2 agonists for wheeze/dyspnoea
Crystalloid (Ringers Lactate/Balsol) 20 ml/kg, repeat as necessary - large amounts may be required
Promethazine 12.5 mg slow IV (max 25 mg)
Hydrocortisone 200 mg slow IV
Start if poor response/deterioration despite repeated IM doses (see infusion protocol)
If cardiac arrest 2° anaphylaxis: ACLS protocol + immediate high-dose adrenaline + high volume IV fluids

drugs.adult_dose: 0.5 ml IM STAT (0.01 mg/kg up to 0.5 mg), using 1mg/ml 1:1000 concentration
drugs.frequency: Repeat every 3-5 minutes if no improvement. For cardiac arrest: 3-5mg every 2-3 minutes (high-dose escalation).
drugs.notes: First-line treatment. Do not delay. Nebulized adrenaline (5mg) for partial airway obstruction.
Children - weight dependent. H1 antihistamine.
Children - weight dependent. May help prevent biphasic reaction.

warnings: Biphasic reaction can occur in 5-20% of cases
Risk factors for biphasic: delayed adrenaline, slow response, repeated doses, need for IV fluids, lack of steroids
If partial airway obstruction: nebulized adrenaline 5mg, prepare for difficult airway
If complete airway obstruction: bag-mask ventilation, prepare for difficult airway
Adrenaline infusion may be needed for poor response
Do NOT give beta-blockers - unopposed alpha agonism
protocols.disposition: Monitor for biphasic reaction (5-20% recurrence). Risk factors: delayed adrenaline, slow response, repeated doses needed, IV fluids required, lack of steroid use. Observation period required.
protocols.notes_updates:
Disposition: Monitor for biphasic reaction (5-20% recurrence). Risk factors: delayed adrenaline, slow response, repeated doses needed, IV fluids required, lack of steroid use. Observation period required.
Warnings: Biphasic reaction can occur in 5-20% of cases; Risk factors for biphasic: delayed adrenaline, slow response, repeated doses, need for IV fluids, lack of steroids; If partial airway obstruction: nebulized adrenaline 5mg, prepare for difficult airway; If complete airway obstruction: bag-mask ventilation, prepare for difficult airway; Adrenaline infusion may be needed for poor response; Do NOT give beta-blockers - unopposed alpha agonism

decontamination.skin_decon: Stop offending agent (ACE-I/ARB) - document in discharge summary

management_steps.details: Continuous monitoring
Dyspnoea, dysphagia/odynophagia, dysphonia, stridor, hoarseness, drooling. CALL FOR HELP if present.
Assess for shock: fluid bolus and vasopressors per anaphylaxis guidelines if needed

Avoid airway manipulation if possible. Most skilled person to attempt intubation. Ideally intubate in theatre with awake fibre-optic laryngoscopy and surgical airway double setup. LMA unlikely to be successful - move directly to cricothyrotomy if intubation fails.
ACE-I/ARB (bradykinin-mediated) vs allergic (histaminergic). Check exposures, medications, family history, past presentations.
STOP offending agent. Tranexamic acid 1g over 10 min. FFP 2 units. Adrenaline, steroids, antihistamines unlikely to be effective. Many resolve with observation over 24-48 hours.
Adrenaline 0.5ml IM, Solucortef 200mg IV, Promethazine 12.5mg IV. Follow anaphylaxis pathway.

Fibreoptic laryngoscopy (preferably in theatre), C1 esterase inhibitor assays, C4 levels, serial tryptase levels. CT abdomen if severe abdominal pain. CT neck to exclude mimics (do NOT send with unprotected airway).

drugs.notes: For ACE-I/ARB (bradykinin-mediated) angioedema. Reduces conversion of plasminogen to plasmin which reduces bradykinin production.
For ACE-I/ARB angioedema. FFP contains ACE which degrades bradykinin.

drugs.adult_dose: 0.5 ml neat IM antero-lateral thigh, repeat 2-5 min or start infusion
drugs.notes: For histaminergic cases only. Unlikely effective in bradykinin-mediated (ACE-I/ARB) cases.
For histaminergic cases
For histaminergic cases

warnings: HAE (Hereditary Angioedema) vs allergic angioedema have different management pathways
ACE-I/ARB cases (bradykinin-mediated): Adrenaline, steroids, antihistamines UNLIKELY effective
LMA unlikely to be successful in angioedema - move directly to cricothyrotomy if intubation fails
Do NOT send to CT with an unprotected airway
Avoid airway manipulation if possible - failed attempts may worsen compromise
Most skilled person should attempt intubation
Many ACE-I/ARB cases can be managed with observation only, tend to resolve over 24-48 hours
protocols.disposition: Isolated face/lips involvement: monitor 4-8 hours, consider discharge with outpatient follow-up. Monitored admission for: incomplete resolution, previous angioedema history, tongue/laryngeal/pharyngeal oedema, concern for severe allergic reaction. Ensure patient aware of ACEi/ARB brand names (drugs ending in 'pril' and 'sartan') - give written report and document on TTO.
protocols.notes_updates:
Disposition: Isolated face/lips involvement: monitor 4-8 hours, consider discharge with outpatient follow-up. Monitored admission for: incomplete resolution, previous angioedema history, tongue/laryngeal/pharyngeal oedema, concern for severe allergic reaction. Ensure patient aware of ACEi/ARB brand names (drugs ending in 'pril' and 'sartan') - give written report and document on TTO.
Warnings: HAE (Hereditary Angioedema) vs allergic angioedema have different management pathways; ACE-I/ARB cases (bradykinin-mediated): Adrenaline, steroids, antihistamines UNLIKELY effective; LMA unlikely to be successful in angioedema - move directly to cricothyrotomy if intubation fails; Do NOT send to CT with an unprotected airway; Avoid airway manipulation if possible - failed attempts may worsen compromise; Most skilled person should attempt intubation; Many ACE-I/ARB cases can be managed with observation only, tend to resolve over 24-48 hours

protocols.item: Toxic Alcohol Ingestion (Ethylene Glycol, Methanol, Isopropyl Alcohol)

cns: Ethylene glycol: coma, seizures, meningism, muscle spasms, ocular paralysis
Methanol: Parkinsonism, transverse myelitis, basal ganglia haemorrhages, inebriation
Isopropyl: profound intoxication, cerebellar signs, coma

management_steps.details: Even if patient initially appears well
2 IV lines, monitors, HGT, VBG, urine beta-HCG, ECG
Especially potassium, magnesium
20 ml/kg if hypotensive
Amount and time of ingestion, co-ingestants, comorbidities, chronic medications, symptoms prior to arrival
Ocular (methanol: snowstorm vision), neurologic, cardiopulmonary
FBC, U&E, LFT, Paracetamol/Salicylate level, urine tox screen, serum alcohol level. Calculate anion gap and osmolar gap - HAGMA with high osmolar gap suggests toxic alcohol ingestion.
Sodabic boluses 1 ml/kg 8.5% until pH > 7.2
IV benzodiazepines. Monitor for arrhythmias/hypoglycaemia as cause.
Intubation prior to ethanol administration (if using ethanol as antidote)
Fomepizole 15 mg/kg loading then 10 mg/kg 12hrly OR Ethanol 40% 1.5-2 ml/kg load then 0.3 ml/kg/hr NGT
Ethylene glycol: pyridoxine 100mg QID + thiamine 100mg QID. Methanol: folic acid 50mg 4-6hrly.
Haemodialysis effective, usually required. Medicine/renal consult.
Supportive treatment only + PPI. No antidote required.

drugs.adult_dose: 15 mg/kg loading dose, then 10 mg/kg 12 hourly
drugs.notes: Preferred antidote for ethylene glycol and methanol. Continue until vital signs and lab values normalize or patient receives dialysis.

drugs.adult_dose: 1.5-2 ml/kg loading dose, then 0.3 ml/kg/hr maintenance
drugs.notes: Alternative if fomepizole unavailable. Airway protection required prior to administration (intubate first). Continue until vital signs and lab values normalize or patient receives dialysis.
Cofactor for ethylene glycol - promotes conversion to non-toxic metabolites
Cofactor for ethylene glycol
Cofactor for methanol - promotes conversion to non-toxic metabolites

warnings: HAGMA with high osmolar gap suggests toxic alcohol ingestion
Airway protection required prior to ethanol administration
Haemodialysis usually required - consult renal early
Isopropyl alcohol does NOT cause acidosis (metabolized to acetone, ketosis only)
Ethylene glycol causes artefactual lactic acidosis, hypocalcaemia, prolonged QT, acute renal failure due to calcium oxalate crystalluria
Methanol causes ocular toxicity: blurry vision, reduced visual acuity, photophobia, snowstorm vision
Decontamination NOT recommended for toxic alcohols
protocols.disposition: Medicine/renal consult. Will likely require ICU/High care. Continue serial ECG and VBG monitoring in ED.
protocols.notes_updates:

management_steps.details: Resuscitation (Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Seizure control, Correct hypoglycaemia, Correct hyperthermia, Resuscitation antidotes) -> Risk Assessment -> Supportive Care & Monitoring -> Investigations -> Decontamination & Enhanced Elimination -> Antidotes -> Disposition

Agents: Organophosphates, carbamates, physostigmine, mushroom toxins. Features: DUMBELS + nicotinic (fasciculations, weakness) + CNS (confusion, seizures, coma). Management: Atropine boluses/infusion. See Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitor Overdose Protocol.

Agents: Antihistamines, antiparkinsonians, atropine, antispasmodics, plant toxins (Devil's trumpet, Jimson weed, Nightshade). Features: Delirium, tachycardia, dry/flushed skin, dilated pupils, hyperthermia, myoclonus, urinary retention, seizures, decreased bowel sounds. Management: Benzodiazepines for agitation, activated charcoal, physostigmine (expert consultation).

Agents: Amphetamines ('Tik'), cocaine, withdrawal states, hypermetabolic syndromes (MH, NMS), novel street drugs, body packers. Features: Agitation, delusions, paranoia, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, mydriasis, hyperreflexia, seizures. Management: Benzodiazepines for agitation, supportive care. AVOID beta-blockers (unopposed alpha agonism).

Agents: Opioids, Nyaope and other opioid-based illicit substances. Features: Sedation, coma, respiratory and cardiovascular depression, miosis. Management: Naloxone 0.04-2 mg IV boluses. Titrate to respiratory effort.

Agents: Opioids, benzodiazepines, barbiturates, Nyaope. Features: Sedation, coma, respiratory and cardiovascular depression, miosis/mydriasis. Management: Mainstay is ventilatory and cardiovascular support. Opioids: naloxone. Benzos: AVOID flumazenil (can precipitate withdrawal/seizures).

drugs.adult_dose: 0.04-0.05 mg/kg IV, double every 5 min until atropinisation
drugs.notes: For cholinergic toxidrome (organophosphate/carbamate). Only treats muscarinic symptoms.
For opioid toxidrome. Titrate to respiratory effort, not full consciousness.
For anticholinergic toxidrome. Expert consultation required. Contraindicated in TCA overdose, cardiac conduction abnormalities.
For agitation in sympathomimetic and anticholinergic toxidromes. Mainstay for sedative-hypnotic supportive care.
