Physostigmine contraindicated in TCA overdose and cardiac conduction abnormalities
All toxicology patients need ECG and paracetamol level
FASTHUGS IN BED PLEASE monitoring framework applies
Sodium channel blockers: wide QRS >100ms = 1/3 seizures risk, >160ms = ventricular dysrhythmias risk
Potassium channel blockers: prolonged QT, risk of Torsades de Pointes
Activated charcoal (1 g/kg) if life-threatening quantity and within 1 hour of ingestion

Muscarinic: DUMBELS: Diaphoresis, diarrhoea; Urination; Miosis; Bradycardia, bronchorrhoea, bronchospasm; Emesis; Lacrimation; Salivation
Nicotinic: Fasciculations, weakness, hypertension, tachycardia, mydriasis
Anticholinergic: Mad as a hatter: Delirium, Blind as a bat: Dilated pupils, blurred vision, Red as a beet: Flushed skin, hyperthermia, Dry as a bone: Dry skin/mucous membranes, urinary retention, Hot as a hare: Hyperthermia, Tachycardia, myoclonus, decreased bowel sounds, seizures

HIET: Monitor HGT every 20 min for 1st hour, then hourly
Replace potassium if <2.5 mmol/L during HIET
Dextrose infusion via central line for euglycaemia maintenance
Therapeutic endpoints for HIET: EF>50%, SBP>90mmHg, HR>60bpm, resolution of acidaemia, euglycaemia, urine output 1-2ml/kg/hr, QRS<120ms, improved GCS
Consider ECMO if no response to HIET
Beta-blocker OD may lead to profound CNS depression
Dihydropyridine class (non-selective CCBs) at lower doses may cause peripheral vasodilation with reflex tachycardia
protocols.disposition: Int Med consult (consider renal consult for dialysis). Will likely require ICU/High care. Continue serial ECG and VBG monitoring in ED.
protocols.notes_updates:
Cns: CNS depression, Profound CNS depression (beta-blocker OD may lead to this)
Disposition: Int Med consult (consider renal consult for dialysis). Will likely require ICU/High care. Continue serial ECG and VBG monitoring in ED.
Warnings: Propranolol: Also acts as Na channel blocker - TCA-like features of OD; Sotalol: Blocks myocardial K channels - QT prolongation, Torsades in OD; CCB causes hyperglycaemia; BB causes hypoglycaemia; HIET: Monitor HGT every 20 min for 1st hour, then hourly; Replace potassium if <2.5 mmol/L during HIET; Dextrose infusion via central line for euglycaemia maintenance; Therapeutic endpoints for HIET: EF>50%, SBP>90mmHg, HR>60bpm, resolution of acidaemia, euglycaemia, urine output 1-2ml/kg/hr, QRS<120ms, improved GCS; Consider ECMO if no response to HIET; Beta-blocker OD may lead to profound CNS depression; Dihydropyridine class (non-selective CCBs) at lower doses may cause peripheral vasodilation with reflex tachycardia

reversal_agents.name: HIET (High-Dose Insulin)
reversal_agents.dose: Actrapid 1 IU/kg load then 0.5-10 IU/kg/hr infusion

protocols.notes_updates: Remembered by the mnemonic 'Resus - RSI - DEAD' | RESUSCITATION | Airway | Breathing | Circulation | Seizure control | Correct hypoglycaemia | Correct hyperthermia | Resuscitation antidotes: e.g. atropine for organophosphates; NaHCO3 for tricyclic antidepressants & salicylates; HIET for beta-blockers | RISK ASSESSMENT | Agent(s) | Dose(s) | Time since ingestion | Current clinical status | Patient factors | SUPPORTIVE CARE & MONITORING | FASTHUGS IN BED Please (see below) - applies where appropriate | INVESTIGATIONS | ECG and paracetamol level for ALL | Further tests as indicated by specific presentation | Rationale for investigations: Refine risk assessment or prognosis | Exclude or confirm an important differential | Exclude or confirm an important specific poisoning | Exclude or confirm a complication requiring specific management | Establish an indication for antidote administration | Establish an indication for institution of enhanced elimination | Monitor response to therapy | Define an endpoint for therapeutic interventions | DECONTAMINATION & ENHANCED ELIMINATION | Activated charcoal (1g/kg) may be considered if a patient has ingested a potentially life-threatening quantity of substance/s and presents within 1 hour of ingestion | Consider transferring for dialysis (see box) | INGESTIONS THAT MAY REQUIRE DIALYSIS | Barbiturates | Salicylates | Lithium | Toxic alcohols (methanol, ethanol, ethylene glycol) | Theophylline/Caffeine | INH (Seldom) | Metformin | Dabigatran | Sodium valproate | Carbamazepine | FASTHUGS IN BED Please | F - fluid therapy & feeding | A - analgesia & antiemetics | S - sedation | T - thrombo-prophylaxis | H - head up position (30 degrees) | U - ulcer prophylaxis | G - glucose control | S - skin/eye care; suctioning | I - indwelling catheter | N - nasogastric tube | B - bowel cares | E - environment (temp control) | D - de-escalation | P - psychosocial support | DISPOSITION | Urgent Medicine/ICU referral

drugs.notes: Consider if potentially life-threatening ingestion and presents within 1 hour
Resuscitation antidote for organophosphates/carbamates
For tricyclic antidepressants and salicylates overdose

drugs.adult_dose: Actrapid 1 IU/kg IV stat, then 0.5 IU/kg/hr titrate to max 10 IU/kg/hr. Dextrose 25g/hr maintenance.
drugs.notes: For beta-blocker and calcium channel blocker overdose. Start with 50ml 50% dextrose bolus. Monitor HGT every 20 min for 1st hour, then hourly. Monitor potassium - replace if <2.5 mmol.

drugs.adult_dose: Calcium chloride 10ml 10% over 10 min via large bore IV or central line (target Ca 2mmol)
drugs.notes: For calcium channel blocker overdose
For local anaesthetic toxicity and consider for beta-blocker/CCB overdose
Antidote for iron overdose
For methanol toxicity
For methaemoglobinaemia
Antidote for paracetamol overdose
Antidote for isoniazid (INH) overdose
For warfarin toxicity

management_steps.details: Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Seizure control, Correct hypoglycaemia, Correct hyperthermia. Give resuscitation antidotes if indicated (atropine for OPs, NaHCO3 for TCAs/salicylates, HIET for beta-blockers).
management_steps.caution: Address life-threatening issues first

management_steps.details: Identify: Agent(s), Dose(s), Time since ingestion, Current clinical status, Patient factors.
management_steps.caution: Accurate risk assessment guides all subsequent management

management_steps.details: FASTHUGS IN BED Please: F-fluid therapy & feeding, A-analgesia & antiemetics, S-sedation, T-thrombo-prophylaxis, H-head up 30 degrees, U-ulcer prophylaxis, G-glucose control, S-skin/eye care & suctioning, I-indwelling catheter, N-nasogastric tube, B-bowel cares, E-environment/temp control, D-de-escalation, P-psychosocial support.
management_steps.caution: Apply where appropriate to all poisoned patients

management_steps.details: ECG and paracetamol level for ALL patients. Further tests as indicated. Rationale: refine risk assessment/prognosis, exclude/confirm differential diagnosis, exclude/confirm specific poisoning, exclude/confirm complications, establish indication for antidote, establish indication for enhanced elimination, monitor response, define endpoint.
management_steps.caution: Never miss paracetamol overdose - test ALL

management_steps.details: Activated charcoal (1g/kg) may be considered if potentially life-threatening quantity ingested and presents within 1 hour. Consider dialysis for specific ingestions (barbiturates, salicylates, lithium, toxic alcohols, theophylline/caffeine, INH, metformin, dabigatran, sodium valproate, carbamazepine).
management_steps.caution: Charcoal only within 1 hour of ingestion for life-threatening quantities

management_steps.details: Give specific antidote if available and indicated: Atropine (OPs/carbamates), Calcium (CCBs), Desferrioxamine (iron), Ethanol/folic acid (methanol), HIET (beta-blockers), Intralipid (local anaesthetics), Methylene blue (methaemoglobinaemia), NAC (paracetamol), Pyridoxine (INH), Sodium bicarbonate (TCAs/salicylates), Vit K/FFP (warfarin).
management_steps.caution: Refer to specific overdose protocols for dosing details

management_steps.details: Urgent Medicine/ICU referral as indicated by clinical status and risk assessment.
management_steps.caution: Even if patient initially appears well, may require ICU level care

diagnostic_criteria.risk_assessment_components: Agent(s), Dose(s), Time since ingestion, Current clinical status, Patient factors
diagnostic_criteria.investigation_rationale: 1) Refine risk assessment or prognosis, 2) Exclude/confirm differential, 3) Exclude/confirm specific poisoning, 4) Exclude/confirm complication, 5) Establish antidote indication, 6) Establish enhanced elimination indication, 7) Monitor response, 8) Define endpoint

clinical_features.toxidromes_to_consider: See specific toxidrome protocol for details
clinical_features.general_approach: Resus-RSI-DEAD mnemonic covers all poisoned patients systematically

protocols.monitoring: FASTHUGS IN BED Please: F-fluid therapy & feeding, A-analgesia & antiemetics, S-sedation, T-thrombo-prophylaxis, H-head up 30 degrees, U-ulcer prophylaxis, G-glucose control, S-skin/eye care & suctioning, I-indwelling catheter, N-nasogastric tube, B-bowel cares, E-environment/temp control, D-de-escalation, P-psychosocial support
warnings: ECG and paracetamol level for ALL poisoned patients
Activated charcoal only within 1 hour of ingestion for potentially life-threatening quantities
Dialysis may be required for: barbiturates, salicylates, lithium, toxic alcohols, theophylline/caffeine, INH, metformin, dabigatran, sodium valproate, carbamazepine
FASTHUGS IN BED Please applies where appropriate to all poisoned patients
Disposition: Urgent Medicine/ICU referral as indicated
protocols.disposition: Urgent Medicine/ICU referral as indicated by clinical status and risk assessment. All poisoned patients require appropriate level of care based on risk stratification.

protocols.notes_updates: Use the Paracetamol Treatment Nomogram to establish need for antidote | If high dosage suspected, rather give the antidote to the patient | DO NOT withhold NAC even if ingestion was 48-72 hours prior to presentation | Start treatment ASAP if indicated, preferably <8 hours post-ingestion | IF IN DOUBT, GIVE NAC | TOXIC DOSE: 150mg/kg or >10g in adults | INITIAL ASSESSMENT: Assess and support ABCs | Monitor glucose | Quantify amount ingested and co-ingestants | Establish timeframe from ingestion to presentation | Presentation <1 hour from ingestion: Activated charcoal 50g PO (not via NGT) | ECG for signs of co-ingestants e.g. prolonged QT | LAB INVESTIGATIONS: Blood gas, U&E, salicylate level | Paracetamol levels (provided >4 hours post ingestion) | No need for AST/ALT/INR unless >12 hours post ingestion | NAC CAUTIONS/SIDE-EFFECTS: Anaphylaxis, vomiting | Separate IV infusion as may be inactivated by other medications | Side-effects less common with SNAP Protocol | PREGNANCY: Paracetamol crosses placenta | Administer NAC as above | HIGH RISK PATIENTS: Hypotensive, pre-existing liver dysfunction (e.g. alcoholic), elderly | DISPOSITION: Internal Medicine referral | Refer to Protocol: General management principles of a poisoned/overdose patient

drugs.adult_dose: 150mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 1 hour, then 50mg/kg in 500ml 5% Dextrose IV over 4 hours, then 100mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose IV over 16 hours
drugs.paediatric_dose: Weight-based dosing as per adult regimen
drugs.notes: Total 21-hour regimen; old regimen with higher side-effect profile

drugs.adult_dose: 100mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 2 hours, then 200mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose IV over 10 hours
drugs.paediatric_dose: Weight-based dosing as per adult regimen
drugs.notes: Preferred dosing regimen; fewer side-effects; inform admitting doctor to continue this regimen; total 12-hour regimen
Give if presentation <1 hour from ingestion; do NOT administer via NGT

management_steps.details: Ensure airway, breathing, circulation are stable; monitor glucose
Attempt to quantify amount ingested and any co-ingestants; establish timeframe from ingestion to presentation
management_steps.caution: Toxic dose: 150mg/kg or >10g in adults

management_steps.details: 50g PO if presentation <1 hour from ingestion
management_steps.caution: Do NOT administer via NGT

management_steps.details: ECG for co-ingestant signs (e.g. prolonged QT); Blood gas, U&E, salicylate level; Paracetamol level if >4 hours post-ingestion; AST/ALT/INR only if >12 hours post-ingestion

management_steps.action: Use nomogram to determine need for NAC
management_steps.details: Plot paracetamol level against time since ingestion on treatment nomogram
management_steps.caution: If high dosage suspected, rather give antidote; IF IN DOUBT, GIVE NAC

management_steps.details: Preferred: SNAP Protocol - 100mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose over 2hr, then 200mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose over 10hr; Alternative: Old regimen - 150mg/kg over 1hr, then 50mg/kg over 4hr, then 100mg/kg over 16hr
management_steps.caution: Start ASAP preferably <8hrs post-ingestion; do NOT withhold even if 48-72hrs post-ingestion; separate IV infusion; watch for anaphylaxis and vomiting

management_steps.details: All paracetamol overdose patients requiring NAC need admission
management_steps.caution: Inform admitting doctor to continue SNAP Protocol if used

diagnostic_criteria.nomogram_use: Plot paracetamol level against time since ingestion; treatment required if above treatment line
diagnostic_criteria.paracetamol_level_timing: Levels must be taken >4 hours post-ingestion for nomogram interpretation

clinical_features.phase_1: Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, malaise, diaphoresis, pallor
clinical_features.phase_2: 24-72 hours: Right upper quadrant pain, elevated liver enzymes
clinical_features.phase_3: 72-96 hours: Peak hepatotoxicity, jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy

protocols.monitoring: Monitor for NAC side-effects (anaphylaxis, vomiting); liver function if >12 hours post-ingestion; serial paracetamol levels; blood glucose monitoring
warnings: DO NOT withhold NAC even if ingestion was 48-72 hours prior to presentation
IF IN DOUBT, GIVE NAC
Start treatment preferably <8 hours post-ingestion
NAC may cause anaphylaxis and vomiting - monitor closely
Separate IV infusion as NAC may be inactivated by other medications
High risk patients: hypotensive, pre-existing liver dysfunction (alcoholic), elderly
Paracetamol crosses the placenta - administer NAC in pregnancy as above
Do NOT give activated charcoal via NGT
protocols.disposition: Internal Medicine referral for all patients requiring NAC treatment

protocols.notes_updates: Scorpion stings may cause life-threatening envenomation | Species identification guide: scorpions with thick tails and slender pincers are more venomous than those with thin tails and large pincers | Scorpions are mainly nocturnal and active during summer months | Apart from local pain and occasional mild local inflammation, most scorpion stings are relatively harmless | In South Africa, only two Parabuthus species associated with severe envenomation: Parabuthus granulatus and Parabuthus transvaalicus | Severity usually evident within 15-60 minutes, but in adults systemic symptoms may be delayed for 8 hours | Children may present with unique restlessness marked by crying and thrashing - indication of severe systemic envenomation | Bulbar paralysis accompanied by respiratory failure is more frequent in children and primary cause of death | Differential diagnoses without history of sting: neurotoxic spider bites, snake bites, cholinesterase inhibitor toxicity, drug overdose, Guillain-Barre syndrome, tetanus | Scorpion antivenom should be given to all patients with symptoms and signs of severe envenomation | Standard antivenom dose: 5-10mL IV for both children and adults, diluted in small volume normal saline, given over 15 minutes | May take 2-6 hours to reach maximum effect | Additional dose of 5mL may be given after 6 hours if response inadequate | Patients kept under observation for 6-12 hours after antivenom administration | Pain management: if simple analgesia ineffective, infiltrate sting site with local anaesthetic | Opioids are relatively ineffective and increase risk of respiratory depression | Generalised muscle cramps: IV calcium gluconate 10% (Adults 10mL, children 0.5mL/kg) temporarily alleviates for 20-30 minutes | Support cardiorespiratory function as needed | Do NOT give antihistamines and steroids routinely | Tetanus toxoid if required | Poisons Information Helpline of the Western Cape: 0861 555 777

drugs.adult_dose: 5-10mL IV diluted in small volume normal saline, given over 15 minutes; additional 5mL after 6 hours if inadequate response
drugs.paediatric_dose: 5-10mL IV diluted in small volume normal saline, given over 15 minutes; additional 5mL after 6 hours if inadequate response
drugs.notes: For severe envenomation only; onset 2-6 hours; observe 6-12 hours after administration
For generalised muscle cramps; temporary relief for 20-30 minutes
For local pain if simple analgesia ineffective
Give if tetanus status not up to date

management_steps.details: Most stings cause local pain only; assess for systemic symptoms; severity evident within 15-60 minutes (may be delayed 8 hours in adults)
management_steps.caution: Children may present with unique restlessness (crying and thrashing) indicating severe envenomation

management_steps.action: Supportive care - ABC and cardiorespiratory support
management_steps.details: Support airway, breathing, circulation as needed; monitor for respiratory failure especially in children
management_steps.caution: Bulbar paralysis with respiratory failure more frequent in children and is primary cause of death

management_steps.action: Local wound care and pain management
management_steps.details: Simple analgesia first line; if ineffective infiltrate sting site with local anaesthetic; avoid opioids
management_steps.caution: Opioids are relatively ineffective and increase risk of respiratory depression

management_steps.details: IV calcium gluconate 10%: Adults 10mL, children 0.5mL/kg; provides temporary relief for 20-30 minutes

management_steps.action: Administer scorpion antivenom for severe envenomation
management_steps.details: 5-10mL IV diluted in small volume normal saline over 15 minutes; may repeat 5mL after 6 hours if inadequate response
management_steps.caution: Observe patient 6-12 hours after administration; onset of effect 2-6 hours

management_steps.details: Give tetanus toxoid if immunisation status not up to date
Observe for 6-12 hours after antivenom; monitor cardiorespiratory function
management_steps.caution: Do NOT give antihistamines and steroids routinely

diagnostic_criteria.severity_assessment: Mild: local pain and occasional mild local inflammation; Severe: systemic symptoms and signs of envenomation
diagnostic_criteria.timecourse: Severity usually evident within 15-60 minutes; adults may have delayed symptoms up to 8 hours
diagnostic_criteria.high_risk_species: Parabuthus granulatus and Parabuthus transvaalicus in South Africa
diagnostic_criteria.identification_guide: Thick tails and slender pincers = more venomous; thin tails and large pincers = less venomous

severe_envenomation_features: General weakness - unable to walk, difficulty in breathing, dysphagia and drooling
pediatric_features: Unique restlessness marked by crying and thrashing indicating severe envenomation
Respiratory failure - primary cause of death

protocols.monitoring: Observe 6-12 hours after antivenom administration; monitor cardiorespiratory function; watch for respiratory failure especially in children
warnings: Scorpions with thick tails and slender pincers are more venomous
Severity may be delayed up to 8 hours in adults
Children: unique restlessness (crying/thrashing) indicates severe envenomation
Bulbar paralysis with respiratory failure more frequent in children - primary cause of death
Opioids are relatively ineffective and increase risk of respiratory depression
Do NOT give antihistamines and steroids routinely
Only two South African species cause severe envenomation: Parabuthus granulatus and Parabuthus transvaalicus
protocols.disposition: Observe for 6-12 hours after antivenom administration; admit patients with severe envenomation or respiratory compromise; contact Poisons Information Helpline: 0861 555 777

protocols.notes_updates: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) - Total range 3-15 | Record best motor response | GCS Eye Opening: 4=Opening spontaneously, 3=Opening to verbal command, 2=Opening to pressure, 1=None | GCS Verbal Response: 5=Coherent speech, 4=Confused disorientated, 3=Words only, 2=Sounds only, 1=None | GCS Motor Response: 6=Obeys commands, 5=Localises painful stimulus, 4=Withdraws from painful stimulus, 3=Flexion to painful stimulus, 2=Extension to painful stimulus, 1=None | GCS <13 on initial assessment OR <15 at 2 hours after injury indicates need for CT | CT Imaging Criteria - Absolute indications: GCS <13 on initial assessment OR <15 at 2 hours after injury | Suspected open/depressed skull fracture/base of skull fracture/facial fractures | Post-traumatic seizure | Two or more episodes of vomiting since head injury | Intubated | Focal neurological deficit | Anticoagulant patients (Warfarin/DOAC): Perform non-contrast CT Brain and C-spine | Relative indications (if LOC or amnesia): Risk factors - Age >65, Bleeding/clotting disorder, Dangerous mechanism, >30min retrograde amnesia | If risk factors present: Perform CT Brain and C-spine | If absent: No imaging required | Dangerous mechanism: Pedestrian/cyclist struck by vehicle, Ejection from vehicle, Fall from height >1m or 5 stairs | Base of skull fracture signs: Raccoon eyes (periorbital ecchymosis), CSF otorrhoea/rhinorrhoea, Battle sign (bruising over mastoid process), Haemotympanum | Neuroprotective measures: Head up 30-45 degrees | MAP >90mmHg unless ICH then MAP >75-80mmHg | Normocarbia PCO2 34-40mmHg (lower end of normal) | Normoxia PaO2 60-100mmHg, O2 sats target >95% | Normothermia (Temp <37.5C) | Normoglycaemia (Blood glucose target 5-10 mmol/l) | Correct electrolyte abnormalities | Monitor for seizures - Phenytoin 20mg/kg IV max rate 50mg/min loading dose in traumatic ICH | Sedation and analgesia to avoid coughing/gagging (raises ICP) | Consider Fentanyl 1-3 mcg/kg over 60 seconds, 3 minutes prior to intubation | Avoid: Neck pressure (C-spine collars, tight ETT tape, CVP in IJV) | Excessive PEEP and elevated airway pressures | Steroids in traumatic ICH (no role) | Features of raised ICP - Early signs: Headache, vomiting, altered LOC, Visual alterations, abducens nerve palsies | Late signs: Cushings triad (Hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respiration), Unilateral dilated/non-reactive pupil with decreased GCS, Fixed constricted pupils | POCUS: Optic nerve sheath diameter >5mm | Discharge criteria: Normal conscious state GCS 15/15 | Patient no longer intoxicated | Responsible person available | Head Injury Discharge Advice Sheet provided | Discharge advice - Return if: Unconsciousness, Drowsiness >1 hour, Problems understanding/speaking/confusion, Loss balance/problems walking, Weakness arms/legs, Eyesight problems, Severe headache, Vomiting, Seizures, Bleeding from ears | Expected minor symptoms: Mild headache, nausea, dizziness, irritability, concentration problems, memory problems, tiredness, lack of appetite, sleep problems - should resolve within 2 weeks | Advice: Do not stay home alone first 24 hours | Stay within reach of telephone | Plenty rest, avoid stress | No alcohol or drugs | No sleeping pills/sedatives/tranquilisers unless prescribed | No contact sport for 3 weeks without doctor approval | No driving or machinery unless completely recovered | Admission/Referral: Neurosurgery registrar HJH during working hours; After 16:00 call CMJAH registrar | Normal hours - HJH under Neurosurgery; After hours - HJH under General Surgery (Neurosurgery take-over next morning) | Admission criteria: Surgical intervention required | Abnormal CT (neuro observation/IV antibiotics/O2) | Normal CT but not meeting discharge criteria | Admission required for other indications

diagnostic_criteria.ct_absolute_indications: GCS <13 initial OR <15 at 2hrs | Open/depressed skull fracture | Post-traumatic seizure | >2 vomiting episodes | Intubated | Focal neurological deficit
diagnostic_criteria.ct_relative_risk_factors: Age >65 | Bleeding/clotting disorder | Dangerous mechanism | >30min retrograde amnesia
diagnostic_criteria.dangerous_mechanism: Pedestrian/cyclist struck by vehicle | Ejection from vehicle | Fall >1m or 5 stairs
diagnostic_criteria.base_of_skull_fracture_signs: Raccoon eyes | CSF otorrhoea/rhinorrhoea | Battle sign | Haemotympanum

clinical_features.early_signs_racied_icp: Headache, vomiting, altered LOC, visual alterations, abducens nerve palsies
clinical_features.late_signs_raised_icp: Cushings triad (HTN, bradycardia, irregular respiration), unilateral dilated pupil, fixed constricted pupils

drugs.notes: For traumatic ICH seizure prophylaxis
3 minutes prior to intubation, to blunt sympathetic response

management_steps.details: Eye opening, verbal, motor response. Record best motor response. GCS 3-15.
management_steps.caution: GCS <13 initially or <15 at 2hrs needs CT

management_steps.details: Check absolute and relative indications for CT Brain and C-spine
management_steps.caution: Anticoagulant patients need CT regardless

management_steps.details: Head up 30-45deg, MAP >90 (or >75-80 if ICH), Normocarbia PCO2 34-40, Normoxia PaO2 60-100, Temp <37.5C, Glucose 5-10 mmol/l
management_steps.caution: Avoid neck pressure, excessive PEEP, steroids

management_steps.details: Early and late signs. POCUS optic nerve sheath diameter >5mm
management_steps.caution: Cushings triad = late sign

management_steps.details: GCS 15, not intoxicated, responsible person available = discharge with advice sheet. Otherwise admit.

protocols.monitoring: GCS monitoring | MAP >90mmHg (or >75-80 if ICH) | PCO2 34-40mmHg | PaO2 60-100mmHg O2 sats >95% | Temp <37.5C | Blood glucose 5-10 mmol/l | Optic nerve sheath diameter on POCUS | Seizure monitoring
warnings: GCS <13 on initial assessment or <15 at 2 hours after injury = CT needed
Avoid steroids in traumatic ICH - no role
Avoid neck pressure from C-spine collars, tight ETT tape, CVP in IJV
Avoid excessive PEEP and elevated airway pressures
Rabies immunoglobulin is CRITICAL in head/neck/face wounds due to short incubation period
protocols.disposition: Discharge: GCS 15/15, not intoxicated, responsible person available, discharge advice sheet given | Admission: Surgical intervention needed, abnormal CT, normal CT but not meeting discharge criteria, other indications | Neurosurgery registrar HJH working hours; CMJAH after 16:00

protocols.notes_updates: EFAST (Extended Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma) | 2 accredited EFASTs required | EFAST cannot exclude retroperitoneal or hollow viscus injuries | EFAST can ONLY confirm: free fluid in abdomen, haemo-pneumothorax, pericardial fluid | Less sensitive/specific in pregnancy and paediatrics | RUSH protocol (Rapid Ultrasound for Shock and Hypotension) referenced as policy annexure | Standard RUSH views include: Heart (pericardial/subxiphoid), IVC, Lung (sliding/sign), Morrisons pouch/hepatorenal, Splenorenal, Suprapubic/pelvic, Paracolic gutters | Detailed RUSH protocol not available in extracted text

diagnostic_criteria.efast_can_confirm: Free fluid in abdomen | Haemo-pneumothorax | Pericardial fluid
diagnostic_criteria.efast_limitations: Less sensitive in pregnancy | Less sensitive in paediatrics
diagnostic_criteria.rush_views: Heart (pericardial/subxiphoid) | IVC | Lung (sliding/sign) | Morrisons pouch/hepatorenal | Splenorenal | Suprapubic/pelvic | Paracolic gutters

management_steps.details: 2 accredited EFASTs required. Assess for free fluid in abdomen, haemo/pneumothorax, pericardial fluid.
management_steps.caution: Cannot exclude retroperitoneal or hollow viscus injuries

management_steps.details: Assess heart (pericardial/subxiphoid), IVC, lung sliding, Morrisons pouch, splenorenal, suprapubic, paracolic gutters
management_steps.caution: RUSH protocol is detailed annexure - refer to full policy

protocols.monitoring: Serial EFASTs as clinically indicated | Correlation with clinical picture and other imaging
warnings: EFAST cannot exclude retroperitoneal or hollow viscus injuries
EFAST is less sensitive/specific in pregnancy and paediatrics
2 accredited EFASTs are required
Positive EFAST for free fluid only confirms presence, not source
protocols.disposition: EFAST findings guide further imaging (CT pan-scan) and surgical consultation as clinically indicated

{'low_risk': 'Non-specific pain < 6 weeks with no red flags, monoradiculopathy/sciatica - DC with simple analgesics and physio/spine OPD follow up', 'high_risk': 'Manage underlying cause, admit to appropriate subspecialty once stable'}
red_flags: New pain at extremes of age (< 20 or > 50)
Constant, non-mechanical pain, or worse lying down/at night
Immune suppression (HIV, DM, steroids, immune suppressants)
examination_concerning_features: Sensory: saddle anaesthesia, paraesthesia, sensory level
Reflexes: any abnormal reflexes, including Babinski
Sphincter dysfunction: decreased rectal tone, urinary retention/incontinence
Midline spinal tenderness, deformity, swelling, fluctuance
Systemic: wasting, fever, lymphadenopathy, other pathologic fractures

haematological: Spinal epidural haematoma - anticoagulants, recent lumbar puncture/epidural/spinal surgery

gynae: Lower back pain common in pregnancy

imaging.x_rays: Neither sensitive nor specific for back pain
imaging.mri: Back pain with new neurology, or suspicion for epidural abscess requires URGENT MRI

specific_management.epidural_abscess: Antibiotics - Consider Ceftriaxone, Cefazolin or Vancomycin

protocols.disposition: Certify death at time of BRAIN DEATH; Counsel family prior to cessation of mechanical ventilation; Consider for organ donation referral if appropriate
protocols.notes_updates: Disposition: Certify death at time of BRAIN DEATH; Counsel family prior to cessation of mechanical ventilation; Consider for organ donation referral if appropriate
prerequisites: Systolic blood pressure > 100mmHg (or MAP > 60, age appropriate paeds targets)
Significant metabolic, electrolyte, endocrine abnormality excluded
examination_criteria: Pupils fixed and non-reactive (do not have to be dilated)
Absent cough reflex on deep suctioning down ETT
No facial grimace to supra-orbital pain
Absence of motor response in all limbs (spinal reflexes may persist)
Absent oculovestibular (cold caloric) reflex - visible intact eardrum, HOB 30°, 50ml ice-cold water flushed into canal, observe for 60 seconds, eye movement should be absent

apnoea_test.performers: Two doctors should perform a single apnoea test together
apnoea_test.prerequisites: Only proceed if all other brainstem reflexes are absent and patient is haemodynamically stable (may be on inotropes)
steps: Disconnect from mechanical ventilation and supply continuous O2 via T-piece
No spontaneous respiratory effort once CO2 has risen > 60 and pH < 7.3 is compatible with brain death
Halt testing if patient becomes unstable or spontaneous respirations are witnessed

findings_incompatible_with_brain_death: True extensor or flexor motor response
protocols.requirements: Testing can be done by 2 doctors simultaneously, one of whom must have more than 5 years experience. No doctor may be involved with the transplant team.

protocols.notes_updates: Burn Depth Classification | First degree (Superficial/Epidermal): Red, painful, dry, no blisters. Healing: 3-6 days without scarring | Second degree partial (Superficial dermal): Blisters, moist, very painful, pink/red, blanches. Healing: 7-21 days, may have pigment changes | Second degree deep (Deep dermal): Moist/dry, mottled, less painful, does not blanch. Healing: >21 days, scarring likely, may need grafting | Third degree (Full thickness): Dry, leathery, charred, white/tan/brown, painless (nerve destruction). Does not heal spontaneously, requires grafting | Fourth degree (Full thickness + fascia/muscle/bone): Charred, exposed bone/tendon. Life/limb threatening | TBSA Calculation: Lund and Browder Chart or E-burn app (most accurate, accounts for age-related changes). Rule of Nines for rapid adult estimation | Modified Brooke Formula for fluid resuscitation: 2ml x %burn x weight(kg) | Administration: Half fluid in first 8 hours, half in following 16 hours | Fluid type: Ringers Lactate/Balsol | Indications: Adults TBSA >20%, Children >15% | Fluid goals: Urine output 1-2mls/kg/hr | Lactate clearance within 2 hours | CRT <3 seconds | Smaller TBSA = normal maintenance fluids/oral intake UNLESS metabolic acidosis present | Burn Severity - Minor: Partial thickness <10%, no special areas involved | Minor management: Analgesia Morphine 0.1mg/kg IVI or Fentanyl 1mcg/kg IVI + Paracetamol | Dressings: Jelonet and gauze, Chloromex ointment for face (no dressing) | Review SOPD/Local Clinic | Moderate: Partial thickness 10-14%, smaller TBSA + unstable patient, major special area involvement, deep burns <10% | Moderate management: Paracetamol/NSAIDS + Morphine 0.1mg/kg or Fentanyl 1mcg/kg IVI or Ketamine 1-2mg/kg IVI or 2-4mg/kg IMI | IV line and maintenance fluids | Venous blood gas | Catheter only for perineal/genital burns | Dressings: Jelonet and gauze | Discuss with surgeon | Major: Partial thickness >15% adults (>10% paeds), deep burns >10%, inhalation injury, electrical burns | Major management: Secure airway | IV access and fluid resuscitation per Modified Brooke | Remember maintenance fluids for children | Urinary catheter and NGT for all patients, early NGT feeds | Analgesia: Paracetamol/NSAIDS + Morphine 0.1mg/kg or Fentanyl 1mcg/kg IVI or Ketamine 1-2mg/kg IVI or Ketamine 2-4mg/kg IM | Consider analgesia + sedation infusion | Dressings: Jelonet and gauze | Discuss with surgeon ASAP | Management Priorities: Airway/Breathing/Circulation | Burns <3 hours: cool running water 20 minutes | Dry patient and keep warm | Burn wound cleaning and assessment | Procedural sedation | All blisters and debris must be removed | Adult: wash burn and surrounding area with sterile water/saline | Child: wash whole patient with sterile water/saline | Shave head in child with facial burns | Evaluate depth and TBSA using Lund and Browder or E-burn app | Call surgeon/transfer to burns unit (CHBAH Burns/CMJAH 163) | Temperature and glucose monitoring | Analgesia | Consider TRAUMA and TOX as co-pathology | 3-hour burn bundle: Patient stabilised, fluid therapy commenced and monitored, analgesia, wounds cleaned and dressed, surgeons informed, escharotomy if needed | Special areas: Face, Hands, Feet, Genitalia/perineum, Major joints | Escharotomy: Indicated for circumferential burn with vascular compromise (pain, paraesthesia, decreased pulse, pale limb) | Ideally by surgeon in theatre | Incisions along axial lines, medial and lateral of limbs and torso | 1-2cm through into normal tissues above and below burn | Cut deep enough to view subcutaneous tissue - may need to breach fascia | Maintain haemostasis with pressure | Avoid: Ulnar nerve posterior to medial epicondyle | Common peroneal nerve at head of fibula | Posterior tibial artery and nerve posterior to medial malleolus | Hands: Cutaneous incisions strictly mid-axial lateral or medial | Never cut palmar aspects or tips of fingers | Inhalation Injury - Suspect if: Respiratory distress, stridor, accessory muscle use, hoarseness | Blisters/oedema/charring of oropharynx | Deep burns to lower face/neck | Decreased GCS | Carbonaceous sputum | RR >30, Sats <90% | ABG: PaO2 <60, CO >10%, PF ratio <200 | Isolated nasal hair singeing/facial skin burns is NOT indication for intubation | Inhalation management: Intubate early, plan for difficult airway | LMA unlikely to be useful, plan direct conversion to surgical airway | Ventilate with 100% O2 until CO toxicity excluded | Consider inhaled bronchodilators, mucolytic agents (NAC), early chest physio, therapeutic bronchoscopy (ETT >7.5), regular suctioning | Carbon Monoxide - Suspect if: Indoor/enclosed space + fire/generator/gas stove | Vehicle exhaust fumes | Altered mental status, nausea, headache, confusion, ataxia, dizziness, seizures | O2 saturation gap (high sats, low PaO2) | CO management: 100% O2, check carboxyhaemoglobin on co-oximetry | Electrical Injuries - Household low voltage (240V AC): Superficial burns, rhabdo unlikely, dysrhythmias rare | Industrial high voltage (>1000V up to 765kV): Deep burns, tetany, rhabdomyolysis, cardiac dysrhythmias. Cable theft = high voltage | Lightning (up to 1 billion V DC): Variable burns, ferning pattern/Lichtenberg figures (pathognomonic, fades 24-36hrs) | Electrical management: Monitor cardiac dysrhythmias (most resolve spontaneously) | Monitor rhabdomyolysis, AKI, compartment syndrome | May need MORE fluids than TBSA estimation - titrate to UO >2ml/kg/hr | Low voltage, no chest pain, no syncope, normal ECG: early DC | High voltage: cardiac monitoring 6-8 hrs | Palliative care: >50% TBSA - involve ED consultant and surgeon early, senior consultant decision ONLY | Palliative management: IV access for morphine and midazolam infusion, no investigations, comfort care, counsel family, offer psychology referral

diagnostic_criteria.depth_first_degree: Red, painful, dry, no blisters. Healing 3-6 days without scarring
diagnostic_criteria.depth_second_partial: Blisters, moist, very painful, pink/red, blanches. Healing 7-21 days
diagnostic_criteria.depth_second_deep: Moist/dry, mottled, less painful, does not blanch. Healing >21 days, scarring likely
diagnostic_criteria.depth_third_degree: Dry, leathery, charred, white/tan/brown, painless. Requires grafting
diagnostic_criteria.tbsa_method: Lund and Browder Chart or E-burn app (most accurate). Rule of Nines for rapid adult estimation
diagnostic_criteria.fluid_administration: Half in first 8 hours, half in next 16 hours
diagnostic_criteria.moderate_criteria: Partial thickness 10-14%, smaller TBSA + unstable, major special areas, deep burns <10%
diagnostic_criteria.major_criteria: Partial thickness >15% adults (>10% paeds), deep burns >10%, inhalation injury, electrical burns
diagnostic_criteria.special_areas: Face, Hands, Feet, Genitalia/perineum, Major joints
diagnostic_criteria.inhalation_suspect_if: Respiratory distress, stridor, hoarseness, oropharynx blisters/charring, deep face/neck burns, decreased GCS, carbonaceous sputum, RR>30, Sats<90%, PaO2<60, CO>10%, PF<200
diagnostic_criteria.co_suspect_if: Indoor fire/generator/gas stove, vehicle exhaust, altered mental status, O2 saturation gap

clinical_features.second_partial: Blisters, moist, very painful, pink/red, blanches
clinical_features.second_deep: Moist/dry, mottled, less painful, does not blanch
clinical_features.inhalation_signs: Respiratory distress, stridor, hoarseness, oropharyngeal blisters, carbonaceous sputum, RR>30, Sats<90%
clinical_features.co_toxicity_signs: Altered mental status, nausea, headache, confusion, ataxia, dizziness, seizures, O2 saturation gap

classification.moderate_burns: Partial thickness 10-14%, smaller TBSA + unstable, major special area involvement, deep burns <10%
classification.major_burns: Partial thickness >15% adults (>10% paeds), deep burns >10%, inhalation injury, electrical burns

management_steps.details: Remove all blisters and debris. Adult: wash burn with sterile water/saline. Child: wash whole patient. Shave head if facial burns in child.
Use Lund and Browder Chart or E-burn app. Classify as minor, moderate, or major.
Modified Brooke: 2ml x %burn x weight(kg). Half in 8hrs, half in 16hrs. RL/Balsol.
management_steps.caution: Adults >20% TBSA, Children >15% TBSA

Jelonet and gauze. Chloromex for face (no dressing).
Inhalation injury, CO toxicity, electrical injury, escharotomy if needed

protocols.monitoring: Urine output 1-2mls/kg/hr | Lactate clearance within 2 hours | CRT <3 seconds | Temperature | Blood glucose | Fluid resuscitation volumes and response
warnings: Isolated nasal hair singeing/facial skin burns is NOT an indication for intubation
Cable theft = high voltage electrical injury
>50% TBSA - palliative care consideration, senior consultant decision ONLY
Avoid nerve blocks/regional anaesthesia if compartment syndrome suspected
Never administer undiluted phenylephrine

protocols.notes_updates: NEXUS Criteria - If ALL criteria met, NO indication for C-spine imaging | NEXUS criteria: GCS 15 and stable vitals | No focal neurological deficit | No altered LOC | No intoxication | No midline spinal tenderness | No distracting injury | NEXUS LESS RELIABLE IF AGE >65 | Canadian C-spine Rule - Stepwise decision tool | Not applicable if: Non-trauma pain | GCS <15 | Unstable vitals | Age <16 | Acute paralysis | Known vertebral disease | Previous C-spine surgery | Pregnant | High-risk factors (imaging indicated): Age >65 | Focal peripheral neurological deficit or paraesthesias | Dangerous mechanism: Pedestrian or cyclist struck by vehicle | MVA with ejection/high speed/roll-over | Fall from height >1m or 5 stairs | Axial load to head (e.g. diving) | Quad-bike accident | Low-risk factors: Involved in simple rear-end MVA | Comfortable in sitting position in ED | Ambulatory at any time since injury | Delayed onset of neck pain | Absence of midline neck tenderness | Range of motion test: Able to rotate neck 45 degrees left and right | CT decision: If any indication for CT Brain as per head injury protocol - Perform non-contrast CT Brain AND C-spine | NO ROLE FOR X-RAYS IF CT AVAILABLE

warnings: TIME SENSITIVE LIMB THREATENING DIAGNOSIS! DO NOT DELAY!
protocols.notes_updates: Causes: Blunt soft tissue injury, Burns (especially electrical), Casts, Contrast extravasation/high-speed fluid injectors, DVT - phlegmasia cerulea/alba dolens, Fractures (common), Haemorrhage (Haemophilia), Infections, IV/IO infusions, Skin/skeletal traction, Snake bites (NB more likely PSEUDO-compartment syndrome)

six_ps.note: Classic signs - do NOT wait for all signs, especially late signs
early_signs: Pain out of proportion to the injury
late_signs: Pulselessness (pulses usually PRESENT until very late)
six_ps.notes: Absent pulses may be due to systemic hypotension, arterial occlusion or vascular injury. Do not delay until late signs appear.

pressure_thresholds.measurement: Serial or continuous measurements preferred. Can use A-line set.

management.urgency: TIME SENSITIVE LIMB THREATENING DIAGNOSIS! DO NOT DELAY!
steps: Do not delay surgery for imaging e.g. CT angio UNLESS truly necessary
Reduce fractures but avoid traction/tight casts
Place limb at the level of the heart
Liberal analgesia - avoid nerve blocks/regional anaesthesia!
Consider concomitant rhabdomyolysis and crush syndrome

causes: Snake bites (NB more likely PSEUDO-compartment syndrome)

general_principles: Reduce fractures (avoid traction/tight casts in compartment syndrome risk)
Regain length and alignment of involved bone

protocols.notes_updates: Primary Survey: CABC (ATLS adapted) | C - Catastrophic haemorrhage control | A - Airway with cervical spine protection | B - Breathing and ventilation | C - Circulation with haemorrhage control | D - Disability (neurological status) | E - Exposure/environment control | Secondary Survey: DEFG - Detailed examination after primary survey stabilised | General Approach: Triage to RESUS AREA even if initially appears well | Keep in headblocks until C-spine clear - No hard C-spine collars | Remove from hard backboard ASAP to avoid pressure injuries | Fully undress and log-roll ALL potential polytrauma | 2 large-bore IV lines (green, grey, orange), or IO/CVP if difficult access | Document head to toe clinical exam, log-roll findings, extremity pulses, neuro exam, compartment checks, core temp | Consider rectal/PV exam | Urine catheter, BHCG in all females of childbearing age | Warm and dry, monitor for hypothermia | Analgesia, ATT +/- antibiotics +/- Tranexamic acid if indicated | Minimum 1 blood gas, ideally serial gases to trend pH/lactate/Hb | Consider Calcium supplementation | Refractory shock: Consider spinal cord injury, occult bleeding, anaphylaxis | Bind pelvis, bilateral finger thoracostomies, additional blood products, damage control surgery, resuscitative thoracotomy | Discuss all polytrauma and ventilated cases with Surgery ASAP | Fluid considerations: NO COLLOIDS (Voluven, Gelofusin) | Avoid large crystalloid volumes - give blood products | Consider massive transfusion protocol: shock index >1, major mechanism, patient factors, clinical gestalt | If trauma patient requires adrenaline infusion, most likely need more blood products/theatre | Exception: Spinal cord injury with neurogenic shock (consider phenylephrine infusion) | Imaging: LODOX or CXR + Pelvis XR + extremities per clinical findings | 2 accredited EFASTs | Pan-scan: injuries to >1 anatomical region, dangerous mechanism + decreased GCS, sensory fallout, unexplained rising lactate/dropping Hb/persistent haemodynamic instability | Head and Neck CT: see respective protocols. All trauma Head CT MUST include C-spine CT and facial views | NO Skull or C-spine X-rays in ED - CT is gold standard | Extremity X-rays: joint above and below injury | Emergency haemorrhage control: Open wounds - Direct pressure, haemostatic sutures, catheter balloon tamponade, tourniquet | Fractures - Pelvic sheet binder, TRAC-3 splint, reduce and backslab | Chest/abdo - Resuscitative thoracotomy (check indications) | Dangerous mechanisms: All PVAs (pedestrian vehicle accidents) | MBAs unless extremely trivial (e.g. stationary bike fell over) | MVAs: high-velocity, unrestrained, roll-over, heavy vehicle collisions, ejection, T-bone, head-on, steering wheel impact | Assault: Mob, trunk GSW, multiple trunk stabs, screwdriver stabs | Massive Transfusion: Definition - Whole blood volume (~70ml/kg) in 24 hours, or 50% in 4 hours | Indications: Shock index >1, major mechanism, patient factors, clinical gestalt | Ratios: PRC:FFPs:Mega-unit Platelets = 4:4:1 | Packed Red Cells: Acute haemorrhage based on clinical features, lactate, vital signs, Hb <10 | 1 unit raises Hb by 1.5, Hct by 3% | Transfuse PRC and FFP 1:1 if >1 unit PRC required | Fresh Frozen Plasma: 15-20ml/kg | Acute haemorrhage, warfarin overdose INR >1.5 and active bleeding | Platelets: 10ml/kg | Plt <10 (consider even if not bleeding), Plt <50 with bleeding/procedure, platelet dysfunction with active bleeding, massive transfusion | Freeze-Dried Plasma: Ward stock, room temp. 1 unit FDP = 1 unit FFP. For life-threatening bleeding until FFP available | Tranexamic acid: 1g IV if indicated | Consider calcium supplementation | Massive transfusion complications: ABO mismatch and haemolysis | Anaphylaxis | TRALI | TACO | Hypothermia | Electrolyte abnormalities (low Ca/Mg/K)

diagnostic_criteria.dangerous_mechanisms: All PVAs | MBAs unless trivial | High-velocity MVAs, unrestrained, roll-over, ejection | Assault: mob, trunk GSW, multiple stabs

protocols.notes_updates: Risk Assessment - Reportable: Animal bites if any suspicion of rabies | Risk animals: Dogs, cats, cattle, goats, horses, sheep, pigs, jackals, wild cat species, mongoose, seals, dassies (rock hyrax), squirrel, antelope, bats | NOT rats/mice/reptiles/birds | Risk behaviour: Unprovoked attack, unusual behaviour (aggressive, salivating, wild appearing tame, uncoordinated movements) | Category of Exposure | Category 1 (Touching/feeding animal, licking of intact skin): No action if history of vaccination reliable. If history not reliable, manage as category 2 | Category 2 (Nibbling of uncovered skin, superficial scratch NO SKIN BREACH NO BLEEDING): Wound management + Rabies vaccine full course + Rabies immunoglobulin if immune-compromised | Category 3 (Bites or scratches that penetrate skin, licking of mucous membranes, ANY contact with a bat): Wound management + Rabies vaccine full course + Rabies immunoglobulin | RIG is CRITICAL in head/neck/face wounds - short incubation period | Wound Management: Flush well with soap and water | Clean with 0.05% chlorhexidine or 10% iodine | Avoid suturing and local anaesthetic agents | Antibiotics if indicated | Tetanus toxoid booster (TIG if severe wounds) | Rabies Vaccine (Verorab): 1 Amp/dose for adults and children | Schedule: Day 0, 3, 7, and 14 | Route: IMI deltoid or anterolateral thigh (avoid gluteus maximus) | If dose delayed, resume and complete regimen, do not restart | SAFE in lactation and pregnancy, same dose | Human Rabies Immunoglobulin (Human Rabigam): Dose 20 IU/kg | Infiltrate entire dose around/into wound | Dilute with saline if more volume needed for multiple wounds | Can be given up to 7 days post vaccine administration, give ASAP | Equine Rabies Immunoglobulin (Equirab): Dose 40 IU/kg | 1:150,000 risk of anaphylaxis | Administer in setting with resuscitation equipment available | Human Rabigam dosing table: 5kg=100 units/0.7ml, 10kg=200/1.3ml, 15kg=300/2.0ml, 20kg=400/2.7ml, 25kg=500/3.3ml, 30kg=600/4.0ml, 40kg=800/5.3ml, 50kg=1000/6.7ml, 60kg=1200/8.0ml, 70kg=1400/9.3ml, 80kg=1600/10.7ml, 90kg=1800/12.0ml, 100kg=2000/13.3ml | Equine Equirab dosing table: 5kg=200/1ml, 10kg=400/2ml, 15kg=600/3ml, 20kg=800/4ml, 25kg=1000/5ml, 30kg=1200/6ml, 40kg=1600/8ml, 50kg=2000/10ml, 60kg=2400/12ml, 70kg=2800/14ml, 80kg=3200/16ml, 90kg=3600/18ml, 100kg=4000/20ml | NICD 24-hour clinician hotline: 0800 212 552 | HJH ID Consultants | Source: NICD Rabies Guidelines 2021

protocols.monitoring: Monitor for signs of infection at bite site | Observe for allergic reaction if Equirab used | NICD hotline: 0800 212 552 for consultation
warnings: Rabies immunoglobulin is CRITICAL in head/neck/face wounds due to short incubation period
Equirab (equine) has 1:150,000 risk of anaphylaxis - administer with resuscitation equipment
Avoid gluteus maximus for rabies vaccine injection
If vaccine dose delayed, resume and complete regimen - do not restart
NOT rats/mice/reptiles/birds - these do not carry rabies
protocols.disposition: Complete vaccine course Days 0, 3, 7, 14 | Contact NICD 24-hour hotline 0800 212 552 or HJH ID Consultants for guidance

management_steps.details: Evaluate for systemic envenomation. Check coagulation studies.
Apply pressure bandage and splint affected limb. Keep patient still.
management_steps.caution: Do NOT apply tourniquet, do NOT wash bite site (affects venom identification)

management_steps.details: Pseudo-compartment syndrome, rhabdomyolysis, coagulopathy, neurotoxicity
management_steps.caution: Snake bites can cause pseudo-compartment syndrome - differentiate from true compartment syndrome

management_steps.details: Refer to EMCT Snakebite Emergencies pathway for detailed antivenom dosing and management
management_steps.caution: Polyvalent antivenom dosing, pressure immobilisation technique, and contraindications per external protocol

protocols.monitoring: Coagulation studies | Compartment pressure if limb swelling | Renal function (rhabdomyolysis) | Respiratory function if neurotoxic snake suspected
warnings: Snake bites can cause pseudo-compartment syndrome
Snake bite is a rare cause of rhabdomyolysis
Do NOT apply tourniquet
Do NOT wash bite site - affects venom identification
Full protocol referenced from external EMCT Snakebite Emergencies - limited detail in extracted text
protocols.disposition: Refer to EMCT Snakebite Emergencies pathway for definitive management | ICU admission if systemic envenomation | Surgical consultation if compartment syndrome suspected

protocols.notes_updates: SAFETY IS THE FIRST PRIORITY! | Contain the patient to protect staff and self | VERBAL DE-ESCALATION - SAVE THE PATIENT: Support - Let's work together | Acknowledge - I see this is hard for you | Validate - I'd probably feel the same if I were you | Emotion Naming - You seem upset/angry/sad | Verbal de-escalation is most effective for mild/moderate agitation | Do not attempt to negotiate with a completely incoherent patient | AGITATION SEVERITY: MILD: Agitated but cooperative | MODERATE: Disruptive without danger | SEVERE: Threat to staff or self | EXTREME: High degree of agitation, Diaphoretic, tachypnoeic, hyperthermic, Unusual strength, Impervious to pain and fatigue, Unable to maintain attention, Incoherent, Severe metabolic acidosis | EXTREME AGITATION IS A LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY | PHYSICAL RESTRAINTS: Restraints must be prescribed by a doctor on Form 48 | Appropriate PPE for all staff involved | Never turn your back on the patient or become trapped in an enclosed space | At least 6 staff: 1 per limb, 1 to control the head, 1 to administer medication | NEVER restrain pts by placing pressure on the neck or covering the mouth and nose | Use 4 or 5 point medical grade restraints | Restrain in supine position with HOB 30 degrees up | Female pt: at least 1 female team member | Restraint time: 4 hours adults, 2 hours adolescents (9-17 years) | Tie restraints to bedframe, not rails | Ensure the airway is patent and maintained | Regular limb and pulse checks (30 mins) | Minimal time restrained, review regularly | Use in conjunction with chemical restraint, to avoid physical injury/rhabdomyolysis | Place an oxygen mask on the face to avoid biting/spitting | SEDATED PATIENTS MUST BE PLACED IN RECOVERY POSITION IN A MONITORED BED UNTIL THEY ARE FULLY AWAKE AND ALERT | ANY SEDATION CAN CAUSE RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION AND HYPOTENSION | RESUSCITATION FACILITIES MUST BE AVAILABLE | SPECIAL POPULATIONS: ELDERLY: avoid benzodiazepines. Consider 0.5 mg haloperidol IM or atypical antipsychotics | ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL: benzodiazepines e.g. Diazepam 10mg every 5 minutes | PREGNANCY: benzodiazepines, 1st and 2nd generation antipsychotics safe in pregnancy. Avoid valproic acid, carbamazepine, lithium. Physical restraints last option, restrain in left lateral position to avoid IVC compression | CAUSES: AGITATION IS A SYMPTOM, NOT A DIAGNOSIS - IT IS VITAL TO FIND THE UNDERLYING CAUSE URGENTLY | NEUROLOGIC - SOL, ICH, CVA, post-ictal, neurodegenerative | METABOLIC - hypoxia, hypercarbia, hyperthermia, hypo/hyperglycemia, thyrotoxicosis, nutritional deficiencies, organ failure, electrolyte abnormalities | INFECTIOUS - CNS infections, any systemic infection, NB travel hx | TOXICOLOGIC - intoxication, withdrawal, overdoses, drug interactions, steroids, antibiotics, serotonin syndrome, NMS, MH, recreational/hallucinogenic/stimulant drug use | TRAUMA - Head injury, electrical injury, sexual assault | PSYCHIATRIC - psychosis, mania, personality disorders | SITUATIONAL - pain, discomfort, emotional trauma, frustration due to poor communication, waiting times, interpersonal conflict | FIRST 5 MINUTES: Apply monitors including capnography | 2 large-bore IV lines | Assess and treat: Hypoxia - O2 | Hyperthermia - core temperature, start cooling measures | Hypoglycaemia - HGT check, 50% dextrose | Hypovolaemia - 20ml/kg crystalloid bolus | ABC management as indicated | Intubate early if airway is threatened/likely to become compromised | NEXT 10 MINUTES: Blood gas - correct electrolytes, acidosis | Head to toe examination - Toxidromes, trauma, track marks | Systemic and neurological exam | ECG - toxicology features, ischaemia, arrhythmias, QT interval | Urine for dipstix, BetaHCG and tox screen | Special investigations: bloods, CXR, CTB, LP as appropriate | POCUS as indicated - fluid status, diagnostic aid | Comprehensive history: co-morbidities, psychiatric conditions, drug use, recent illness/activities/trauma, previous episodes, baseline functional level. NB History from a reliable collateral source | NEXT FEW HOURS: Continue monitoring level of agitation and consciousness | Monitor and de-escalate restraints as appropriate | Follow up all investigations and formulate a working diagnosis | Start definitive management for underlying pathology | Consult appropriate inpatient services e.g. Psychiatry, Neurology, ID, GIT etc | Monitor for agitation complications e.g. rhabdomyolysis, hyperthermia, dehydration | Other healthcare services e.g. social work, psychology as indicated

drugs.notes: Mild agitation: 1-2mg SL. Can be combined with Haloperidol in same syringe - halve the dose of each drug
Moderate: 2-5mg IM, onset 10 minutes. Severe: 5-10mg IM/IV stat. Monitor for respiratory depression. Avoid in elderly

NB SECTION 21 FORM required. Can be repeated every 30-60 minutes. Onset of action: 30 minutes. Max 3 doses. Elderly: 2.5mg. Can combine with Lorazepam in same syringe - halve dose of each drug. Can combine with Clonazepam - separate syringes, halve dose of each
Alternative if Haloperidol OS. Avoid in elderly
Severe/Extreme agitation only. Onset <5 minutes IM. Avoid in elderly
Geriatic: 1mg. Can be combined with Haloperidol - separate syringes. Halve the dose of each drug
Least recommended. Avoid PR route! Avoid in elderly. For alcohol withdrawal: 10mg every 5 minutes

management_steps.details: Contain the patient to protect staff and self. Appropriate PPE for all staff involved. Never turn your back on the patient or become trapped in an enclosed space

Support: Let's work together | Acknowledge: I see this is hard for you | Validate: I'd probably feel the same | Emotion Naming: You seem upset/angry/sad. Most effective for mild/moderate agitation. Do not attempt to negotiate with a completely incoherent patient

MILD: Agitated but cooperative | MODERATE: Disruptive without danger | SEVERE: Threat to staff or self | EXTREME: High degree of agitation, diaphoretic, tachypnoeic, hyperthermic, unusual strength, impervious to pain and fatigue, incoherent. Extreme agitation is a life-threatening emergency

Apply monitors including capnography | 2 large-bore IV lines | Assess and treat: Hypoxia (O2), Hyperthermia (core temperature, cooling), Hypoglycaemia (HGT check, 50% dextrose), Hypovolaemia (20ml/kg crystalloid bolus) | ABC management | Intubate early if airway threatened
management_steps.caution: EXTREME AGITATION IS A LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY

management_steps.details: Blood gas (correct electrolytes, acidosis) | Head-to-toe examination (toxidromes, trauma, track marks) | Systemic and neurological exam | ECG (toxicology features, ischaemia, arrhythmias, QT) | Urine dipstix, BetaHCG, tox screen | Special investigations: bloods, CXR, CTB, LP as appropriate | POCUS | Comprehensive history from patient and collateral source

MILD: Lorazepam 1-2mg SL | MODERATE: Midazolam 2-5mg IM OR Oral antipsychotic e.g. Haloperidol 5mg OR Haloperidol 2.5-10mg IM (onset up to 30 min, max 3 doses q30-60min). Alternative: Etomine 40mg IM stat | SEVERE/EXTREME: Ketamine 5mg/kg IM (<5 min onset) OR Haloperidol 10mg IM AND Midazolam 10mg IM
management_steps.caution: NB SECTION 21 FORM for Haloperidol. SEDATED PATIENTS MUST BE PLACED IN RECOVERY POSITION IN MONITORED BED UNTIL FULLY AWAKE. ANY SEDATION CAN CAUSE RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION AND HYPOTENSION. RESUSCITATION FACILITIES MUST BE AVAILABLE

management_steps.details: Prescribed by doctor on Form 48 | At least 6 staff: 1 per limb, 1 head, 1 medication | 4 or 5 point medical grade restraints | Supine position HOB 30 degrees | Restraint time: 4 hours adults, 2 hours adolescents (9-17 years) | Tie to bedframe not rails | Regular limb and pulse checks (30 mins) | Use with chemical restraint to avoid injury/rhabdomyolysis | Oxygen mask to avoid biting/spitting
management_steps.caution: NEVER restrain by placing pressure on neck or covering mouth and nose. Female pt: at least 1 female team member

management_steps.details: Continue monitoring agitation and consciousness | Monitor and de-escalate restraints | Follow up investigations and formulate working diagnosis | Start definitive management for underlying pathology | Consult appropriate services: Psychiatry, Neurology, ID, GIT etc | Monitor for complications: rhabdomyolysis, hyperthermia, dehydration | Social work, psychology as indicated

diagnostic_criteria.agitation_severity_extreme: High degree of agitation, Diaphoretic, tachypnoeic, hyperthermic, Unusual strength, Impervious to pain and fatigue, Unable to maintain attention, Incoherent, Severe metabolic acidosis - LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY

clinical_features.metabolic_causes: hypoxia, hypercarbia, hyperthermia, hypo/hyperglycemia, thyrotoxicosis, nutritional deficiencies, organ failure, electrolyte abnormalities
clinical_features.infectious_causes: CNS infections, any systemic infection, NB travel history
clinical_features.toxicologic_causes: intoxication, withdrawal, overdoses, drug interactions, steroids, antibiotics, serotonin syndrome, NMS, MH, recreational/hallucinogenic/stimulant drug use
clinical_features.trauma_causes: Head injury, electrical injury, sexual assault
clinical_features.situational_causes: pain, discomfort, emotional trauma, frustration due to poor communication, waiting times, interpersonal conflict

protocols.monitoring: Apply monitors including capnography | Regular limb and pulse checks every 30 minutes for restrained patients | Monitor level of agitation and consciousness | Monitor for complications: rhabdomyolysis, hyperthermia, dehydration | Sedated patients must be in recovery position in monitored bed until fully awake and alert
warnings: EXTREME AGITATION IS A LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY
SAFETY IS THE FIRST PRIORITY - contain the patient to protect staff and self
AGITATION IS A SYMPTOM, NOT A DIAGNOSIS - IT IS VITAL TO FIND THE UNDERLYING CAUSE URGENTLY
NEVER restrain pts by placing pressure on the neck or covering the mouth and nose
Never turn your back on the patient or become trapped in an enclosed space
SEDATED PATIENTS MUST BE PLACED IN RECOVERY POSITION IN A MONITORED BED UNTIL THEY ARE FULLY AWAKE AND ALERT
ANY SEDATION CAN CAUSE RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION AND HYPOTENSION - RESUSCITATION FACILITIES MUST BE AVAILABLE
Do not attempt to negotiate with a completely incoherent patient
Physical restraints last option in pregnancy - restrain in left lateral position to avoid IVC compression
Avoid benzodiazepines in elderly - consider 0.5mg haloperidol IM or atypical antipsychotics
NB SECTION 21 FORM required for Haloperidol
protocols.disposition: Consult appropriate inpatient services: Psychiatry, Neurology, ID, GIT etc based on underlying cause | Other healthcare services e.g. social work, psychology as indicated | Continue definitive management for underlying pathology

protocols.notes_updates: DEFINITIONS: Crush Injury: physical injury from prolonged compression. May be due to direct effect of trauma or ischaemia related to compression | Crush Syndrome: systemic manifestations resulting from crush injury, i.e. rhabdomyolysis leading to acute kidney injury, multisystem organ injury or death | CAUSES: Extensive soft tissue trauma (community assault, sjambok injury) | Snake bite (rare) | Compartment syndrome | Strenuous exercise | Hyperthermia | Electric injury | Burns (3rd and 4th degree) | Severe sepsis | Metabolic myopathies | Tetanus muscle spasms | Drug overdose (Isoniazid, Corticosteroids, Colchicine, Anti-malarials, Heroin, Zidovudine) | CLINICAL EXAMINATION: Tramline bruising | Tense, oedematous, painful muscle groups | Dark / tea-coloured urine | Circulatory shock | INVESTIGATIONS: Blood gas - metabolic acidosis (bicarb < 20, pH < 7.3, elevated lactate) | U-dipstix - haemoglobin / protein | eFAST | Bloods: Creatinine kinase, U&E, CMP | No indication for repeat CK/trending of levels | ED MANAGEMENT: ABCD approach to resuscitation | Aggressive fluid administration - Crystalloids (Balsol / Ringers Lactate) at rate 15-30 ml/kg/hr | Consider Normal saline/5%dextrose-bicarb solution if potassium is raised | Monitor urinary output - Aim for urinary output of 2-4ml/kg/hr, 200-300 mls/hr | Correct Electrolyte Abnormalities | Indication for Mannitol (expert consultation): urine output > 20mls/hr - CONTRAINDICATED IN ANURIA/SEVERE OLIGURIA | Indications for NaHCO3 8.5% (1ml/kg) (expert consultation): severe metabolic acidosis | hyperkalaemia correction | AVOID: Synthetic colloids e.g. Voluven, Gelofusin | Loop diuretics | Potassium containing fluids if K+ is raised | Nephrotoxic drugs | Indications for dialysis: Refractory acidosis | Refractory hyperkalaemia | DISPOSITION: Refer Surgery depending on cause

drugs.notes: Aggressive fluid administration. Mainstay of resuscitation
Consider if potassium is raised
Expert consultation only. Indicated if urine output > 20mls/hr. CONTRAINDICATED IN ANURIA/SEVERE OLIGURIA
Expert consultation. Indications: severe metabolic acidosis, hyperkalaemia correction

management_steps.details: Standard primary survey approach
Crystalloids (Balsol / Ringers Lactate) at 15-30 ml/kg/hr IV. Consider Normal saline/5% dextrose-bicarb solution if potassium is raised
management_steps.caution: AVOID synthetic colloids (Voluven, Gelofusin). AVOID potassium containing fluids if K+ is raised. AVOID loop diuretics. AVOID nephrotoxic drugs

management_steps.details: Aim for urinary output of 2-4ml/kg/hr (200-300 mls/hr)
Monitor and correct all electrolyte disturbances
Expert consultation required. Indicated if urine output > 20mls/hr
management_steps.caution: CONTRAINDICATED IN ANURIA/SEVERE OLIGURIA

management_steps.details: 1ml/kg IV. Expert consultation required
management_steps.caution: Indications: severe metabolic acidosis, hyperkalaemia correction

management_steps.details: Blood gas (metabolic acidosis: bicarb <20, pH <7.3, elevated lactate) | U-dipstix (haemoglobin/protein) | eFAST | Bloods: CK, U&E, CMP | No indication for repeat CK/trending
Refer Surgery depending on cause

diagnostic_criteria.crush_injury_definition: Physical injury from prolonged compression. May be due to direct effect of trauma or ischaemia related to compression
diagnostic_criteria.crush_syndrome_definition: Systemic manifestations resulting from crush injury, i.e. rhabdomyolysis leading to acute kidney injury, multisystem organ injury or death

protocols.monitoring: Monitor urinary output - aim for 2-4ml/kg/hr (200-300 mls/hr) | Monitor electrolytes | Monitor for metabolic acidosis on blood gas
warnings: CONTRAINDICATED IN ANURIA/SEVERE OLIGURIA for Mannitol
AVOID synthetic colloids e.g. Voluven, Gelofusin
AVOID loop diuretics
AVOID potassium containing fluids if K+ is raised
AVOID nephrotoxic drugs
No indication for repeat CK/trending of levels
protocols.disposition: Refer Surgery depending on cause

protocols.notes_updates: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS: Anaphylaxis and angioedema: LMA unlikely to be effective. If unable to intubate, consider early surgical airway | UGIB/Haemoptysis: Consider suction assisted laryngoscopy and decontamination (SALAD). DL may offer better views than VL due to blood on fibre-optic scope | Stridor: LMA unlikely to be effective. If unable to intubate, consider early surgical airway. Early ENT consultation | Ludwig's Angina/Retropharyngeal Abscess: Avoid airway manipulation if possible. Likely to require formal surgical airway in theatre | PREDICT THE DIFFICULT AIRWAY - LEMON: L: Look externally | E: Evaluate 3-3-2 rule (mouth opening at least 3 fingers, distance from tip of chin to hyoid bone at least 3 fingers, distance from hyoid bone to thyroid cartilage at least 2 fingers) | M: Mallampati score | O: Obesity/Obstruction | N: Neck mobility | S: Saturation (oxygen) | SETUP FOR THE PREDICTED DIFFICULT AIRWAY: Prepare all equipment and staff beforehand if time allows | Video laryngoscope prepared if not initial device | Difficult/surgical airway box on top shelf in resus | Double setup - 1 staff member to intubate, 1 for surgical airway, scrubbed and ready. All equipment laid out and ready | Mark cricothyroid membrane with permanent marker prior to induction | Consider DSI (delayed sequence intubation) and vocal cord visualization prior to muscle relaxation | OPTIMISE ALL FACTORS: Most experienced intubator | Height of bed | Patient position | Neck extension/shoulder elevation/ramping | Different laryngoscope blade/video scope | 2-handed pull technique | Consider awake or nasal intubation - experienced operator ONLY | CRICOTHYROTOMY REFERENCES FROM OTHER PROTOCOLS: From Angioedema protocol: Prepare for an EXTREMELY difficult airway (see Difficult airway & Cricothyrotomy protocols) - Avoid airway manipulation if possible | Most skilled person to attempt intubation; failed attempts may worsen compromise | Ideally intubate in theatre with awake fibre-optic laryngoscopy and surgical airway double setup | LMA unlikely to be successful, move directly to Cricothyrotomy if intubation fails | From Epiglottitis protocol: URGENT ENT consult - intubate in theatre | Prepare for emergency cricothyrotomy if pt must be intubated in ED | Fibreoptic laryngoscopy = gold standard | Avoid supraglottic devices | Dual setup for surgical airway | From Burns/Inhalation injury protocol: Intubate early, plan for difficult airway (see difficult airway & surgical airway protocols) | LMA unlikely to be useful, plan for direct conversion to surgical airway in failed intubation | ALGORITHM REFERENCE: https://aimeairway.ca/algorithms

management_steps.action: Predict the difficult airway using LEMON assessment
management_steps.details: L: Look externally | E: Evaluate 3-3-2 rule (mouth opening at least 3 fingers, chin-to-hyoid at least 3 fingers, hyoid-to-thyroid at least 2 fingers) | M: Mallampati score | O: Obesity/Obstruction | N: Neck mobility | S: Saturation (oxygen)

Prepare all equipment and staff beforehand if time allows | Video laryngoscope prepared if not initial device | Difficult/surgical airway box on top shelf in resus | Double setup: 1 staff to intubate, 1 for surgical airway scrubbed and ready | Mark cricothyroid membrane with permanent marker prior to induction | Consider DSI and vocal cord visualization prior to muscle relaxation

Most experienced intubator | Correct height of bed | Optimal patient position | Neck extension/shoulder elevation/ramping | Different laryngoscope blade/video scope | 2-handed pull technique | Consider awake or nasal intubation - experienced operator ONLY
management_steps.caution: Awake or nasal intubation: experienced operator ONLY

management_steps.details: Anaphylaxis/Angioedema: LMA unlikely to be effective, consider early surgical airway | UGIB/Haemoptysis: Consider SALAD technique, DL may be better than VL | Stridor: LMA unlikely to be effective, consider early surgical airway, early ENT consult | Ludwig's Angina/Retropharyngeal abscess: Avoid airway manipulation if possible, likely formal surgical airway in theatre
management_steps.caution: In angioedema: LMA unlikely to be successful, move directly to Cricothyrotomy if intubation fails

management_steps.details: From referenced protocols: Most skilled person to attempt intubation; failed attempts may worsen compromise | LMA unlikely to be successful in special circumstances (angioedema, inhalation injury, stridor) - move directly to cricothyrotomy | Dual setup for surgical airway should be ready | Cricothyroid membrane should be marked prior to induction
management_steps.caution: Failed intubation attempts may worsen airway compromise. Be prepared to move directly to surgical airway

management_steps.details: Confirm ETT placement | Secure ETT | Repeat BP and check all vital signs | Chest X-Ray | ABG after 30 minutes | Sedation if required | Attach to ventilator with HME Filter

diagnostic_criteria.lemon_assessment: L: Look externally | E: Evaluate 3-3-2 rule | M: Mallampati score | O: Obesity/Obstruction | N: Neck mobility | S: Saturation
diagnostic_criteria.3-3-2_rule: Mouth opening at least 3 fingers | Distance from tip of chin to hyoid bone at least 3 fingers | Distance from hyoid bone to thyroid cartilage at least 2 fingers

clinical_features.difficult_airway_predictors: LEMON: Look externally, Evaluate 3-3-2 rule, Mallampati, Obesity/Obstruction, Neck mobility, Saturation

protocols.monitoring: Capnography | Pulse oximetry | Blood pressure | All vital signs post-intubation | ABG after 30 minutes
warnings: Anaphylaxis and angioedema: LMA unlikely to be effective. If unable to intubate, consider early surgical airway
Stridor: LMA unlikely to be effective. If unable to intubate, consider early surgical airway. Early ENT consultation
Ludwig's Angina/Retropharyngeal Abscess: Avoid airway manipulation if possible. Likely to require formal surgical airway in theatre
Failed intubation attempts may worsen compromise - most skilled person should attempt
Mark cricothyroid membrane with permanent marker prior to induction
Awake or nasal intubation - experienced operator ONLY
Double setup: 1 staff member to intubate, 1 for surgical airway scrubbed and ready
protocols.disposition: Depends on underlying condition requiring airway management | Urgent ENT consultation for stridor, Ludwig's angina, retropharyngeal abscess | Ideally intubate in theatre with awake fibre-optic laryngoscopy and surgical airway double setup for angioedema

classification.name: Uncomplicated Hyperglycaemia
classification.criteria: HGT >11 mmol/L, no ketones, no nausea/vomiting, no dehydration, normal mental state, no acidosis

classification.name: Complicated Hyperglycaemia (DKA/HHS)
classification.criteria: Any of: urine ketones positive, nausea/vomiting, dehydration, altered mental state, acidotic breathing, pH < 7.35, HCO3 < 20, BE < -4

monitoring.target: < 15 mmol/L before switching to dextrose-containing fluids

warnings: Always assess for DKA/HHS criteria in hyperglycaemic patients
protocols.disposition: Uncomplicated: discharge with referral to Poly Clinic/local clinic within 3 days. Complicated: Admit to Medicine.
protocols.notes_updates:
Causes: Infection (UTI, pneumonia), Infarction, Ischaemia, Ignorance (defaulter), Intoxication, Implantation (pregnancy), Intra-abdominal (pancreatitis, cholecystitis, appendicitis), Undiagnosed Type 1 DM (age < 35 years)
Diagnostic criteria:
  Blood glucose (HGT): > 11 mmol/L (diagnostic)
  pH: < 7.35  (critical - refer to DKA protocol)
  HCO3: < 20 mmol/L (critical - refer to DKA protocol)
  Base Excess: < -4  (critical - refer to DKA protocol)
Classification:
  Uncomplicated Hyperglycaemia: HGT >11 mmol/L, no ketones, no nausea/vomiting, no dehydration, normal mental state, no acidosis
  Complicated Hyperglycaemia (DKA/HHS): Any of: urine ketones positive, nausea/vomiting, dehydration, altered mental state, acidotic breathing, pH < 7.35, HCO3 < 20, BE < -4
Disposition: Uncomplicated: discharge with referral to Poly Clinic/local clinic within 3 days. Complicated: Admit to Medicine.
Warnings: Always assess for DKA/HHS criteria in hyperglycaemic patients

workup_flowchart.step_2: Assess for DKA/HHS criteria (ketones, vomiting, dehydration, altered mental state, acidotic breathing, pH < 7.35, HCO3 < 20, BE < -4)
workup_flowchart.step_3_if_no: Manage as uncomplicated hyperglycaemia - find and treat cause, optimise diabetes medication, diabetic education, discharge with referral to follow up within 3 days
workup_flowchart.step_4_if_yes: Manage as DKA/HHS (see DKA/HHS protocol), find and treat cause, admit

classification.name: DKA
classification.criteria: HGT > 11 (often > 30), pH < 7.3, HCO3 < 18, AG > 16, osmolality < 320, ketones positive, metabolic acidosis

classification.name: HHS
classification.criteria: HGT > 30, pH > 7.3 (minimal acidosis), osmolality > 350, ketones absent/weakly positive, altered mental state common

classification.name: Euglycemic DKA
classification.criteria: DKA with near-normal glucose; risk factors: SGLT2 inhibitors, carbohydrate restriction, fasting, dehydration, alcohol, partially treated DKA

drugs.notes: Conditions: Delay until K+ > 3.5. Max: 10 U stat
Conditions: Alternative to hourly boluses
Conditions: To maintain K+ 4-5 mmol/L
Conditions: Not bolus fluids
Conditions: Use KPO4 if phosphate low
Conditions: ONLY if pH < 6.8, HCO3 < 5 (expert consultation required)
Conditions: HHS and severe DKA - prophylaxis
Conditions: Supplementation when feeding restarted

monitoring.target: If > 110, change to 1/2 saline or RHS

warnings: Delay insulin until K+ > 3.5 mmol/L
Do NOT routinely use sodium bicarbonate
Avoid intubation if possible
Do not correct too fast - risk of cerebral oedema
DKA and HHS may overlap
protocols.disposition: Admit Medicine unless surgical precipitant. ICU consult if pH < 7, shock, or CNS abnormalities.

formulas.name: Corrected Potassium
formulas.formula: For every 0.1 pH drop from 7.4, K+ drops by 0.5

formulas.name: Corrected Sodium for Glucose
formulas.formula: For every 5.5 mmol/L glucose above 5.5: Na increases by 1.6 if glucose 11-22; by 2.4 if glucose > 22

protocols.notes_updates:
Diagnostic criteria:
  Blood glucose (HGT): > 11, often > 30 mmol/L (diagnostic)
  pH: < 7.3  (diagnostic)
  HCO3: < 18 mmol/L (diagnostic)
  Anion Gap: > 16 mmol/L (diagnostic)
  Serum Osmolality: < 320 mOsm/kg (DKA (vs HHS > 350))
  Urine Ketones: Positive  (diagnostic)
  Base Excess: < -4  (diagnostic)
Classification:
  DKA: HGT > 11 (often > 30), pH < 7.3, HCO3 < 18, AG > 16, osmolality < 320, ketones positive, metabolic acidosis
  HHS: HGT > 30, pH > 7.3 (minimal acidosis), osmolality > 350, ketones absent/weakly positive, altered mental state common
  Euglycemic DKA: DKA with near-normal glucose; risk factors: SGLT2 inhibitors, carbohydrate restriction, fasting, dehydration, alcohol, partially treated DKA
Disposition: Admit Medicine unless surgical precipitant. ICU consult if pH < 7, shock, or CNS abnormalities.
Warnings: Delay insulin until K+ > 3.5 mmol/L; Do NOT routinely use sodium bicarbonate; Avoid intubation if possible; Do not correct too fast - risk of cerebral oedema; DKA and HHS may overlap
protocols.transition_to_sc_insulin: Restart maintenance regimen 1-3 hours prior to stopping DKA protocol, top up with sliding scale (in consultation with Internal Medicine). FEED the patient.

classification.name: HHS
classification.criteria: HGT > 30, osmolality > 350, minimal acidosis/normal pH, ketones absent/weakly positive, altered mental state common

classification.name: HHS-DKA Overlap
classification.criteria: May have features of both HHS and DKA

drugs.notes: Conditions: Delay until K+ > 3.5. Max: 10 U stat
Conditions: Alternative to hourly boluses
Conditions: To maintain K+ 4-5 mmol/L
Conditions: Not bolus fluids
Conditions: HHS and severe DKA - prophylaxis

monitoring.target: Monitor trends - slower correction than DKA

warnings: Slower fluid correction than DKA
Higher thromboembolism risk than DKA - anticoagulate
Do not correct too fast - risk of cerebral oedema
Altered mental state is common presentation
protocols.disposition: Admit Medicine. ICU consult if shock or CNS abnormalities.
protocols.notes_updates:
Diagnostic criteria:
  Blood glucose (HGT): > 30 mmol/L (diagnostic)
  Serum Osmolality: > 350 mOsm/kg (diagnostic)
  pH: > 7.3 (minimal acidosis)  (diagnostic - distinguishes from DKA)
  Anion Gap: Normal (12-16) mmol/L (diagnostic)
  Urine Ketones: Absent or weakly positive  (diagnostic)
Classification:
  HHS: HGT > 30, osmolality > 350, minimal acidosis/normal pH, ketones absent/weakly positive, altered mental state common
  HHS-DKA Overlap: May have features of both HHS and DKA
Disposition: Admit Medicine. ICU consult if shock or CNS abnormalities.
Warnings: Slower fluid correction than DKA; Higher thromboembolism risk than DKA - anticoagulate; Do not correct too fast - risk of cerebral oedema; Altered mental state is common presentation

Avoid succinylcholine (scoline) - risk of hyperkalaemia
