Warnings: HEAT STROKE IS A LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY; Avoid succinylcholine (scoline) - risk of hyperkalaemia; Avoid antipyretics - NSAIDs and aspirin not effective; Target temp < 39°C within 30 minutes; Often an underlying medical condition contributing to hyperthermia; Check for toxidrome features

classification.name: Stage I (Swiss)
classification.criteria: Conscious, shivering; Temperature 32-35°C
classification.management: Warm environment & clothing, warm sweet drinks & active movement

classification.name: Stage II (Swiss)
classification.criteria: Impaired consciousness, not shivering; Temperature 28-32°C
classification.management: Cardiac monitoring, careful movement, full-body insulation, active external rewarming & minimally invasive rewarming

classification.name: Stage III (Swiss)
classification.criteria: Unconscious, not shivering, vital signs present; Temperature 24-28°C
classification.management: Stage II + airway management + central active warming (warm IV fluids 42°C, body cavity lavage 40°C, renal replacement therapy, ECMO/bypass)

classification.name: Stage IV (Swiss)
classification.criteria: Vital signs absent; Temperature < 24°C
classification.management: All of above + CPR, adrenaline, defibrillation, ECMO as available

monitoring.target: Watch for Osborne J waves, AF, bradyarrhythmias
Confirm cardiac activity before declaring death

warnings: TREAT HYPOTHERMIA AGGRESSIVELY - ALL OTHER INTERVENTIONS ARE LIKELY TO FAIL
Careful movement of patient to avoid arrhythmias
Ensure intubated before gastric lavage
Warm IV fluids only give if need fluids - prevents cooling rather than promotes warming
ALWAYS LOOK FOR TRAUMA IN DEPRESSED CONSCIOUSNESS
Never declare death in hypothermic patient without warming to 32°C first
protocols.disposition: Stage I: Warm environment. Stage II-III: Admit to high care/ICU. Stage IV: Aggressive resuscitation with ECMO if available.
protocols.notes_updates:
Causes: At risk population - elderly and infants, Hypoglycaemia, Sepsis, Intoxication, Hypothyroidism, Adrenal insufficiency, Malnutrition, Trauma, Elderly with co-morbidities, Burns, Shock, Environment, Iatrogenic (be cautious with fluid and blood products)
Diagnostic criteria:
  Core temperature (non-trauma): ≤ 35 °C (diagnostic)
  Core temperature (trauma): ≤ 36 °C (diagnostic)
Classification:
  Stage I (Swiss): Conscious, shivering; Temperature 32-35°C
  Stage II (Swiss): Impaired consciousness, not shivering; Temperature 28-32°C
  Stage III (Swiss): Unconscious, not shivering, vital signs present; Temperature 24-28°C
  Stage IV (Swiss): Vital signs absent; Temperature < 24°C
Disposition: Stage I: Warm environment. Stage II-III: Admit to high care/ICU. Stage IV: Aggressive resuscitation with ECMO if available.
Warnings: TREAT HYPOTHERMIA AGGRESSIVELY - ALL OTHER INTERVENTIONS ARE LIKELY TO FAIL; Careful movement of patient to avoid arrhythmias; Ensure intubated before gastric lavage; Warm IV fluids only give if need fluids - prevents cooling rather than promotes warming; ALWAYS LOOK FOR TRAUMA IN DEPRESSED CONSCIOUSNESS; Never declare death in hypothermic patient without warming to 32°C first

FEATURES OF RAISED ICP/IMPENDING HERNIATION: Headache, vomiting, altered LOC, visual alterations, abducens nerve palsies (lateral gaze palsy) | Cushing's triad: Hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respiration - late manifestation | Unilateral dilated/non-reactive pupil with decreased GCS, or fixed constricted pupils | POCUS: Optic nerve sheath diameter >5mm | NEUROPROTECTIVE MEASURES: Head up 30-45 degrees | MAP >90mmHg unless ICH then MAP >75-80mmHg | Normocarbia (PCO2 34-40mmHg) - lower end of normal | Normoxia (PaO2 60-100mmHg) - avoid hypoxia, O2 sats target >95% | Normothermia (Temp <37.5) | Normoglycaemia (Blood glucose target 5-10 mmol/l) | Correct any electrolyte abnormalities | Monitor for seizures - Phenytoin 20mg/kg IV, max rate 50mg/min loading dose in traumatic ICH | Sedation and analgesia to avoid coughing - coughing/gagging raises ICP | Consider Fentanyl 1-3 mcg/kg over 60 seconds, 3 minutes prior to intubation as premed to blunt intubation response | Avoid neck pressure - C-spine collars, tight ETT tape, CVP in IJV | Avoid excessive PEEP and elevated airway pressures | Avoid raised thoracic/abdominal compartment pressures | No role for steroids in traumatic ICH | Consider Dexamethasone 6-12mg IV tds in tumours/bacterial meningitis/other vasogenic oedema | OSMOTHERAPY OPTIONS: | 1. Hypertonic sodium bicarbonate: 50ml of 1mEq/ml (same tonicity as 6% saline), 100-150ml as emergent therapy (adult) | 2. Hypertonic saline: 3ml/kg 5% OR 5ml/kg 3%, central line not required, monitor sodium avoid hypernatraemia >155, avoid NAGMA/hyperchloraemia (convert to hypertonic bicarbonate therapy), avoid increasing Na by more than 6-8 mmol/day, less adverse effects than mannitol, bolus preferred over continuous infusion | 3. Mannitol (less preferred): 1.5-2g/kg over 30-60 minutes, 20% solution (100g in 500ml), large peripheral/central line, complications: nephrotoxic, may induce diuresis, decrease cerebral perfusion, hypotension, electrolyte derangement, rebound raised ICP as mannitol crosses BBB | NEUROSURGICAL INTERVENTIONS: Tumour resection | Haemorrhage evacuation/Burr holes | External ventricular drain (EVD) placement | Shunt placement | Decompressive hemicraniectomy | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND SPECIFIC MANAGEMENT: | Communicating hydrocephalus (meningitis often cryptococcal, SAH, sagittal sinus thrombosis): LP/IVD with opening pressures, remove excess CSF | Non-communicating hydrocephalus (ventricular obstruction due to haemorrhage/inflammation/mass often TB): Interventricular drain (IVD), DO NOT LP | Vasogenic oedema (tumour, abscess, encephalitis, demyelinating disease, trauma, cerebral venous thrombosis, PRES): May be steroid responsive, efficacy of osmotherapy may be limited due to BBB disruption | Cytotoxic oedema (ischaemic stroke, HIE, DAI, acute hepatic failure, prolonged seizures): Treat underlying cause, NOT responsive to steroids | Interstitial oedema (obstructive hydrocephalus forces CSF into white matter): Treat underlying cause, EVD | Osmotic oedema (acute hyponatraemia, rebound oedema from rapid weaning of hyperosmolar therapy, dialysis disequilibrium syndrome, DKA paediatric): Treat underlying cause, correct electrolytes | Venous outflow obstruction (cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, jugular vein compression/thrombus, external neck compression/masses, SVC syndrome, thoracic/abdominal compartment syndrome): Treat underlying cause | Intracranial haemorrhage (SAH, SDH, EDH, intraparenchymal, intraventricular): Surgical evacuation based on location/type | Tension pneumocephalus (trauma): 100% oxygen, urgent neurosurgical consult

drugs.notes: For seizure prophylaxis in traumatic ICH; monitor for seizures in all raised ICP patients
Premedication 3 minutes prior to intubation to blunt intubation response; sedation and analgesia to avoid coughing which raises ICP
For tumours, bacterial meningitis, other vasogenic oedema; NO role in traumatic ICH
Same tonicity as 6% saline; for emergent osmotherapy
Central line not required; monitor sodium avoid hypernatraemia >155; avoid increasing Na by >6-8 mmol/day; bolus preferred over continuous infusion; less adverse effects than mannitol

20% solution (100g in 500ml); less preferred than hypertonic saline; requires large peripheral/central line; complications: nephrotoxic, diuresis, decreased cerebral perfusion, hypotension, electrolyte derangement, rebound raised ICP as mannitol crosses BBB
For hepatic encephalopathy; reduces ammonia thus reducing cerebral oedema
Short-acting benzodiazepine for seizure termination in raised ICP/hepatic encephalopathy
To prevent raised intracranial pressure in hepatic encephalopathy

management_steps.action: Recognise features of raised ICP/impending herniation
management_steps.details: Headache, vomiting, altered LOC, visual alterations, abducens nerve palsies, Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respiration), unilateral dilated/non-reactive pupil, PONS >5mm
management_steps.caution: Cushing's triad is a late manifestation

management_steps.details: Head up 30-45 degrees, maintain MAP >90mmHg (unless ICH then >75-80mmHg), normocarbia PCO2 34-40mmHg, normoxia PaO2 60-100mmHg (O2 sats >95%), normothermia <37.5, normoglycaemia 5-10mmol/l, correct electrolytes
management_steps.caution: Avoid neck pressure, excessive PEEP, raised thoracic/abdominal pressures

management_steps.details: Adequate sedation and analgesia to avoid coughing/gagging which raises ICP; consider Fentanyl 1-3mcg/kg over 60 seconds 3 minutes prior to intubation
Monitor for seizures; Phenytoin 20mg/kg IV max 50mg/min loading dose in traumatic ICH; Midazolam 0.1mg/kg for acute seizure termination

Communicating hydrocephalus, non-communicating hydrocephalus, vasogenic oedema, cytotoxic oedema, interstitial oedema, osmotic oedema, venous outflow obstruction, intracranial haemorrhage, tension pneumocephalus
management_steps.caution: Management varies by cause - e.g. DO NOT LP in non-communicating hydrocephalus

management_steps.details: Preferred: Hypertonic saline 3ml/kg 5% OR 5ml/kg 3%; Alternative: Mannitol 1.5-2g/kg over 30-60 minutes; Emergent: Hypertonic sodium bicarbonate 100-150ml
management_steps.caution: Monitor sodium avoid hypernatraemia >155; avoid increasing Na >6-8 mmol/day; mannitol less preferred due to more complications

management_steps.details: Dexamethasone 6-12mg IV TDS for tumours, bacterial meningitis, other vasogenic oedema
management_steps.caution: NO role for steroids in traumatic ICH; NOT responsive in cytotoxic oedema

management_steps.details: For tumour resection, haemorrhage evacuation/burr holes, EVD placement, shunt placement, decompressive hemicraniectomy
management_steps.caution: Urgent neurosurgical consult for tension pneumocephalus and intracranial haemorrhage

clinical_features_of_raised_icp: Headache, vomiting, altered LOC, visual alterations
Cushing's triad: Hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respiration (late)
Unilateral dilated/non-reactive pupil with decreased GCS

signs_of_impending_herniation: Unilateral dilated/non-reactive pupil with decreased GCS

causes_by_category.communicating_hydrocephalus: Meningitis (often cryptococcal), SAH, sagittal sinus thrombosis
causes_by_category.non_communicating_hydrocephalus: Ventricular system obstruction due to haemorrhage/inflammation/mass lesions (often TB)
causes_by_category.vasogenic_oedema: Tumour, abscess, encephalitis, demyelinating disease, trauma, cerebral venous thrombosis, PRES
causes_by_category.cytotoxic_oedema: Ischaemic stroke, HIE, DAI, acute hepatic failure, prolonged seizures
causes_by_category.interstitial_oedema: Obstructive hydrocephalus forces CSF into white matter
causes_by_category.osmotic_oedema: Acute hyponatraemia, rebound oedema from rapid weaning of hyperosmolar therapy, dialysis disequilibrium syndrome, DKA (paediatric)
causes_by_category.venous_outflow_obstruction: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, jugular vein compression/thrombus, external neck compression/masses, SVC syndrome, thoracic/abdominal compartment syndrome

protocols.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure (MAP >90mmHg or >75-80mmHg if ICH), O2 sats (>95%), PCO2 (34-40mmHg), temperature (<37.5), blood glucose (5-10mmol/l), electrolytes, sodium (avoid hypernatraemia >155), serum osmolarity (<320 mOsm/L if on mannitol), GCS, pupil response, seizure activity
warnings: Cushing's triad is a late manifestation of raised ICP
No role for steroids in traumatic ICH
DO NOT perform LP in non-communicating hydrocephalus
Mannitol crosses BBB and can cause rebound raised ICP
Avoid neck pressure - C-spine collars, tight ETT tape, CVP in IJV raise ICP
Avoid excessive PEEP and elevated airway pressures
Avoid cough/gagging - raises ICP
Cytotoxic oedema NOT responsive to steroids
Vasogenic oedema may have limited response to osmotherapy due to BBB disruption
protocols.disposition: Urgent neurosurgical consultation for all patients with raised ICP/impending herniation; ICU admission for ongoing monitoring and management; neurosurgical interventions include tumour resection, haemorrhage evacuation/burr holes, EVD placement, shunt placement, decompressive hemicraniectomy

management_steps.details: Suction, tubes, oxygen, pharmacology, IV access, confirmatory devices, bougie, alternative devices, rescue devices, surgical airway
management_steps.caution: Ensure all equipment functional before starting

management_steps.details: Mallampati score, Measurements (3-3-1 or 3-3-2 rule), Atlanto-occipital extension, Pathology assessment
management_steps.caution: Predict difficult airway using LEMON criteria

management_steps.details: Stretcher height appropriate, Patient at end of bed, Sniff position, Special positioning for obese (ramping) and paediatric patients
management_steps.caution: Obese patients require ramping/shoulder elevation

management_steps.details: 100% oxygen for 3-5 minutes via FMO2/BVM/NIV
management_steps.caution: Critically ill patients may need longer pre-oxygenation or NIV

protocols.item: Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy Algorithm (In-Hospital ACLS)

management_steps.details: High-quality CPR, defibrillation when indicated. Assemble maternal cardiac arrest team.
A-Anesthetic complications, B-Bleeding, C-Cardiovascular, D-Drugs, E-Embolic, F-Fever, G-General non-obstetric causes (H's and T's), H-Hypertension
Relief of aortocaval compression with lateral uterine displacement. High-quality CPR is priority.
Difficult airway is common in pregnancy. Use most experienced provider. Endotracheal intubation or supraglottic airway. Waveform capnography.
management_steps.caution: In pregnancy, a difficult airway is common. Use the most experienced provider.

management_steps.details: Administer medications as per standard ACLS
management_steps.caution: If receiving IV magnesium, stop and give calcium chloride/gluconate

management_steps.details: If no ROSC within 5 minutes of resuscitation, perform perimortem cesarean delivery. Ideally perform in 5 minutes depending on provider resources and skill sets.
management_steps.caution: Goal of perimortem cesarean delivery is to improve maternal and fetal outcomes

protocols.item: Blood and Blood Products including Freeze-Dried Plasma

Only necessary if >2 units to be transfused (>50ml/kg/hr adults)
