warnings: {'reaction': 'ABO mismatch and haemolysis', 'features': 'Fever, tachycardia, chest pain, shock, DIC', 'management': 'STOP TRANSFUSION immediately, supportive care'}
{'reaction': 'Rh mismatch', 'features': '', 'management': 'Rh negative females of childbearing age MUST receive Rh negative products'}
{'reaction': 'Anaphylaxis', 'features': 'See anaphylaxis protocol', 'management': 'STOP transfusion, manage per anaphylaxis protocol'}

{'reaction': 'TRALI (Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury)', 'features': 'ARDS-type non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, shock. More common with FFP.', 'management': 'STOP transfusion, supportive care'}

{'reaction': 'TACO (Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload)', 'features': 'Fluid overload, may mimic TRALI. More common in chronic anaemia/poor circulatory compliance.', 'management': 'STOP transfusion, diuretics, supportive care'}
{'reaction': 'Hypothermia', 'features': 'More common with massive transfusion of cooled products', 'management': 'Blood warming'}
{'reaction': 'Electrolyte abnormalities', 'features': 'Low Calcium/Magnesium/Potassium', 'management': 'Monitor and replace'}

protocols.notes_updates: SUB-PROTOCOL: Tumour Lysis Syndrome | Pathophysiology: Massive tumour cell lysis - release of tumour cell contents into circulation causing electrolyte derangement (low Ca2+, high K+, high PO4, high uric acid), renal failure, metabolic acidosis, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias | Triggers: Chemotherapy, Radiotherapy, High dose steroids, Hormonal therapy, Surgery, Rapidly growing tumour | Risk factors: Leukaemia, Lymphoma, Aggressive treatment, High tumour burden | Diagnostic criteria: Cairo-Bishop criteria | Management: Monitored bed | Aggressive IV fluids (balanced crystalloid) - aim urine output >2ml/kg/hr | If volume overloaded: Lasix | Manage electrolyte abnormalities (hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia) | Supportive treatment | Dialysis indications: oliguria, anuria, volume overload, persistent hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemia-induced symptomatic hypocalcaemia | Disposition: Urgent Oncology referral. ICU referral for severe cases | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Febrile Neutropenia | Definition: Fever + neutropenia (ANC <1000/microlitre). Fever: single oral temp >38.3C or >38C for >1 hour | Often 5-10 days after last chemotherapy | Management: Rapid work-up and empiric antibiotics | Look carefully for signs of infection but defer PR (typhlitis) | Look everywhere: skin, mucous membranes, peri-anal, indwelling lines/catheters | Consider typhlitis: necrotising enterocolitis, appendicitis mimic - CT Abdo, NGT, NPO, IV fluid | Send appropriate samples (blood cultures, urine culture, etc.) | Early antibiotics: Tazocin adequate broad spectrum | Consider fungal cover | Differential: Drug reaction, PE, other oncological emergency | Disposition: Early oncology referral | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Superior Vena Cava (SVC) Syndrome | Pathophysiology: Mechanical obstruction of SVC. Internal: clot. External: tumour/lymph node | Cancers: Lung, NHL Lymphoma | Symptoms: Dyspnoea, Facial oedema, Cough, Chest pain, Dysphagia, Distended neck veins, Facial plethora, Arm oedema | RARE and DANGEROUS: vocal cord oedema | Imaging: CXR can be normal, widened mediastinum, pleural effusion. CT gold standard | Management: Manage airway if needed (expect difficult airway) | Elevate head, supportive treatment | Steroids: Dexamethasone 8mg STAT | Consider anti-coagulation if thrombus suspected | Disposition: Oncology referral. Ultimate Rx: Chemotherapy, radiotherapy, stenting | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Malignant Spinal Cord Compression | Cancers: Breast, Prostate, Lung, Lymphoma, Multiple myeloma | Symptoms: Back pain (worse at night/lying), Bladder/bowel symptoms, Lower limb weakness, Sensory deficit (saddle anaesthesia), Acute paralysis | Imaging: X-rays can lack sensitivity. CT. Best = MRI | Management: Supportive care | Pain management | Dexamethasone 10mg STAT | Urgent Ortho Spine referral | Urgent Radiation oncology referral | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Hypercalcaemia of Malignancy | Presentation: Stones, Bones, Abdominal Moans and Psychic Groans | Symptoms - Neuro: Fatigue, muscle weakness, hyporeflexia, delirium, low GCS | GIT: N+V, constipation, ileus, PUD, pancreatitis | Cardiac: Arrhythmias | Renal: Polydipsia, polyuria, kidney stones, AKI | General: Pruritus, dehydration | Causes: Multiple myeloma, Breast cancer, Lung cancer, Kidney cancer | ECG: Osborne waves, ST elevation, shortened QTc, widened T-wave, bundle branch patterns, heart block, brady-dysrhythmias | Management: Monitored bed | Aggressive IV fluids | If overloaded: Consider Lasix bolus | Bisphosphonates (prolonged onset of action) | Disposition: Oncology referral if known, Renal referral if unknown | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Malignant Pericardial Effusion | Cancers: Lung, Breast, Oesophageal, Melanoma, Leukaemia, Lymphoma | Signs: Exertional dyspnoea, Chest pain, Tachycardia, Pulsus paradoxus | Becks triad: muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins, hypotension (only 1/3 patients) | Worried if: Tachypnoea, Tachycardia, Hypotension, Narrow pulse pressure, Altered GCS | Bedside US: Fluid around heart, RA collapse in late diastole, RV collapse in early diastole | ECG: Low amplitude QRS, electrical alternans | Management: Emergent Rx: Pericardiocentesis (Emergency ED procedure in tamponade) | US guided - Seldinger technique with CVP set. NB: DO NOT SLASH MYOCARDIUM | Elevate bed 30-45 degrees, aseptic, identify deepest pocket (parasternal/apical/subxiphoid) | Supportive: Fluid resus only if hypovolaemic | Try avoiding intubation - worsens haemodynamic status | Disposition: Oncology referral if known patient

diagnostic_criteria.tumour_lysis_cairo_bishop: Low Ca2+, high K+, high PO4, high uric acid, renal failure, metabolic acidosis
diagnostic_criteria.tumour_lysis_triggers: Chemotherapy, Radiotherapy, High dose steroids, Hormonal therapy, Surgery, Rapidly growing tumour
diagnostic_criteria.tumour_lysis_dialysis_indications: Oliguria, Anuria, Volume overload, Persistent hyperkalaemia, Hyperphosphataemia-induced symptomatic hypocalcaemia
diagnostic_criteria.febrile_neutropenia_definition: Fever + ANC <1000/microlitre. Fever: single oral temp >38.3C or >38C for >1 hour
diagnostic_criteria.svc_imaging: CXR: normal/widened mediastinum/pleural effusion. CT gold standard
diagnostic_criteria.spinal_compression_cancers: Breast, Prostate, Lung, Lymphoma, Multiple myeloma
diagnostic_criteria.hypercalcaemia_ecg: Osborne waves, ST elevation, shortened QTc, widened T-wave, bundle branch patterns, heart block, brady-dysrhythmias
diagnostic_criteria.pericardial_bedside_us: Fluid around heart, RA collapse late diastole, RV collapse early diastole
diagnostic_criteria.pericardial_becks_triad: Muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins, hypotension (only 1/3 patients)

clinical_features.tumour_lysis: Electrolyte derangement (low Ca2+, high K+, high PO4, high uric acid), renal failure, metabolic acidosis, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias
clinical_features.svc_symptoms: Dyspnoea, facial oedema, cough, chest pain, dysphagia, distended neck veins, facial plethora, arm oedema. RARE: vocal cord oedema
clinical_features.spinal_compression_symptoms: Back pain worse at night/lying, bladder/bowel symptoms, lower limb weakness, saddle anaesthesia, acute paralysis
clinical_features.hypercalcaemia_symptoms: Stones/Bones/Abdominal Moans/Psychic Groans. Neuro: fatigue, weakness, delirium. GIT: N+V, constipation, ileus. Cardiac: arrhythmias. Renal: polydipsia, polyuria, AKI
clinical_features.pericardial_effusion_signs: Exertional dyspnoea, chest pain, tachycardia, pulsus paradoxus, Becks triad (muffled heart sounds, JVP, hypotension)

drugs.notes: For volume overload in tumour lysis syndrome and hypercalcaemia
8mg for SVC syndrome, 10mg for malignant spinal cord compression
Empiric antibiotic for febrile neutropenia
For hypercalcaemia of malignancy - prolonged onset of action

management_steps.action: Tumour Lysis Syndrome: Aggressive IV fluids
management_steps.details: Balanced crystalloid, aim urine output >2ml/kg/hr. Manage electrolytes.
management_steps.caution: Dialysis if oliguria, anuria, volume overload, persistent hyperkalaemia

management_steps.details: Rapid work-up, Tazocin broad spectrum, consider fungal cover, blood cultures
management_steps.caution: Defer PR exam (typhlitis risk). Often 5-10 days post-chemo.

management_steps.details: Dexamethasone 8mg STAT. Elevate head. Consider anticoagulation if thrombus.
management_steps.caution: Expect difficult airway if vocal cord oedema. CT is gold standard.

management_steps.action: Spinal Cord Compression: Steroids and urgent referral
management_steps.details: Dexamethasone 10mg STAT. Urgent Ortho Spine and Radiation Oncology referral.
management_steps.caution: MRI is best imaging.

management_steps.details: Aggressive IV fluids. Lasix if overloaded. Bisphosphonates.
management_steps.caution: Monitor ECG for arrhythmias.

management_steps.details: US-guided Seldinger technique with CVP set. Identify deepest pocket.
management_steps.caution: DO NOT SLASH MYOCARDIUM. Avoid intubation if possible (worsens haemodynamics).

protocols.monitoring: Monitored bed for tumour lysis and hypercalcaemia | Serial electrolytes, renal function | ECG for hypercalcaemia and pericardial effusion | Bedside US for pericardial effusion
warnings: Tumour lysis syndrome: Dialysis indications include oliguria, anuria, volume overload, persistent hyperkalaemia
SVC syndrome: Vocal cord oedema is rare and dangerous - expect difficult airway
Pericardial tamponade: DO NOT SLASH MYOCARDIUM during pericardiocentesis
Try avoiding intubation in pericardial tamponade - worsens haemodynamic status
Febrile neutropenia: Often 5-10 days after last chemotherapy
protocols.disposition: Tumour Lysis: Urgent Oncology referral, ICU if severe | Febrile Neutropenia: Early oncology referral | SVC Syndrome: Oncology referral, ultimate Rx = chemo/radiotherapy/stenting | Spinal Compression: Urgent Ortho Spine + Radiation Oncology | Hypercalcaemia: Oncology if known, Renal if unknown | Pericardial Effusion: Oncology referral

protocols.notes_updates: SUB-PROTOCOL: Acute Red Eye - Differential Diagnosis | 1. Conjunctivitis: Vision NOT affected | Pain: scratchy sensation | Pupil: Normal, reactive | IOP: Normal | Signs: Mucoid/purulent discharge | Treatment: Topical chloramphenicol, saline eye drops | Disposition: Discharge - return if reduced vision | 2. Acute angle-closure glaucoma: Vision VERY REDUCED/blurred, halos around lights | Pain: SEVERE pain, photophobia | Pupil: Moderately dilated, non-reactive | IOP: Raised | Signs: Nausea, vomiting | Treatment: Topical beta-blocker (timolol 0.5% 1-2 drops), topical cholinergic (pilocarpine 2% 1-2 drops q15min until pupillary constriction), Acetazolamide 500mg IV/PO, +/- Mannitol. Consider steroid eye drops - consult ophthalmology first | Disposition: URGENT Ophthalmology referral | 3. Anterior uveitis/Iritis: Vision SLIGHTLY REDUCED/blurred | Pain: Moderate deep aching pain, worse with eye movements, photophobia | Pupil: Small, irregular, poorly reactive | IOP: Normal | Signs: Ciliary flush - diffuse redness most pronounced at limbus | Treatment: Topical chloramphenicol, saline eye drops. Consider steroid eye drops - consult ophthalmology first | Disposition: Semi-urgent Ophthalmology referral | 4. Corneal abrasion or ulcer: Vision SLIGHTLY REDUCED/blurred | Pain: Moderate to severe pain, scratchy sensation, photophobia | Pupil: Normal, reactive | IOP: Normal | Signs: Corneal defect that stains with fluorescein | Treatment: Topical chloramphenicol, saline eye drops | Disposition: Semi-urgent Ophthalmology referral | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Chemical Eye Injury | Alkalis: Lime, Mortar, Plaster, Drain cleaner, Oven cleaner, Ammonia | Acids: Toilet cleaners, Battery fluid, Pool acid | Neutral: Solvents, Detergents, Aerosols, Mace, Pepper spray | History: Chemical involved, Time of exposure, First aid administered, Symptoms (vision loss, pain, redness) | Examination: Measure eye pH using litmus paper, Visual acuity, Fundoscopy, Intraocular pressure (tonometry) | Management: Copious irrigation minimum 30 minutes, 3L Normal Saline | Topical anaesthetic drops should not delay irrigation | Continue irrigation until pH of tears = 7.5 | URGENT Ophthalmology referral | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Penetrating Eye Injury | History: Time of injury, Type of incident, Description of object, Tetanus status | Examination: Visual acuity BOTH EYES, Eye movement, Pupil size and reactivity | KEY RULES: DO NOT place pressure on eye | DO NOT place pad over eye | DO NOT remove penetrating object/foreign bodies | DO NOT try to clean the eye | Management: Cover with protective shield (plastic shield/cup) | Keep NPO | IV Kefazolin and oral Ciprofloxacin | ATT (tetanus prophylaxis) | Antiemetic | Analgesia | Urgent CT Orbits and brain | Urgent Ophthalmology referral | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Retinal Artery Occlusion | Presentation: Acute painless monocular visual loss | Management: URGENT Ophthalmology referral within 4-6 hours | Ocular massage | Lowering IOP (topical beta-blocker, acetazolamide) | Rebreathing into paper bag (increase CO2) | Thrombolysis may be considered in selected cases | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Blunt Eye Injury | Key differentials: | Retrobulbar haematoma with orbital compartment syndrome: Proptosis, elevated IOP, impaired eye movement, decreased visual acuity, RAPD, blown pupil | Guitar pick sign on POCUS. ED Mx: lateral canthotomy. Consider TXA | Hyphaema: Blood in anterior chamber. High risk: sickle cell, bleeding disorders, anticoagulation. Consider TXA. Reverse anticoagulation, head up, dilate pupil, control IOP | Globe rupture: See penetrating injury | Traumatic retinal detachment: See acute vision loss | Orbital blow-out fracture: Check eye movements

diagnostic_criteria.conjunctivitis: Vision not affected, scratchy pain, normal reactive pupil, normal IOP, mucoid/purulent discharge
diagnostic_criteria.acute_angle_closure_glaucoma: Very reduced/blurred vision with halos, severe pain/photophobia, moderately dilated non-reactive pupil, raised IOP, nausea/vomiting
diagnostic_criteria.anterior_uveitis_iritis: Slightly reduced/blurred vision, moderate deep aching pain worse with eye movement, photophobia, small irregular poorly reactive pupil, normal IOP, ciliary flush
diagnostic_criteria.corneal_abrasion_ulcer: Slightly reduced/blurred vision, moderate-severe pain/photophobia, normal reactive pupil, normal IOP, corneal defect stains with fluorescein
diagnostic_criteria.retinal_artery_occlusion: Acute painless monocular visual loss - URGENT referral within 4-6 hours
diagnostic_criteria.retrobulbar_haematoma: Proptosis, elevated IOP, impaired eye movement, decreased visual acuity, RAPD, blown pupil, guitar pick sign on POCUS
diagnostic_criteria.hyphaema: Blood in anterior chamber. High risk: sickle cell, bleeding disorders, anticoagulation
diagnostic_criteria.chemical_alkalis: Lime, Mortar, Plaster, Drain cleaner, Oven cleaner, Ammonia
diagnostic_criteria.chemical_acids: Toilet cleaners, Battery fluid, Pool acid
diagnostic_criteria.chemical_neutral: Solvents, Detergents, Aerosols, Mace, Pepper spray

clinical_features.conjunctivitis: Mucoid/purulent discharge, scratchy sensation, normal vision
clinical_features.acute_glaucoma: Severe pain, photophobia, halos around lights, nausea/vomiting, mid-dilated fixed pupil
clinical_features.iritis: Ciliary flush (redness at limbus), small irregular pupil, pain worse with eye movement
clinical_features.corneal_abrasion: Pain, photophobia, corneal defect staining with fluorescein
clinical_features.retrobulbar_haematoma: Proptosis, elevated IOP, impaired eye movement, RAPD, blown pupil

drugs.notes: Topical beta-blocker for acute angle-closure glaucoma and retinal artery occlusion
Topical cholinergic for acute angle-closure glaucoma
For acute angle-closure glaucoma and retinal artery occlusion
For acute angle-closure glaucoma if needed
For conjunctivitis, iritis, corneal abrasion
For penetrating eye injury
For penetrating eye injury
Consider for retrobulbar haematoma and hyphaema

management_steps.action: Acute Red Eye: Assess vision, pain, pupil, IOP
management_steps.details: Differentiate conjunctivitis, glaucoma, iritis, corneal abrasion. Check visual acuity, pupil reactivity, IOP.
management_steps.caution: Acute angle-closure glaucoma is an emergency

management_steps.details: Copious irrigation minimum 30 minutes with 3L Normal Saline. Continue until pH = 7.5.
management_steps.caution: Do NOT delay irrigation. Topical anaesthetic should not delay irrigation. Alkali injuries are worse than acids.

management_steps.details: Protective shield, NPO, IV Kefazolin + oral Ciprofloxacin, tetanus, antiemetic, analgesia, CT orbits.
management_steps.caution: DO NOT place pressure on eye, DO NOT pad, DO NOT remove object, DO NOT clean eye

management_steps.details: Urgent Ophthalmology within 4-6 hours. Ocular massage, lower IOP, rebreathing into paper bag.
management_steps.caution: Time-critical - vision loss is irreversible if delayed

management_steps.details: Retrobulbar haematoma (lateral canthotomy if indicated), hyphaema (TXA, reverse anticoagulation), globe rupture, blow-out fracture.
management_steps.caution: Guitar pick sign on POCUS for retrobulbar haematoma. Lateral canthotomy if orbital compartment syndrome.

protocols.monitoring: Visual acuity both eyes | IOP monitoring | pH monitoring for chemical injuries | Pupil size and reactivity
warnings: Acute angle-closure glaucoma: URGENT Ophthalmology referral required
Retinal artery occlusion: Urgent referral within 4-6 hours - time-critical
Chemical eye injury: Copious irrigation minimum 30 minutes, do not delay
Penetrating eye injury: DO NOT place pressure, DO NOT pad, DO NOT remove object, DO NOT clean
Alkali chemical injuries are generally worse than acid injuries
protocols.disposition: Conjunctivitis: Discharge, return if reduced vision | Acute glaucoma: URGENT Ophthalmology | Iritis/Corneal abrasion: Semi-urgent Ophthalmology | Chemical injury: URGENT Ophthalmology after irrigation | Penetrating injury: URGENT Ophthalmology, CT orbits | Retinal artery occlusion: URGENT within 4-6 hours

protocols.notes_updates: SUB-PROTOCOL: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA) | History: Sudden onset abdo/flank/back/groin/sciatic pain | Syncope | Hypovolaemic shock if leaking/ruptured | Lower limb ischaemia/cyanosis | Physical: Pulsatile abdominal mass (50%) | Unexplained hypotension/tachycardia | Tender/distended abdomen | May present with melena/GI bleed | Abdominal wall ecchymosis (flank/umbilical/thigh/scrotum - low sensitivity) | Risk factors: Male | Age >50 | Smoking | Atherosclerosis | Hypertension | Family history | Imaging: POCUS 1st line: OUTER LUMEN TO OUTER LUMEN aorta >3cm = aneurysmal, >6cm = high risk of rupture, free fluid = rupture until proven otherwise | CTA gold standard - preferred pre-op for surgical planning | UNSTABLE PATIENTS NEED THEATRE, NOT CT | Management: Urgent vascular consult | Resus as for major trauma - 2 IV lines, consider CVP | Minimise clear IV fluids | Crossmatch for massive transfusion, emergency blood in interim | Hypotensive resuscitation - aim SBP 80-90 mmHg | Tranexamic acid 1g stat | Fentanyl 1mcg/kg analgesia | Labetalol infusion if HPT emergency | Complications: Sudden death | Organ ischaemia (renal, mesenteric, lower limb) | Distal embolization | Rhabdomyolysis | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm/Dissection | History: Sudden tearing central chest pain radiating to back | Sudden neuro deficit | Physical: May present as HPT emergency, ACS, CVA, ADHF, SVC syndrome, pericardial tamponade | Pulse/BP discrepancy | New aortic regurg murmur | Unexplained syncope/hypotension | Risk factors: As for AAA | Connective tissue disorders | Bicuspid aortic valve | Syphilis | Imaging: CXR: widened mediastinum, loss of aortic knuckle, calcium sign | POCUS: widened aortic root, pericardial effusion | CTA gold standard | Types: Stanford A: Any ascending aorta involvement | Stanford B: Descending aorta only | Management: Urgent cardiothoracic consult | As for AAA management | NB: Pain control | Labetalol infusion 1st - aim pulse <60 bpm, SBP <110 mmHg | Add Nitrocine infusion if BP still elevated | Complications: Sudden death | Stroke | MI | Pericardial tamponade | ADHF due to valvular regurgitation | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Critical and Acute Limb Ischaemia | History: New/worsening pain/claudication | Altered sensation | Muscle weakness | The 6 Ps: PAIN, PALLOR, PULSELESSNESS, PERISHING COLD, PARAESTHESIA, PARALYSIS (LATE SIGNS) | Physical: Cold, blue/mottled limb | Decreased/absent pulses | Tissue necrosis | Embolus vs Thrombosis: Embolus = Acute onset, complete cutoff, no prior claudication/skin changes/ulcers/bruits (clinical diagnosis) | Thrombosis = Chronic, usually good collaterals, history of claudication/ulcers/chronic skin changes/bruits (CTA diagnosis) | Risk factors: Smoking | Atherosclerosis | Hypertension | AF | Large vessel aneurysms | DVT | DM | Investigations: Handheld Doppler | ABI <0.5 = SEVERE ARTERIAL ISCHAEMIA | CT Angio | Management: Urgent surgical/vascular consult | Fluid bolus, maintain MAP >65mmHg | Adequate analgesia, O2 if necessary | Anticoagulation in consultation with Vascular Surgery team | Complications: Limb loss | Rhabdomyolysis

diagnostic_criteria.aaa_pocus: Outer lumen to outer lumen aorta >3cm = aneurysmal, >6cm = high rupture risk, free fluid = rupture until proven otherwise
diagnostic_criteria.aaa_risk_factors: Male, Age >50, Smoking, Atherosclerosis, Hypertension, Family history
diagnostic_criteria.aaa_imaging: POCUS 1st line, CTA gold standard. UNSTABLE PATIENTS NEED THEATRE NOT CT
diagnostic_criteria.thoracic_types: Stanford A: Any ascending aorta involvement | Stanford B: Descending aorta only
diagnostic_criteria.thoracic_imaging: CXR: widened mediastinum, loss of aortic knuckle, calcium sign | POCUS: widened aortic root, pericardial effusion | CTA gold standard
diagnostic_criteria.limb_ischaemia_6_ps: PAIN, PALLOR, PULSELESSNESS, PERISHING COLD, PARAESTHESIA, PARALYSIS (late)
diagnostic_criteria.embolus_vs_thrombosis: Embolus = acute onset, complete cutoff, no prior claudication (clinical). Thrombosis = chronic, good collaterals, claudication history (CTA)

clinical_features.aaa_presentation: Sudden abdo/flank/back/groin/sciatic pain, syncope, hypotensive shock, pulsatile abdominal mass (50%)
clinical_features.thoracic_dissection_presentation: Sudden tearing central chest pain radiating to back, pulse/BP discrepancy, new aortic regurg murmur
clinical_features.limb_ischaemia_6_ps: PAIN, PALLOR, PULSELESSNESS, PERISHING COLD, PARAESTHESIA, PARALYSIS (late signs)
clinical_features.limb_ischaemia_physical: Cold, blue/mottled limb, decreased/absent pulses, tissue necrosis

drugs.notes: For AAA/ruptured aneurysm
Analgesia for AAA
For AAA if HPT emergency. For thoracic dissection: aim pulse <60, SBP <110.
Add if BP still elevated after labetalol in thoracic dissection

management_steps.details: 2 IV lines, consider CVP. POCUS first. CTA if stable. Hypotensive resuscitation SBP 80-90.
management_steps.caution: UNSTABLE PATIENTS NEED THEATRE, NOT CT

management_steps.details: TXA 1g stat, crossmatch for massive transfusion, emergency blood, analgesia.

management_steps.action: Thoracic dissection: BP and rate control
management_steps.details: Labetalol infusion 1st - aim pulse <60, SBP <110. Add Nitrocine if needed.
management_steps.caution: Urgent cardiothoracic consult. May present as HPT emergency, ACS, CVA, ADHF, tamponade.

management_steps.action: Acute limb ischaemia: Urgent vascular consult
management_steps.details: Fluid bolus, MAP >65, analgesia, O2. Anticoagulation per vascular surgery.
management_steps.caution: Distinguish embolus (acute, clinical diagnosis) vs thrombosis (chronic, CTA diagnosis)

protocols.monitoring: Serial BP and HR (aim pulse <60, SBP <110 for thoracic dissection) | MAP >65 for limb ischaemia | SBP 80-90 for AAA | Doppler/ABI for limb ischaemia
warnings: UNSTABLE AAA PATIENTS NEED THEATRE, NOT CT
AAA POCUS: Outer lumen to outer lumen >3cm = aneurysmal, >6cm = high rupture risk
Thoracic dissection may present as HPT emergency, ACS, CVA, ADHF, SVC syndrome, or pericardial tamponade
ABI <0.5 = SEVERE ARTERIAL ISCHAEMIA
Paralysis is a LATE sign in limb ischaemia
protocols.disposition: AAA: Urgent vascular consult, theatre if unstable/unruptured | Thoracic dissection: Urgent cardiothoracic consult | Acute limb ischaemia: Urgent surgical/vascular consult

protocols.item: ICU Referral, Medicolegal Documentation & Triage SOP
protocols.notes_updates: SUB-PROTOCOL: ICU Referral Criteria | Indications: Ventilated patients | Patients requiring vasopressor/inotropic support | Patients requiring invasive monitoring | Multi-organ dysfunction | Severe sepsis/septic shock | Post-cardiac arrest care | Severe traumatic brain injury | Status epilepticus | Severe metabolic derangements | Airway compromise requiring ongoing management | Process: Discuss with senior ED doctor | Contact ICU registrar/consultant | Provide clear handover (SBAR) | Document referral and response | Ensure ongoing care until ICU accepts/assumes care | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Medicolegal Recordkeeping | Principles: Legible, dated, timed, signed entries | Objective factual documentation | All clinical decisions documented with rationale | Consent discussions recorded | Refusals of treatment documented | Patient quotes for important conversations | Chain of custody for specimens | J88 form: For assault cases - complete medicolegal examination and documentation | Natural death: BI 1663 or DHA 1663 - for normal disease processes | Unnatural death: D28 (HJH269) - criminal/accidental processes, drug-related deaths. Do NOT fill BI 1663 | Forensic post-mortem: D28 taken with corpse to forensic mortuary. Police report required. Postmortem mandatory by law | Academic post-mortem: Family/HCW can request autopsy for cause of death. Form available in ED. Consent by next of kin | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Triage SOP | All patients triaged as soon as possible after arrival | Dedicated triage nurse required at all times | Triage allocation: 1 doctor for complex cases (24/7, ad hoc), 2 enrolled nurses supervised by senior staff | All patients must have vital signs: BP, Pulse, RR, Temp, O2 sat, Finger prick glucose | Additional triage investigations: urinalysis, pregnancy test (all females of childbearing age), ECG | TEWS score must be calculated accurately | Patient screened for SATS discriminators | Patients triaged using South African Triage Scale (SATS) | SATS posters prominently displayed | Triage area cleaned daily, equipment in working order | HJH treats children 12+ (surgical) and 14+ (medical). No paediatrics/obstetrics | RED patients to resus without delay | If HJH on formal divert: EMS patients triaged, stabilised, then formally diverted | Stable patients not from HJH drainage areas directed back to drainage area facility | Age criteria: Surgical 12 years and above | Medical 14 years and above

diagnostic_criteria.icu_referral_indications: Ventilated patients | Vasopressor/inotropic support | Invasive monitoring | Multi-organ dysfunction | Severe sepsis/septic shock | Post-cardiac arrest care | Severe TBI | Status epilepticus | Severe metabolic derangements | Airway compromise
diagnostic_criteria.triage_vital_signs: BP, Pulse, RR, Temp, O2 sat, Finger prick glucose
diagnostic_criteria.triage_additional_investigations: Urinalysis, pregnancy test (all females childbearing age), ECG
diagnostic_criteria.triage_scale: South African Triage Scale (SATS) with TEWS score
diagnostic_criteria.j88_form: For assault cases - medicolegal examination and documentation
diagnostic_criteria.natural_death_form: BI 1663 or DHA 1663 - normal disease processes
diagnostic_criteria.unnatural_death_form: D28 (HJH269) - criminal/accidental processes, drug-related deaths. Do NOT fill BI 1663
diagnostic_criteria.forensic_post_mortem: D28 taken with corpse to forensic mortuary. Police report required. Postmortem mandatory by law
diagnostic_criteria.academic_post_mortem: Family/HCW can request autopsy. Form available in ED. Consent by next of kin

management_steps.details: Discuss with senior ED doctor. Contact ICU registrar/consultant with SBAR handover. Document referral and response.
management_steps.caution: Ensure ongoing care until ICU accepts/assumes care

management_steps.details: Legible, dated, timed, signed entries. Objective factual documentation. J88 for assault. Correct death form (BI 1663 for natural, D28 for unnatural).
management_steps.caution: Do NOT fill BI 1663 for unnatural deaths. Chain of custody for specimens.

management_steps.details: Triage ASAP. All patients: vital signs + TEWS + SATS. Additional: urinalysis, pregnancy test, ECG.
management_steps.caution: RED patients to resus without delay. Surgical 12+, Medical 14+. No paediatrics/obstetrics.

protocols.monitoring: TEWS score accurately calculated | SATS discriminators screened | All vital signs documented | ECG and pregnancy test as indicated
warnings: Do NOT fill BI 1663 for unnatural deaths - use D28 (HJH269)
Forensic post-mortem: D28 taken with corpse to forensic mortuary, police report required, postmortem mandatory by law
HJH treats children 12+ (surgical) and 14+ (medical) - no paediatrics/obstetrics
RED patients to resus without delay
Ensure ongoing care until ICU accepts/assumes care
protocols.disposition: ICU referral per indications with senior ED doctor discussion and SBAR handover | Assault cases: J88 form completed | Deaths: correct form per natural vs unnatural | Triage: RED to resus, formal divert protocol if applicable

protocols.item: Post Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) - Occupational and Non-Occupational
protocols.notes_updates: SUB-PROTOCOL: Non-Occupational HIV PEP (nPEP) | Exposures: Sexual exposure/Burst condom | Assault with exposure to blood from multiple sources | Human bite | Contact sports with blood exposure | Sharing of needles | Blood contact with mucous membranes or non-intact skin | Timing: Start PEP within 2 hours up to 72 hours post-exposure. Give 3 days supply | History required: When exposure occurred, Where, How, Source HIV status, Recipient and source HBV status, Recent CD4/VL of source if known | Source management - Negative source: Start PEP within 2-72hrs. Give 3 days supply. Consider window period. If concerned, take TPHA/HBV/HCV/HIV with consent. Stop after confirmed negative and window period excluded | Unknown source: Start PEP within 2-72hrs. Give 3 days supply. Take TPHA/HBV/HCV/HIV with consent. If source NOT AVAILABLE assume HIV positive due to high community prevalence | Positive source: Start PEP within 2-72hrs. Give 3 days supply. Take TPHA/HBV/HCV/VL. Check ARV history, possible resistance (consult HIV doctor), recent VL/CD4 | Drug regimen: Preferred TLD (Tenofovir/Lamivudine/Dolutegravir) 1 tablet daily for 28 days | Safe in pregnancy | Dual therapy is no longer recommended | Follow-up: Go to Thembalethu next working day to get rest of PEP for 28/7 | Counsel: reassure, provide undivided attention, provide information on side effects | For further information call infectious diseases | FAQ - Patient keeps coming back for NPEP: Look into circumstances and risk behaviour. If negative with unprotected sex, consider PrEP - refer to HIV clinician at TLC | FAQ - Source tests negative but concerned about window period: Still prescribe NPEP. If source in acute/early infection, viral load very high and risk 8-12x higher than at viral set point | FAQ - Breastfeeding: If at all possible, STOP breastfeeding. Only recommence 3 months after exposure and recipient tests HIV negative | FAQ - Not tolerating ARVs and wants to stop early: Animal studies show best results at 28 days. Inadequate protection at 7, 14, or 21 days. Protection not enhanced beyond 28 days. See HIV clinician for regimen change | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Occupational HIV PEP | Significant exposures: Needle stick injuries | Mucosal splash injuries | Sexual assault | Exposure of non-intact skin | Human bites with break of skin and blood exposure | Non-significant exposures: Intact skin exposed to infected material | Immediate actions - Needle stick: Wash wound with water and soap (not alcohol) | Mucosal splash: Irrigate with copious amounts of water or normal saline, before and after removal of contact lenses | Non-intact skin: Wash with water and soap (not alcohol) | Timing: Start PEP within 2 hours up to 72 hours post-exposure. Give 3 days supply | Drug regimen: Preferred TLD (Tenofovir/Lamivudine/Dolutegravir) 1 tablet daily for 28 days - Same as non-occupational PEP | Follow-up: Complete COIDA forms if applicable | Follow up at Thembalethu for remainder of PEP | Window period and acute retroviral syndrome counselling | COIDA forms: WCL2 (employers report of accident) | WCL3 (claim for compensation) | WCL4 (first medical report) | WCL5 (progress/final medical report) | WCL6 (resumption of report) | WCL132 (affidavit by injured) | Affidavit by eyewitness | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Hepatitis B Post-Exposure Prophylaxis | Risk transmission: HepB eAg positive needle stick 22-30% | HepB eAg negative needle stick 1-6% | Mucus membrane: Very low risk | Non-intact skin: Very low risk | Management: Check recipient HBV immunity (anti-HBs) | If non-immune: HBIG + start hepatitis B vaccine series | If partially immune (anti-HBs 10-100 IU/L): Consider HBIG + booster vaccine | If fully immune (anti-HBs >100 IU/L): No treatment needed | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Risk of HIV Transmission | Needle stick: 0.3% (increased with hollow bore needles, deep injury, injuries into blood vessels, high viral load) | Mucus membranes: 0.09% | Non-intact skin: Unknown (thought to be less than mucus membrane) | Window period: 8-12x higher risk if source in acute/early infection | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Acute Retroviral Syndrome (Window Period) | Description: Period between HIV infection and completion of initial immune response (2-4 weeks, can present months later) | Signs/Symptoms: Headache, Fever, Fatigue, Lymphadenopathy, Myalgia, Arthralgia, Sore throat, Mucocutaneous ulcers | Notes: 10-60% of patients asymptomatic. If source has these symptoms with mucocutaneous ulcers, especially suspicious of ARS | Source at most infectious state. Risk of acquiring HIV 8-12x higher | | SEXUAL ASSAULT PROTOCOL | Principles: Timeously attend to survivors | Treat with respect and dignity to avoid secondary trauma | Document in J88 and doctors notes | Consent for examination and forensic collection required | Process: Triage | Resuscitation if required | Open file | Escort to medicolegal room | Full history and physical examination (chaperone required) | Collect evidence with SAECK kit | Counselling for HIV | Bloods: FBC, Creatinine, HIV ELISA, RPR, Hepatitis B and C | Urine: Dipstix, Pregnancy test | Prophylaxis: HIV PEP starter pack (3 days TLD) | STI Prophylaxis: Ceftriaxone 250mg IM stat, Metronidazole 2g PO stat, Azithromycin 1g PO stat | Emergency Contraception: Ovral/Escapelle 2 tablets PO stat then 2 tablets 12 hours later | Nausea Prophylaxis: Metoclopramide 10mg PO 8hrly PRN | Follow-up: Blood results checked at Thembalethu | 28/7 script for ARVs written | Letter to Thembalethu clinic for rest of PEP | Social worker and psychologist referrals on case-by-case basis

diagnostic_criteria.npep_exposures: Sexual exposure/Burst condom | Assault with blood exposure | Human bite | Contact sports blood exposure | Sharing needles | Blood contact with mucous membranes or non-intact skin
diagnostic_criteria.npep_timing: Start within 2 hours up to 72 hours post-exposure. Give 3 days supply.
diagnostic_criteria.occupational_significant_exposures: Needle stick injuries | Mucosal splash injuries | Sexual assault | Exposure of non-intact skin | Human bites with break of skin and blood exposure
diagnostic_criteria.occupational_non_significant: Intact skin exposed to infected material
diagnostic_criteria.hiv_transmission_risk_needle_stick: 0.3% (increased with hollow bore needles, deep injury, high viral load)
diagnostic_criteria.hiv_transmission_risk_window_period: 8-12x higher risk if source in acute/early infection
diagnostic_criteria.hepb_immunity_non_immune: HBIG + start hepatitis B vaccine series
diagnostic_criteria.hepb_immunity_partial: anti-HBs 10-100 IU/L: Consider HBIG + booster vaccine
diagnostic_criteria.ars_symptoms: Headache, Fever, Fatigue, Lymphadenopathy, Myalgia, Arthralgia, Sore throat, Mucocutaneous ulcers

clinical_features.acute_retroviral_syndrome: Headache, Fever, Fatigue, Lymphadenopathy, Myalgia, Arthralgia, Sore throat, Mucocutaneous ulcers. 10-60% asymptomatic.
clinical_features.note: If source has ARS symptoms with mucocutaneous ulcers, most infectious state. Risk 8-12x higher.

drugs.notes: Preferred regimen for HIV PEP. Safe in pregnancy. Start within 2-72 hours.
STI prophylaxis for sexual assault
STI prophylaxis for sexual assault
STI prophylaxis for sexual assault

drugs.adult_dose: 2 tablets PO stat then 2 tablets 12 hours later
drugs.notes: Emergency contraception for sexual assault
Nausea prophylaxis for sexual assault

management_steps.details: Within 72 hours? Significant vs non-significant? Source HIV/HBV status?
management_steps.caution: Start PEP within 2 hours up to 72 hours. Earlier is better.

management_steps.details: Needle stick: Wash with water and soap (NOT alcohol). Mucosal splash: Irrigate copiously with water/saline.
TLD 1 tablet daily for 28 days. Give 3 days supply from ED. Safe in pregnancy.
management_steps.caution: Dual therapy no longer recommended. If source positive, check ARV history and resistance.

management_steps.details: Check recipient anti-HBs. Non-immune: HBIG + vaccine series. Partially immune: HBIG + booster. Fully immune: no treatment.
HIV PEP (3 days TLD) + STI prophylaxis (Ceftriaxone 250mg IM, Metronidazole 2g PO, Azithromycin 1g PO) + Emergency contraception + Nausea prophylaxis
management_steps.caution: Document in J88. Consent required. Chaperone for examination. SAECK kit for evidence.

management_steps.details: Thembalethu next working day for rest of PEP. COIDA forms for occupational. Window period counselling.
management_steps.caution: Inadequate protection at 7, 14, or 21 days - complete 28 days.

protocols.monitoring: Adherence to PEP regimen | Side effects (nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, headache, fatigue, renal dysfunction) | Follow-up HIV testing at 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months
warnings: Start PEP within 2 hours up to 72 hours post-exposure - earlier is better
If source NOT AVAILABLE assume HIV positive due to high community prevalence
Dual therapy is no longer recommended for HIV PEP
Animal studies show inadequate protection at 7, 14, or 21 days - must complete 28 days
If source in acute/early infection, viral load very high and risk 8-12x higher
Breastfeeding: If at all possible, STOP breastfeeding. Only recommence 3 months after exposure and recipient tests HIV negative
For sexual assault: Document in J88, consent required, chaperone for examination
protocols.disposition: Non-occupational PEP: Thembalethu next working day for remainder of 28-day course | Occupational PEP: Complete COIDA forms, Thembalethu follow-up | Sexual assault: J88 documentation, SAECK kit, social worker/psychologist referrals

protocols.notes_updates: Principles of Documentation for Medical Records | 1. The medical record should be complete and legible | 2. The documentation of each patient encounter should include: the date, the reason for the encounter, appropriate history and physical exam in relationship to the patient's chief complaint, review of lab, X-Ray data and other ancillary services where appropriate, assessment, a plan for care (including discharge plan, if appropriate) | 3. The reasons for and results of X-Rays, lab tests and other ancillary services should be documented or included in the medical record | 4. Relevant health risk factors should be identified | 5. The patient's progress, including response to treatment, change in treatment, change in diagnosis, and patient non-compliance, should be documented | 6. The written plan for care should include when appropriate: treatments and medications specifying frequency and dosage, any referrals and consultations, patient/family education, specific instructions for follow-up | 7. The documentation should support the intensity of the patient evaluation and/or the treatment, including thought processes and the complexity of medical decision-making as it relates to the patient's chief complaint for the encounter | 8. All entries to the medical record should be dated and authenticated | 9. The ICD-10 code should be reflected in the documentation in the medical record | DISCHARGE FROM ED: All patients discharged from the Emergency Department must receive an ED discharge summary | This allows local clinic or OPD follow up with all relevant information available to the receiving doctor | ED DISCHARGE SUMMARY should include: patient name, hospital number, date seen, referral doctor/institution, reason for referral, discharge diagnosis, investigation findings (including barcode numbers for X-rays, labs, ECG, other), follow-up/discharge plan with dates where applicable, TTO (take home medication), doctor name, MP number, and signature

management_steps.action: Ensure medical record is complete and legible
management_steps.details: All entries must be dated and authenticated; ICD-10 code should be reflected
management_steps.caution: Incomplete or illegible records compromise medicolegal defensibility

management_steps.details: Include: date, reason for encounter, history and physical exam relevant to chief complaint, review of labs/imaging, assessment, care plan with discharge plan if appropriate
management_steps.caution: Documentation must support intensity of evaluation and complexity of decision-making

management_steps.action: Document reasons and results for all investigations
management_steps.details: X-rays, lab tests, and other ancillary services - document reasons for ordering and include results

management_steps.action: Identify and document relevant health risk factors
management_steps.details: Include risk factors relevant to presentation and management
Include response to treatment, changes in treatment, changes in diagnosis, and any patient non-compliance

management_steps.action: Write a clear plan for care
management_steps.details: Include treatments and medications with frequency and dosage, referrals and consultations, patient/family education, specific follow-up instructions

management_steps.action: Complete ED discharge summary for all discharged patients
management_steps.details: Include: name, hospital number, date seen, referral doctor/institution, reason for referral, discharge diagnosis, investigation findings with barcodes, follow-up plan, TTO, doctor name, MP number, signature
management_steps.caution: Discharge summary enables local clinic or OPD follow-up with all relevant information

protocols.monitoring: Regular review of documentation quality and completeness; ensure all entries dated and authenticated
warnings: Incomplete or illegible medical records compromise medicolegal defensibility
All entries must be dated and authenticated
Documentation must support the intensity of patient evaluation and treatment complexity
All discharged patients must receive an ED discharge summary
protocols.disposition: Ensure complete documentation before patient leaves ED; provide discharge summary for all discharged patients

protocols.notes_updates: Comprehensive AHA 2020 resuscitation algorithms for adult and paediatric patients. | Includes Adult BLS, Adult Cardiac Arrest ACLS, Post-Cardiac Arrest Care, Bradycardia, Tachycardia, Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy, Neonatal Resuscitation, Paediatric BLS (single and 2+ rescuer), Paediatric Cardiac Arrest, Paediatric Post-Cardiac Arrest Care, Paediatric Bradycardia, Paediatric Tachycardia. | CPR Quality: Push hard (at least 2 inches/5 cm) and fast (100-120/min), allow complete chest recoil, minimize interruptions, change compressor every 2 minutes. | Compression-ventilation ratio: Adults 30:2, Paediatric 15:2 (with advanced airway: 1 breath every 6 seconds adults/10 breaths/min, 1 breath every 2-3 seconds paeds). | Shock energy: Biphasic 120-200J initial (or max available if unknown), Monophasic 360J. | Reversible causes (H's and T's): Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen ion (acidosis), Hypo-/hyperkalemia, Hypothermia, Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade (cardiac), Toxins, Thrombosis (pulmonary), Thrombosis (coronary).

drugs.paediatric_dose: 0.01 mg/kg IV/IO (0.1 mL/kg of 0.1 mg/mL concentration), max 1 mg. Repeat every 3-5 min. ET dose: 0.1 mg/kg (0.1 mL/kg of 1 mg/mL concentration)
drugs.notes: Give ASAP in cardiac arrest. Standard dose every 3-5 min throughout arrest.

drugs.adult_dose: First dose: 300 mg bolus IV/IO. Second dose: 150 mg.
drugs.paediatric_dose: 5 mg/kg bolus during cardiac arrest. May repeat up to 3 total doses for refractory VF/pulseless VT.
drugs.notes: Give after 3rd shock in refractory VF/pVT. Follow with maintenance infusion for recurrent VT.

drugs.adult_dose: 150 mg over 10 minutes. Repeat as needed if VT recurs. Follow by maintenance infusion of 1 mg/min for first 6 hours.
drugs.notes: For stable wide-QRS tachycardia only. Loading dose 5mg/kg (max 300mg) in 200ml 5% dextrose over 1 hour. Maintenance: 900mg in 200ml 5% dextrose at 9.5ml/hr over 23 hours.

drugs.item: Lidocaine (Adult Cardiac Arrest - alternative to Amiodarone)
drugs.adult_dose: First dose: 1-1.5 mg/kg IV/IO. Second dose: 0.5-0.75 mg/kg.
drugs.notes: Alternative to amiodarone in cardiac arrest. Also for stable wide-QRS tachycardia.

drugs.adult_dose: First dose: 1 mg bolus IV. Repeat every 3-5 minutes. Maximum: 3 mg.
drugs.paediatric_dose: 0.02 mg/kg IV/IO. May repeat once. Minimum dose 0.1 mg, maximum single dose 0.5 mg.
drugs.notes: For symptomatic bradycardia with pulse. Not effective in heart transplant patients.

drugs.adult_dose: Usual infusion rate 5-20 mcg/kg per minute. Titrate to patient response; taper slowly.
drugs.notes: Alternative to epinephrine infusion for symptomatic bradycardia unresponsive to atropine.

drugs.adult_dose: 2-10 mcg per minute infusion. Titrate to patient response.
drugs.paediatric_dose: 0.01 mg/kg IV/IO (0.1 mL/kg of 0.1 mg/mL concentration). Repeat every 3-5 minutes.
drugs.notes: For bradycardia unresponsive to atropine and transcutaneous pacing.

drugs.adult_dose: First dose: 6 mg rapid IV push; follow with NS flush. Second dose: 12 mg if required.
drugs.paediatric_dose: First dose: 0.1 mg/kg rapid bolus (maximum 6 mg). Second dose: 0.2 mg/kg (maximum 12 mg).
drugs.notes: Only for regular narrow-complex tachycardia. Follow each dose with rapid NS flush.

drugs.adult_dose: 20-50 mg/min until arrhythmia suppressed, hypotension ensues, QRS duration increases >50%, or maximum dose 17 mg/kg given. Maintenance infusion: 1-4 mg/min.
drugs.notes: Avoid if prolonged QT or CHF. Requires blood pressure and ECG monitoring.
Avoid if prolonged QT. Risk of torsades de pointes.

management_steps.details: Ensure the scene is safe before approaching the patient
management_steps.caution: Do not proceed if scene is unsafe

management_steps.details: Check for responsiveness, shout for nearby help, activate emergency response system, get AED and emergency equipment
management_steps.caution: Minimize delay in activating emergency response

management_steps.details: Look for no breathing or only gasping and check pulse simultaneously. Check for no more than 10 seconds.
management_steps.caution: Do not delay CPR if pulse is uncertain

management_steps.details: Monitor until emergency responders arrive
management_steps.caution: Continue to reassess

management_steps.details: Provide rescue breathing: 1 breath every 6 seconds (adult) or 1 breath every 2-3 seconds (paediatric). Check pulse every 2 minutes.
management_steps.caution: If possible opioid overdose, administer naloxone per protocol

management_steps.action: No breathing or only gasping, pulse not felt
management_steps.details: Start CPR immediately. Perform cycles of 30 compressions and 2 breaths (adult). Use AED as soon as available.
management_steps.caution: Push hard (at least 2 inches/5 cm) and fast (100-120/min), allow complete recoil

management_steps.details: Give oxygen, attach monitor/defibrillator. High-quality CPR: push hard (at least 2 inches/5cm) and fast (100-120/min), allow complete chest recoil, minimize interruptions, change compressor every 2 minutes. 30:2 ratio without advanced airway; 1 breath every 6 seconds with advanced airway.
management_steps.caution: Quantitative waveform capnography - if PetCO2 is low or decreasing, reassess CPR quality

management_steps.details: Assess if rhythm is VF/pVT (shockable) or Asystole/PEA (non-shockable)
management_steps.caution: Minimize pauses in CPR for rhythm checks

management_steps.details: Give 1 shock. Biphasic: Manufacturer recommendation (initial 120-200J) or max available. Monophasic: 360J. Resume CPR immediately for 2 minutes.
management_steps.caution: Do not delay CPR after shock - resume immediately

management_steps.details: Continue CPR 2 min, establish IV/IO access. Give epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO ASAP, then every 3-5 minutes.
management_steps.caution: Do not interrupt CPR for drug administration

management_steps.details: Check rhythm after 2 min CPR. If still shockable, give shock. Resume CPR 2 min.
management_steps.caution: Continue CPR immediately after shock

management_steps.details: After 3rd shock, give Amiodarone 300 mg bolus IV/IO OR Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg IV/IO. Continue CPR 2 min.
management_steps.caution: Amiodarone second dose: 150 mg if needed. Lidocaine second dose: 0.5-0.75 mg/kg

management_steps.details: Continue CPR 2 min. IV/IO access. Give epinephrine every 3-5 min. Consider advanced airway, capnography.
management_steps.caution: Do not shock asystole. Search and treat reversible causes (H's and T's)

management_steps.details: Continue CPR 2 min, epinephrine every 3-5 min, treat reversible causes. Check rhythm after each 2 min cycle.
management_steps.caution: Consider appropriateness of continued resuscitation if no ROSC after prolonged effort

management_steps.details: Signs: Pulse and BP, abrupt sustained increase in PetCO2 (typically >/=40 mmHg), spontaneous arterial pressure waves. Go to Post-Cardiac Arrest Care.
management_steps.caution: Post-cardiac arrest care begins immediately

management_steps.details: Early placement of endotracheal tube. Waveform capnography or capnometry to confirm and monitor ET tube placement.
management_steps.caution: Avoid hypoxemia during intubation

management_steps.details: Titrate FiO2 for SpO2 92%-98%. Start at 10 breaths/min, titrate to PaCO2 of 35-45 mmHg.
management_steps.caution: Avoid both hyperoxia and hypoxia

management_steps.details: Administer crystalloid and/or vasopressor or inotrope for goal SBP >90 mmHg or MAP >65 mmHg.
management_steps.caution: Avoid hypotension

management_steps.details: Check for STEMI. If STEMI present, consider emergent cardiac intervention.
management_steps.caution: STEMI requires emergent intervention even in comatose patients

management_steps.details: If patient not following commands, start TTM as soon as possible. Begin at 32-36 degrees C for 24 hours using cooling device with feedback loop.
management_steps.caution: Monitor blood pressure and treat hypotension during rewarming. Prevent shivering.

management_steps.details: If follows commands: awake pathway. If comatose: TTM + obtain brain CT.
management_steps.caution: Consider early brain imaging to diagnose treatable causes

management_steps.details: Heart rate typically <50/min. Identify and treat underlying cause. Maintain patent airway. Oxygen if hypoxemic. IV access. 12-lead ECG if available.
management_steps.caution: Consider possible hypoxic and toxicologic causes

management_steps.details: Check for: acutely altered mental status, signs of shock, acute heart failure.
management_steps.caution: If no symptoms, monitor and observe

management_steps.details: First dose: 1 mg bolus IV. Repeat every 3-5 min. Maximum: 3 mg.
management_steps.caution: Not effective in heart transplant patients

management_steps.details: If atropine ineffective, initiate transcutaneous pacing.
management_steps.caution: Sedation may be needed if patient conscious

management_steps.details: 5-20 mcg/kg/min infusion. Titrate to patient response.
management_steps.caution: Taper slowly when discontinuing

management_steps.details: 2-10 mcg per minute infusion. Titrate to patient response.
management_steps.caution: Requires close hemodynamic monitoring

management_steps.details: Heart rate typically >/=150/min. Identify and treat underlying cause. Maintain patent airway. Oxygen if hypoxemic. IV access. 12-lead ECG.
management_steps.caution: Consider possible hypoxic and toxicologic causes

management_steps.details: Check for: acutely altered mental status, signs of shock, acute heart failure.
management_steps.caution: If unstable, proceed immediately to synchronized cardioversion

management_steps.details: For unstable patients. Consider sedation. Use device-specific recommended energy level to maximize first shock success.
management_steps.caution: Do not delay cardioversion in unstable patient

management_steps.details: Vagal maneuvers (if regular). Adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push, follow with NS flush. Second dose 12 mg if required.
management_steps.caution: Adenosine only if regular and monomorphic

management_steps.details: Beta-blocker or calcium channel blocker. Consider expert consultation.
management_steps.caution: Control rate, identify underlying rhythm

management_steps.details: Consider adenosine only if regular and monomorphic. Antiarrhythmic infusion: Procainamide, Amiodarone, or Sotalol. Expert consultation.
management_steps.caution: If refractory, consider underlying cause and expert consultation

management_steps.action: Cardiac arrest in pregnancy - Assemble team
management_steps.details: Assemble maternal cardiac arrest team including obstetric, neonatal, emergency, anesthesiology, intensive care, and cardiac arrest services.
management_steps.caution: Team planning critical for maternal and fetal outcomes

management_steps.details: High-quality CPR with manual left uterine displacement. Defibrillation when indicated (standard energies - no dose change in pregnancy). Administer medications (epinephrine) as in standard ACLS.
management_steps.caution: Manual left uterine displacement essential to relieve aortocaval compression

management_steps.details: ABCDEFGH: A-Anesthetic complications, B-Bleeding, C-Cardiovascular, D-Drugs, E-Embolic, F-Fever, G-General nonobstetric causes (H's and T's), H-Hypertension
management_steps.caution: Pregnancy-specific causes must be considered

management_steps.details: Airway management: Use most experienced provider. Endotracheal intubation or supraglottic advanced airway. Waveform capnography. 1 breath every 6 seconds (10/min) with continuous compressions once advanced airway in place. IV above diaphragm. Stop IV magnesium if receiving.
management_steps.caution: Difficult airway is common in pregnancy

management_steps.details: If no ROSC within 5 minutes of resuscitation. Goal: ideally within 5 minutes depending on provider resources and skill sets. Neonatal team to receive neonate.
management_steps.caution: Improves both maternal and fetal outcomes. Do not delay.

management_steps.details: Check responsiveness, shout for help, activate emergency response. Single rescuer: 30 compressions to 2 breaths. Two or more rescuers: 15 compressions to 2 breaths.
management_steps.caution: Use AED as soon as available

management_steps.action: Pulse felt but HR <60/min with poor perfusion
management_steps.details: Start CPR. Push hard (>/=1/3 anteroposterior diameter) and fast (100-120/min). 15:2 ratio with 2+ rescuers.
management_steps.caution: Bradycardia with poor perfusion in children requires CPR

management_steps.details: Begin bag-mask ventilation and give oxygen. CPR: Push hard (>/=1/3 AP diameter) and fast (100-120/min), allow complete recoil. 15:2 ratio without advanced airway. With advanced airway: continuous compressions, 1 breath every 2-3 seconds.
management_steps.caution: Minimize interruptions

management_steps.details: First shock: 2 J/kg. Second shock: 4 J/kg. Subsequent shocks: >/=4 J/kg, maximum 10 J/kg or adult dose.
management_steps.caution: Use paediatric paddles/pads if available

management_steps.details: Target normoxemia 94%-99%. Hemodynamic goals: SBP >5th percentile for age/sex. TTM: If comatose, apply 32-34 degrees C followed by 36-37.5 degrees C, or only 36-37.5 degrees C. Prevent and treat fever. Treat seizures. Monitor glucose, avoid hypoglycemia.
management_steps.caution: Multiple modalities for prognosis over any single factor

diagnostic_criteria.ROSC: Pulse and blood pressure | Abrupt sustained increase in PetCO2 (typically >/=40 mmHg) | Spontaneous arterial pressure waves with intra-arterial monitoring
diagnostic_criteria.shockable_rhythm: VF (ventricular fibrillation) or pVT (pulseless ventricular tachycardia)
diagnostic_criteria.paediatric_bradycardia: HR <60/min with signs of poor perfusion
diagnostic_criteria.paediatric_tachycardia_svt: Infant rate >/=220/min, Child rate >/=180/min with P waves absent/abnormal, RR interval not variable, history of abrupt rate change
diagnostic_criteria.paediatric_tachycardia_sinus: Infant rate usually <220/min, Child rate usually <180/min with P waves present/normal, variable RR interval

clinical_features.adult_bls: Unresponsive, no breathing or only gasping, no pulse felt within 10 seconds
clinical_features.cardiac_arrest_signs: Sudden collapse, unresponsive, absent pulse, abnormal or absent breathing
clinical_features.post_cardiac_arrest: Return of spontaneous circulation, may be comatose or following commands
clinical_features.bradycardia_symptoms: Altered mental status, signs of shock, acute heart failure, hypotension, syncope
clinical_features.tachycardia_symptoms: Palpitations, chest pain, dyspnoea, altered mental status, shock, acute heart failure
clinical_features.maternal_arrest_causes: Anesthetic complications, Bleeding, Cardiovascular, Drugs, Embolic, Fever, General causes (H's and T's), Hypertension
clinical_features.reversible_causes_Hs: Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen ion (acidosis), Hypo-/hyperkalemia, Hypothermia
clinical_features.reversible_causes_Ts: Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade (cardiac), Toxins, Thrombosis (pulmonary), Thrombosis (coronary)

protocols.monitoring: Continuous ECG monitoring | Quantitative waveform capnography (PetCO2) | SpO2 monitoring | Blood pressure (intra-arterial if available) | Core temperature during TTM | Serum lactate, urine output, central venous oxygen saturation | Continuous or intermittent EEG if encephalopathy | Blood glucose monitoring | Electrolytes within normal ranges
warnings: Do not interrupt CPR for more than 10 seconds except for shock delivery
Avoid excessive ventilation - can decrease venous return and cardiac output
Amiodarone and lidocaine are for VF/pVT only - do not give in asystole or PEA
Atropine is NOT effective in heart transplant patients
Adenosine is contraindicated in irregular wide-complex tachycardia
Synchronized cardioversion should not be delayed in unstable tachycardia
In pregnancy: manual left uterine displacement is essential; perform perimortem cesarean within 5 minutes if no ROSC
Consider appropriateness of continued resuscitation if no ROSC after prolonged effort
Post-cardiac arrest TTM requires continuous temperature monitoring and shivering prevention
Paediatric drug doses are weight-based - always double-check calculations
ET drug delivery is less reliable than IV/IO - establish IV/IO access as soon as possible
protocols.disposition: Adult: Continue resuscitation until ROSC achieved or termination deemed appropriate. Post-ROSC: ICU admission for continued management including TTM, hemodynamic support, neurological monitoring. | STEMI: Emergent cardiac catheterization. | Paediatric: PICU admission post-ROSC. | Pregnancy: Maternal ICU + neonatal team involvement.

protocols.notes_updates: Standard infusion concentrations and drip rates for common ED infusions. | All infusions are for use with INFUSION PUMP unless otherwise specified. | Weight-based dosing tables provided for common weight ranges (40-120kg). | IMPORTANT: For use in intubated/sedated patients only unless specified otherwise. | Monitor all patients closely for adverse effects including hypotension, respiratory depression, and arrhythmias.

drugs.adult_dose: Loading: 5mg/kg (max 300mg) in 200ml 5% dextrose over 1 hour. Maintenance: 900mg in 200ml 5% dextrose at 9.5ml/hr over 23 hours.
drugs.notes: Add 900mg (18ml) amiodarone to 200ml 5% dextrose. Run at 9.5ml/hr. Continuously monitor vital signs. For stable wide-QRS tachycardia. Maximum total dose 100mg in cardiac arrest (300mg bolus + 150mg second dose).

drugs.adult_dose: Loading: 0.25mg/kg IVI bolus. Analgesia: 0.05-0.1mg/kg/hr (2-12ml/hr for 40-120kg). Mild sedation: >/=0.2mg/kg/hr. Titrate to effect.
drugs.notes: Preparation: 200mg ketamine in 200ml normal saline (1mg/ml). Use ketamine 100mg/ml concentration (NOT 10mg/ml). Withdraw 2ml NS before adding 2ml (200mg) ketamine. Monitor for dysrhythmias and hallucinations. Analgesic at low doses, dissociative sedation at higher doses.

drugs.adult_dose: Loading: Weight-based slow IV push (see table). Maintenance: 0.025-0.4mg/kg/hr (2-96ml/hr for 40-120kg).
drugs.notes: Preparation: 90mg in 180ml normal saline (0.5mg/ml). Loading: 1mg/ml solution (1 amp morphine 15mg/1ml + 14ml NS in 20ml syringe). Continuous morphine infusion for intubated patient. For use with INFUSION PUMP. Monitor for respiratory depression and hypotension.

drugs.adult_dose: Loading: 500mcg/kg undiluted IV push over 60 seconds. Maintenance: Start at 50mcg/kg/min, titrate every 4 minutes in increments of 50mcg/kg/min. Usual maintenance 150-300mcg/kg/min. Maximum 300mcg/kg/min.
drugs.notes: Preparation: Draw up 5 ampoules (100mg/10ml each) undiluted into 50ml syringe for infusion (10mg/ml). Give 10-20ml/kg fluid bolus (Ringers/Balsol) FIRST. If no BP response after 4 minutes, repeat loading dose and increase infusion by 50mcg/kg/min increments up to 300mcg/kg/min. Ultra-short acting beta-blocker. For use with SYRINGE DRIVER only.

drugs.adult_dose: 0.5ml (50mcg) IV STAT as rescue for post-intubation hypotension. Can repeat.
drugs.notes: Preparation: 1mg in 10ml normal saline (1ml adrenaline + 9ml NS in 10ml syringe = 100mcg/ml). 0.5ml = 50mcg. Rescue IV push for post-intubation hypotension only.

Preparation: 12mg in 200ml normal saline (withdraw 12ml NS, add 12ml adrenaline). 100mcg/ml concentration. Can be infused via large-bore peripheral IV in initial resus stages. Consider early CVP placement if long-term infusion anticipated. First-line vasopressor for shock.

drugs.adult_dose: Typical dose: 2.5-10 mcg/kg/min. Max dose: 40 mcg/kg/min. Higher doses may paradoxically worsen hypotension.
drugs.notes: Preparation: 250mg in 200ml normal saline (withdraw 20ml NS, add 20ml/1 amp dobutamine). Inotrope. Consider addition of second inotrope with increasing dose requirements. Monitor for arrhythmias and hypotension at high doses.

drugs.adult_dose: Typical dose: 0.1-6mcg/kg/min. Titrate to effect, use lowest effective dose. Bolus: 50-250mcg (0.5-2.5ml of infusion mixture) for adult.
drugs.notes: Preparation: 2x1ml amps (10mg/ml) in 200ml NS = 0.1mg/ml (100mcg/ml). Withdraw 2ml saline prior to adding. NEVER ADMINISTER UNDILUTED PHENYLEPHRINE. Pure vasopressor, only for vasoplegic patients (e.g. spinal cord injuries). May cause reflex bradycardia. Do not discontinue suddenly - must be titrated down.

drugs.adult_dose: Start 20mg bolus (4ml). Then infusion: Start at 80ml/hr. Increase by 10ml/hr every 5 minutes until BP target reached. Maximum 160ml/hr (2mg/min).
drugs.notes: Preparation: Add remaining 16ml (80mg) + 2nd vial (100mg) of Labetalol to 200ml 0.9% normal saline (0.76mg/ml). Compelling indications: Aortic dissection, Encephalopathy, Stroke (ischaemic - if qualifies for thrombolysis; haemorrhagic - to prevent re-bleed). Combined alpha and beta blocker.

drugs.adult_dose: Initial: 1-2mg/hour. Adjust according to desired patient response. Maximum usually 8-10mg/hour, but up to 50mg/hour may be required in acute decompensated heart failure.
drugs.notes: Standard preparation: 100mg in 200ml NS or 5% dextrose (0.5mg/ml) - withdraw 100ml before adding 10 amps. Concentrated preparation: 20mg in 200ml (0.1mg/ml) - withdraw 20ml before adding 2 amps. NOT a weight-based drug. Monitor BP closely. Use for afterload reduction in acute heart failure and hypertensive emergencies.

drugs.adult_dose: Start at 10mcg/min (12ml/hr). Titrate up to 200mcg/min (240ml/hr) as needed.
drugs.notes: Preparation: 10mg in 200ml normal saline (50mcg/ml) - withdraw 10ml NS before adding 10ml (1 amp). NOT a weight-based drug. Monitor BP closely. Use for ACS, acute pulmonary oedema, hypertensive emergencies. Contraindicated in SBP<90, severe bradycardia <50bpm, RV infarction, recent PDE5 inhibitor use.

drugs.adult_dose: 0.05-0.5mg/kg/hr of EACH drug (4-120ml/hr for 40-120kg). Doses >0.1mg/kg/hr of each drug seldom needed.
drugs.notes: Preparation: 0.5mg/ml of EACH DRUG. 90mg Midazolam + 90mg Morphine in 180ml volume. Remove 44ml from 200ml saline bag, add 6 amps Morphine (15mg/ml) + 6 amps Midazolam (15mg/3ml) = 24ml total drug volume. Only for ventilated patients. High risk of hypotension and respiratory depression at higher doses. Reduce dose of each drug when using combination.

drugs.adult_dose: Loading: 0.2mg/kg slow IV push. Maintenance: 0.05-1.0mg/kg/hr (1-60ml/hr for 40-120kg). Typical range: 0.05-0.3mg/kg/hr.
drugs.notes: Loading preparation: 1mg/ml (1 amp 15mg/3ml + 12ml NS in 20ml syringe). Infusion preparation: 2mg/ml (90mg in 45ml - 6x3ml amps + 27ml NS in 50ml syringe). Higher doses only with expert consultation (typically Delirium Tremens). No analgesic effect. Monitor BP, respiration and O2 saturation closely. For sedation only.

drugs.adult_dose: Loading: 1-3mg/kg slow IV push. Maintenance: 1-15mg/kg/hr (4-180ml/hr for 40-120kg). Rates >4mg/kg/hr not recommended for prolonged sedation.
drugs.notes: Preparation: 400mg pure propofol - 2x20ml amps (40ml) in 50ml empty NS bag = 20mg/ml. For intubated patients only. No analgesic effect. Monitor closely for hypotension and respiratory depression. Discard unused portion and giving set after 6 hours. Can cause significant hypotension - ensure hemodynamic stability before initiation.

management_steps.details: Choose infusion based on clinical indication: Amiodarone (arrhythmia), Ketamine (analgesia/sedation), Morphine (analgesia), Esmolol (rapid beta-blockade), Adrenaline (shock/cardiac arrest), Dobutamine (inotropy), Phenylephrine (vasoplegia), Labetalol (hypertensive emergency), Isoket/Nitrocine (afterload reduction/vasodilation), Midazolam/Propofol (sedation).
management_steps.caution: Ensure correct indication before starting any infusion

management_steps.details: Follow specific preparation instructions for each drug including dilution, concentration, and equipment (infusion pump vs syringe driver). Double-check drug, dose, and calculations.
management_steps.caution: NEVER administer undiluted Phenylephrine. Esmolol requires SYRINGE DRIVER.

management_steps.details: Use weight-based tables provided for patient's weight. Verify rate in ml/hr matches desired mcg/kg/min or mg/kg/hr.
management_steps.caution: Double-check all calculations. Use infusion pump for all weight-based infusions.

management_steps.details: Start at lowest effective dose. Titrate to clinical effect (BP, heart rate, sedation level). Document rate and response.
management_steps.caution: Do not exceed maximum recommended doses without senior consultation

management_steps.details: Monitor vital signs, ECG, SpO2 regularly. Record observations. Watch for adverse effects: hypotension, bradycardia, respiratory depression, arrhythmias.
management_steps.caution: Sedation infusions can cause respiratory depression and hypotension. Resuscitation facilities must be available.

management_steps.details: Titrate down gradually before stopping (especially vasopressors and sedation). Do not discontinue Phenylephrine suddenly - must be titrated down.
management_steps.caution: Abrupt discontinuation of vasopressors can cause rebound hypotension

diagnostic_criteria.indications_amiodarone: Stable wide-QRS tachycardia, Ventricular fibrillation/pulseless VT (bolus in arrest), Recurrent VT
diagnostic_criteria.indications_ketamine: Analgesia (0.05-0.1mg/kg/hr), Procedural sedation (>/=0.2mg/kg/hr), Induction for intubation
diagnostic_criteria.indications_esmolol: Rapid heart rate control (afib with RVR, SVT), Aortic dissection, Thyrotoxicosis
diagnostic_criteria.indications_adrenaline: Cardiac arrest, Anaphylaxis, Severe shock, Post-intubation hypotension (push-dose)
diagnostic_criteria.indications_dobutamine: Cardiogenic shock with low output, Decompensated heart failure
diagnostic_criteria.indications_phenylephrine: Vasoplegic shock (e.g. spinal cord injury), Neurogenic shock
diagnostic_criteria.indications_isoket: Acute decompensated heart failure, Afterload reduction, Hypertensive emergency
diagnostic_criteria.indications_nitrocine: ACS, Acute pulmonary oedema, Hypertensive emergency
diagnostic_criteria.indications_midazolam: Sedation for ventilated patients, Procedural sedation
diagnostic_criteria.indications_propofol: Sedation for intubated patients, Procedural sedation

clinical_features.amiodarone: Used for wide-complex tachycardia, VT, VF
clinical_features.ketamine: Analgesia at low dose, dissociative sedation at higher doses. Preserves airway reflexes and BP.
clinical_features.morphine: Analgesia for severe pain. Causes sedation and respiratory depression at higher doses.
clinical_features.esmolol: Ultra-short acting beta-1 blocker. Onset <1 minute, duration 5-10 minutes. Easily titratable.
clinical_features.adrenaline: Alpha and beta agonist. Increases HR, BP, myocardial contractility. First-line in cardiac arrest.
clinical_features.dobutamine: Beta-1 agonist. Increases cardiac output without significant vasoconstriction. May cause tachycardia.
clinical_features.phenylephrine: Pure alpha-agonist. Causes vasoconstriction. May cause reflex bradycardia.
clinical_features.labetalol: Combined alpha and beta blocker. For BP reduction in hypertensive emergencies.
clinical_features.isoket: Venodilator and arterial dilator. Reduces preload and afterload.
clinical_features.nitrocine: Venodilator > arterial dilator. Reduces preload, coronary vasodilation.
clinical_features.propofol: Sedative-hypnotic. Causes significant hypotension. No analgesic properties.

protocols.monitoring: Continuous cardiac monitoring (ECG) | Blood pressure (non-invasive or arterial line) | SpO2 pulse oximetry | Respiratory rate and depth | Consciousness level | Capnography if available | Document vital signs at regular intervals (minimum every 15 minutes or more frequently if unstable)
warnings: NEVER administer undiluted Phenylephrine - can cause severe hypertension and reflex bradycardia
Esmolol requires SYRINGE DRIVER - not compatible with standard infusion pumps at high concentrations
Propofol can cause significant hypotension - ensure hemodynamic stability before use
Ketamine can cause hallucinations and emergence reactions - consider benzodiazepine co-administration
Morphine and sedation infusions: High risk of hypotension and respiratory depression
Phenylephrine: Do not discontinue suddenly - must be titrated down gradually
Dobutamine: Higher doses may paradoxically worsen hypotension
Labetalol: Contraindicated in asthma, heart block, severe bradycardia, decompensated heart failure
All infusions require continuous monitoring - never leave unattended
Sedation infusions (Midazolam, Propofol): For intubated/ventilated patients only unless specified
Discard propofol and giving set after 6 hours - supports bacterial growth
Double-check all calculations and pump settings before starting
Amiodarone incompatible with many drugs - use dedicated line
Adrenaline push-dose: Only for post-intubation hypotension - NOT for routine BP management
protocols.disposition: All patients on infusions require monitored bed (High care/ICU/Resus). | Continuous supervision until infusion discontinued and patient stable. | Transfer to appropriate level of care with documentation of infusion details including drug, concentration, rate, and response.

interpretation.label: Severe
interpretation.action: Intubate, CT head immediately, neurosurgical referral

interpretation.label: Low risk
interpretation.action: C-spine imaging NOT indicated — clinical clearance possible

components.name: Dangerous mechanism (fall >1m, axial load, high-speed MVC, rollover, bicycle/ejected)
Delayed onset of neck pain (>12h after injury)?

interpretation.label: Imaging required
interpretation.action: C-spine CT/X-ray indicated — high-risk factors present

interpretation.label: Low risk
interpretation.action: Range of motion testing — if full ROM, no imaging needed

interpretation.label: No imaging
interpretation.action: C-spine imaging NOT indicated — clinically clear

interpretation.label: Unlikely appendicitis
interpretation.action: Observe — CT abdomen if clinical concern

interpretation.label: Possible appendicitis
interpretation.action: CT abdomen with contrast — surgical consult

components.name: Active cancer (treatment within 6 months or palliative)
Paralysis, paresis, or recent plaster immobilisation of lower extremity
Recently bedridden >3 days or major surgery within 4 weeks
Localised tenderness along deep venous system
Calf swelling >3cm compared to asymptomatic leg
Pitting oedema confined to symptomatic leg
Alternative diagnosis as likely or more likely than DVT
Clinical signs of DVT (minimum 3 points leg swelling and pain with palpation of deep veins)
Alternative diagnosis less likely than PE
Immobilisation ≥3 days or surgery in previous 4 weeks

interpretation.label: PE unlikely
interpretation.action: PERC rule → D-dimer if PERC not met

interpretation.label: PE excluded
interpretation.action: No further testing needed — PE ruled out

interpretation.label: PERC not met
interpretation.action: Proceed to Wells score or D-dimer

components.name: Blood pressure: SBP <90 or DBP ≤60 mmHg

interpretation.label: Intermediate risk
interpretation.action: Consider short hospitalisation or outpatient — mortality 9%

interpretation.label: High risk
interpretation.action: Admit to hospital — severe pneumonia — mortality 15-40%

options.label: Present
options.desc: Infection, surgery, iodine load, OCP, etc.

interpretation.label: Total 24h fluid
interpretation.action: ½ in first 8h from time of burn, ½ in next 16h

interpretation.label: High risk of poor outcome
interpretation.action: Check full SOFA, start sepsis bundle, consider ICU

interpretation.label: TEWS Orange
interpretation.action: Senior review within 15 min — consider HDU

components.name: All other wounds (dirty, major, crush, burn, frostbite, devitalised tissue)

interpretation.label: No TIG needed
interpretation.action: Give Td booster if >10y since last dose

interpretation.label: TIG not needed
interpretation.action: Give Td booster if >5y since last dose

interpretation.label: Give Td + TIG
interpretation.action: Give Td + 250 IU TIG at separate site

components.name: Burns involving face, hands, feet, genitalia, perineum, or major joints
Circumferential burns of limbs or chest
Burns in patients with significant comorbidities
Any burn in a child <1 year

interpretation.label: Elevated
interpretation.action: Consider causes of high anion gap metabolic acidosis

interpretation.label: High
interpretation.action: MUDPILES — DKA, lactic acidosis, renal failure, etc.

interpretation.label: Corrected Na
interpretation.action: Use corrected Na for treatment decisions

interpretation.label: Free water deficit
interpretation.action: Replace slowly over 48-72h — max correction 10 mmol/L/day
